Qualified Plans ................. 18\nKinds of Plans ......................... 19\nQualification Rules ...................... 20\nSetting up a Qualified Plan ................ 22\nMinimum Funding Requirement ............. 23\nContributions .......................... 23\nEmployer Deduction ..................... 24\nElective Deferrals (401(k) Plans) ............ 25\nQualified Roth Contribution Program ......... 28\nDistributions ........................... 29\nProhibited T ransactions ................... 35\nReporting Requirements .................. 37\nChapter 5. Table and Worksheets for the \nSelf-Employed ........................ 38\nChapter 6. How To Get Tax Help ............. 43\nIndex .................................. 47\nFuture Developments\nFor the latest information about developments related to \nPub. 560, such as legislation enacted after it was \npublished, go to IRS.gov/Pub560 .\nWhat's New\nCompensation limits for 2024 and 2025.  For 2024, the \nmaximum compensation used for figuring contributions \nand benefits is $345,000. This limit increases to $350,000 \nfor 2025.\nElective deferral limits for 2024 and 2025.  The limit on \nelective deferrals, other than catch -up contributions, is \nPublication 560 (2024)  Catalog Number 46574N\nMar 17, 2025 Department of the T reasury  Internal Revenue Service   www.irs.gov\nPage 2 of 48  Fileid: вҖҰ cation-560/2024/a/xml/cycle08/source 15:12 - 17-Mar-2025\nThe type and rule above prints on all proofs including departmental reproduction proofs. MUST be removed before printing.\n$23,000 for 2024 and $23,500 for 2025. These limits ap-\nply for participants in SARSEPs, 401(k) plans (excluding \nSIMPLE plans), section 403(b) plans, and section 457(b) \nplans.\nDefined contribution limits for 2024 and 2025.  The \nlimit on contributions, other than catch -up contributions, \nfor a participant in a defined contribution plan is $69,000 \nfor 2024 and increases to $70,000 for 2025.\nDefined benefit limits for 2024 and 2025.  The limit on \nannual benefits for a participant in a defined benefit plan is \n$275,000 for 2024 and increases to $280,000 for 2025.\nSIMPLE plan salary reduction contribution limits for \n2024 and 2025.  The limit on salary reduction contribu-\ntions, other than catch -up contributions, is $16,000 for \n2024 and increases to $16,500 for 2025. Pursuant to sec-\ntion 117 of the SECURE 2.0 Act of 2022, a higher limit \n($17,600 for 2024) may apply to participants in certain \nSIMPLE plans, effective for taxable years beginning after \nDecember 31, 2023.\nCatch- up contribution limits for 2024 and 2025.  A \nplan can permit participants who are age 50 or over at the \nend of the calendar year to make catch -up contributions in \naddition to elective deferrals and SIMPLE plan salary re-\nduction contributions. The catch -up contribution limit for \ndefined contribution plans other than SIMPLE plans is \n$7,500 for 2024 and 2025. The catch -up contribution limit \nfor SIMPLE plans is generally $3,500 for 2024 and 2025. \nPursuant to section 117(b) of the SECURE 2.0 Act, a \nhigher catch -up limit ($3,850 for 2024 and 2025) may ap-\nply to participants in certain SIMPLE plans, effective for \ntaxable years beginning after December 31, 2023.\nA participant's catch -up contributions for a year can't \nexceed the lesser of the following amounts.\nвҖўThe catch-up contribution limit.\nвҖўThe excess of the participant's compensation over the \nelective deferrals that arenвҖҷt catch-up contributions.\nSee Catch- up contributions under Contribution Limits  and \nLimit on Elective Deferrals  in chapters 3 and 4, respec-\ntively, for more information.\nPlans established after the end of tax year.  For 2023 \nand later years, a sole -proprietor with no employees can \nadopt a section 401(k) plan after the end of the taxe year, \nprovided the plan is adopted by the tax filing deadline \n(without regard to extensions).\nIncreased small employer pension plan startup cost \ncredit. The Secure 2. 0 Act of Division T of the Consolida-\nted Appropriations Act, 2023, P .L. 117 -328 (SECURE 2. 0 \nAct), provides that eligible employers with 1вҖ“50 employ-\nees are eligible for an increased small employer pension \nplan startup cost credit under section 45E of 100% of \nqualified startup costs, subject to the limit described in the \nReminders  section. The credit for eligible employers with \n51вҖ“100 employees remains at 50% of qualified startup \ncosts, subject to the same limit. See the instructions for \nForm 3800 and Form 8881 for more information on the \nstartup cost credit.\nEmployer contributions credit.  The SECURE 2.0 Act \nadded an additional startup cost credit under section 45E available to certain eligible employers, in an amount equal \nto an applicable percentage of the employerвҖҷs contribu-\ntions (not including an elective deferral, as defined in sec-\ntion 402(g)(3)) to an eligible employer plan (other than a \ndefined benefit plan (as described in section 414(j)), sub-\nject to limitation. See the instructions to Form 3800 and \nForm 8881 for more information on the employer contribu-\ntions credit.\nSmall employer military spouse participation credit. \nThe SECURE 2. 0 Act added a new military spouse partici-\npation credit under section 45AA available to eligible small \nemployers who maintain defined contribution plans with \nspecific features that benefit military spouses. See the in-\nstructions for Form 3800 and Form 8881 for more informa-\ntion on the military spouse participation credit.\nDesignated Roth nonelective contributions and des-\nignated Roth matching contributions.  The SECURE \n2.0 Act of 2022 permits certain nonelective contributions \nand matching contributions that are made after December \n29, 2022, to be designated as Roth contributions.\nMatching contributions on account of qualified stu-\ndent loan payments.  Section 110 of the SECURE 2. 0 \nAct of 2022 allows employers to include an optional fea-\nture that would enable them to make matching contribu-\ntions on account of employees' qualified student loan pay-\nments under certain defined contribution retirement plans, \nincluding a SIMPLE IRA plan and a SIMPLE 401(k) plan. \nSection 110 of the SECURE 2.0 Act of 2022 applies to \ncontributions made for plan years beginning after Decem-\nber 31, 2023.\nStarter 401(k) deferral -only arrangement.  Section \n121(a) of the SECURE 2.0 Act of 2022 permits certain eli-\ngible employers to have a starter 401(k) deferral -only ar-\nrangement for plan years beginning after December 31, \n2023.\nAdditional nonelective contributions under a SIMPLE \nIRA plan. Section 116 of the SECURE 2.0 Act of 2022 al-\nlows employers to make additional nonelective contribu-\ntions under a SIMPLE IRA plan, effective for taxable years \nbeginning after December 31, 2023.\nMidyear replacement of SIMPLE IRA plan with safe \nharbor 401(k) plan.  Section 332 of the SECURE 2. 0 Act \nof 2022 allows an employer to replace its SIMPLE IRA \nplan with a safe harbor 401(k) plan during a year, effective \nfor plan years beginning after December 31, 2023.\nRoth IRAs under a SEP arrangement and Roth SIM-\nPLE IRAs under a SIMPLE IRA plan.  Section 601 of the \nSECURE 2.0 Act of 2022 permits contributions under a \nSEP arrangement or a SIMPLE IRA plan to be made to a \nRoth IRA, effective for taxable years beginning after De-\ncember 31, 2022.\nPension- Linked Emergency Savings Accounts.  Sec-\ntion 127 of the SECURE 2.0 Act of 2022 allows employers \nto add an optional feature to provide short -term savings \naccounts established and maintained in connection with a \ndefined contribution retirement plan, and those savings \naccounts are treated as a type of designated Roth ac-\ncount. Section 127 of the SECURE 2.0 Act of 2022 pro-\nvides for the creation of Pension -Linked Emergency \n2 Publication 560 (2024)\nPage 3 of 48  Fileid: вҖҰ cation-560/2024/a/xml/cycle08/source 15:12 - 17-Mar-2025\nThe type and rule above prints on all proofs including departmental reproduction proofs. MUST be removed before printing.\nSavings Accounts (PLESAs) effective for plan years be-\nginning after December 31, 2023.\nReminders\nSmall employer automatic enrollment credit.  The Fur-\nther Consolidated Appropriations Act, 2020, P .L. 116 -94, \nadded section 45T . An eligible employer may claim a tax \ncredit if it includes an eligible automatic contribution ar-\nrangement under a qualified employer plan. The credit \nequals $500 per year over a 3 -year period beginning with \nthe first tax year in which it includes the automatic contri-\nbution arrangement.\nIncrease in credit limitation for small employer plan \nstartup costs.  The Further Consolidated Appropriations \nAct, 2020, P .L. 116 -94, amended section 45E. For tax \nyears beginning after December 31, 2019, eligible em-\nployers can claim a tax credit for the first credit year and \neach of the 2 tax years immediately following. The credit \nequals 50% of qualified startup costs, up to the greater of \nthe limit of (a) $500; or (b) the lesser of (i) $250 for each \nemployee who is not a вҖңhighly compensated employeeвҖқ el-\nigible to participate in the employer plan, or (ii) $5,000.\nNote. The SECURE 2.0 Act further amended section \n45E to increase the credit for tax years beginning after De-\ncember 31, 2022. See WhatвҖҷs New , earlier.\nSee the instructions for Form 3800 and Form 8881 for \nmore information on the small employer automatic enroll-\nment credit and the small employer startup cost credit.\nRestriction on conditions of participation.  Effective \nfor plan years beginning after December 31, 2020, a \n401(k) plan canвҖҷt require, as a condition of participation, \nthat an employee complete a period of service that ex-\ntends beyond the close of the earlier of (a) 1 year of serv-\nice, or (b) the first period of 3 consecutive 12 -month peri-\nods (excluding 12 -month periods beginning before \nJanuary 1, 2021) during each of which the employee has \ncompleted at least 500 hours of service. Effective for plan \nyears beginning after December 31, 2024, 3 consecutive \n12-month periods are reduced to 2 consecutive 12 -month \nperiods.\nRetirement savings contributions credit.  Retirement \nplan participants (including self -employed individuals) \nwho make contributions to their plans may qualify for the \nretirement savings contribution credit. The maximum con-\ntribution eligible for the credit is $2,000. T o take the credit, \nuse Form 8880, Credit for Qualified Retirement Savings \nContributions. For more information on who is eligible for \nthe credit, retirement plan contributions eligible for the \ncredit, and how to figure the credit, see Form 8880 and its \ninstructions or go to IRS.gov/Retirement-Plans/Plan-\nParticipant-Employee/Retirement-Savings-Contributions-\nSavers-Credit .\nPhotographs of missing children.  The IRS is a proud \npartner with the National Center for Missing & Exploited \nChildrenВ® (NCMEC) . Photographs of missing children se-\nlected by the Center may appear in this publication on pa-\nges that would otherwise be blank. Y ou can help bring these children home by looking at the photographs and \ncalling 1 -800-THE- LOST (1 -800-843- 5678) if you recog-\nnize a child.\nIntroduction\nThis publication discusses retirement plans you can set \nup and maintain for yourself and your employees. In this \npublication, вҖңyouвҖқ refers to the employer. See chapter 1 for \nthe definition of the term вҖңemployerвҖқ and the definitions of \nother terms used in this publication. This publication cov-\ners the following types of retirement plans.\nвҖўSEP (simplified employee pension) plans.\nвҖўSIMPLE (savings incentive match plan for employees) \nplans.\nвҖўQualified plans (also called H.R. 10 plans or Keogh \nplans when covering self-employed individuals), in-\ncluding 401(k) plans.\nSEP , SIMPLE, and qualified plans offer you and your \nemployees a tax -favored way to save for retirement. Y ou \ncan deduct contributions you make to the plan for your \nemployees. If you are a sole proprietor, you can deduct \ncontributions you make to the plan for yourself. Y ou can \nalso deduct trustees' fees if contributions to the plan don't \ncover them. Earnings on the contributions are generally \ntax free until you or your employees receive distributions \nfrom the plan.\nUnder a 401(k) plan, employees can have you contrib-\nute limited amounts of their before -tax (after -tax, in the \ncase of a qualified Roth contribution program) pay to the \nplan. These amounts (and the earnings on them) are gen-\nerally tax free until your employees receive distributions \nfrom the plan or, in the case of a qualified distribution from \na designated Roth account, completely tax free.\nWhat this publication covers.  This publication contains \nthe information you need to understand the following top-\nics.\nвҖўWhat type of plan to set up.\nвҖўHow to set up a plan.\nвҖўHow much you can contribute to a plan.\nвҖўHow much of your contribution is deductible.\nвҖўHow to treat certain distributions.\nвҖўHow to report information about the plan to the IRS \nand your employees.\nвҖўBasic features of SEP , SIMPLE, and qualified plans. \nThe key rules for SEP , SIMPLE, and qualified plans \nare outlined in T able 1 .\nSEP plans.  SEP plans provide a simplified method for \nyou to make contributions to a retirement plan for yourself \nand your employees. Instead of setting up a profit -sharing \nor money purchase pension plan with a trust, you can \nadopt a SEP agreement and make contributions directly to \na traditional SEP IRA. For taxable years begininning after \nDecember 31, 2022, section 601 of the SECURE 2.0 Act \nPublication 560 (2024) 3\nPage 4 of 48  Fileid: вҖҰ cation-560/2024/a/xml/cycle08/source 15:12 - 17-Mar-2025\nThe type and rule above prints on all proofs including departmental reproduction proofs. MUST be removed before printing.\nof 2022 provides that an employer's SEP plan may allow \nan employee to designate a Roth IRA as the IRA to which \ncontributions under the SEP plan are made (a Roth SEP \nIRA).\nSIMPLE plans.  Generally, if you had 100 or fewer em-\nployees who received at least $5,000 in compensation last \nyear, you can set up a SIMPLE IRA plan. Under a SIMPLE \nplan, employees can choose to make salary reduction \ncontributions rather than receiving these amounts as part \nof their regular pay. In addition, you will contribute match-\ning or nonelective contributions. Y ou may also make addi-\ntional nonelective contributions. Contributions under an \nemployer's SIMPLE IRA plan are made to an employee's \ntraditional SIMPLE IRA. For tax years beginning after De-\ncember 31, 2022, section 601 of the SECURE 2.0 Act of \n2022 provides that an employer's SIMPLE IRA plan may \nallow an employee to designate that contributions be made to the employee's Roth SIMPLE IRA. T raditional \nSIMPLE IRAs are generally subject to the rules for tradi-\ntional IRAs and Roth SIMPLE IRAs are generally subject \nto the rules for Roth IRAs, however both types of SIMPLE \nIRAs are subject to a number of additional restrictions that \ndo not apply to traditional IRAs or Roth IRAs. The two \ntypes of SIMPLE plans are the SIMPLE IRA plan and the \nSIMPLE 401(k) plan.\nNote. See Q&A K -1 through K -8 of Notice 2024 -2, \n2024- 2 I.R.B. 316, at IRS.gov/irb/2024-02 , for additional \nguidance on Roth SEP IRAs and Roth SIMPLE IRAs.\nQualified plans.  The qualified plan rules are more \ncomplex than the SEP plan and SIMPLE plan rules. How-\never, there are advantages to qualified plans, such as in-\ncreased flexibility in designing plans and increased contri-\nbution and deduction limits in some cases.\n4 Publication 560 (2024)\nPage 5 of 48  Fileid: вҖҰ cation-560/2024/a/xml/cycle08/source 15:12 - 17-Mar-2025\nThe type and rule above prints on all proofs including departmental reproduction proofs. MUST be removed before printing.\nT able 1.  Key Retirement Plan Rules for 2024\nType\n of \nPlan Last Date for Contribution Maximum Contribution Maximum Deduction When To Set Up Plan\nSEP Due date of employer's return \n(including extensions).Smaller of $69,000 or 25%1 \nof participant's \ncompensation.225%1 of all participants' \ncompensation.2Any time up to the due date of \nemployer's return (including \nextensions).\nSIMPLE\nIRA\nand\nSIMPLE\n401(k)Salary reduction contributions:  30 \ndays after the end of the month for \nwhich the contributions are to be made.4\n \nMatching or nonelective \ncontributions:  Due date of employer's \nreturn (including extensions).Employee contribution:  \nSalary reduction contribution \nup to $16,000; $19,500 if \nage 50 or over. \n \nHigher salary reduction \ncontributions apply for \ncertain employers and are \nsubject to a cost-of-living \nadjustment.\n \nEmployer contribution:\nEither  dollar-for-dollar \nmatching contributions, up to \n3% of employee's \ncompensation,3 or fixed \nnonelective contributions of \n2% of compensation.2\n \nHigher matching and fixed \nnonelective contributions \napply for certain employers \nwho elect to allow higher \nsalary reduction \ncontributions. \n \nAdditional nonelective \ncontributions of a uniform \npercentage (up to 10% but \nnot exceeding $5,000 for \n2024) may also be made.Same as maximum \ncontribution.Any time between January 1 \nand October 1 of the calendar \nyear.\n \nFor a new employer coming \ninto existence after October 1, \nas soon as administratively \nfeasible.\nQualified \nPlan: \nDefined \nContribution\n PlanElective deferral:  Due date of \nemployer's return (including \nextensions).4\n \n \nEmployer contribution:\nProfit-Sharing Plan: Due date of \nemployer's return (including \nextensions). Money Purchase Pension \nPlan: 8 1/2 months after the end of the \nplan year.Employee contribution: \nElective deferral up to \n$23,000; $30,500 if age 50 \nor over.\n \nEmployer contribution:\nMoney Purchase Pension \nPlan: Smaller of $69,000 or \n100%1 of participant's \ncompensation.2\n \nProfit-Sharing Plan: Smaller \nof $69,000 or 100%1 of \nparticipant's compensation.225%1 of all participants' \ncompensation,2 plus \namount of elective \ndeferrals made.By the employerвҖҷs tax-filing \ndue date, including \nextensions, for the tax year.\nQualified \nPlan: \nDefined \nBenefit PlanContributions must generally be paid in \nquarterly installments, due 15 days \nafter the end of each quarter, with a \nfinal contribution due 8 1/2 months after \nthe end of the plan year. See Minimum \nFunding Requirement  in chapter 4.Amount needed to provide \nan annual benefit no larger \nthan the smaller of $275,000 \nor 100% of the participant's \naverage compensation for \nthe highest 3 consecutive \ncalendar years.Based on actuarial \nassumptions and \ncomputations.By the employerвҖҷs tax filing \ndue date (although itвҖҷs not best \nto set up after the minimum \nfunding due date).\n1 Net earnings from self-employment must take the contribution into account. See Deduction Limit for Self-Employed Individuals  in chapters 2 and 4.\n2 Compensation is generally limited to $345,000 in 2024.\n3 Under a SIMPLE 401(k) plan, compensation is generally limited to $345,000 in 2024.\n4 Certain plans subject to Department of Labor (DOL) rules may have an earlier due date for salary reduction contributions and elective deferrals, such as 401(k) \nplans. See the вҖңelective deferralвҖқ definition in  Definitions Y ou Need T o Know , later. Solo/self-employed 401(k) plans are non-ERISA plans and donвҖҷt fall under DOL \nrules.\nWhat this publication doesnвҖҷt cover.  Although the pur-\npose of this publication is to provide general information \nabout retirement plans you can set up for your employees, \nit doesn't contain all the rules and exceptions that apply to \nthese plans. Y ou may need professional help and guid-\nance.\nAlso, this publication doesn't cover all the rules that \nmay be of interest to employees. For example, it doesn't \ncover the following topics.\nвҖўThe comprehensive IRA rules an employee needs to \nknow. These rules are covered in Pub. 590-A, Contri-\nbutions to Individual Retirement Arrangements (IRAs); and Pub. 590-B, Distributions from Individual Retire-\nment Arrangements (IRAs).\nвҖўThe comprehensive rules that apply to distributions \nfrom retirement plans. These rules are covered in Pub. \n575, Pension and Annuity Income.\nвҖўThe comprehensive rules that apply to section 403(b) \nplans. These rules are covered in Pub. 571, T ax-Shel-\ntered Annuity Plans (403(b) Plans) For Employees of \nPublic Schools and Certain T ax-Exempt Organiza-\ntions.\nPublication 560 (2024) 5\nPage 6 of 48  Fileid: вҖҰ cation-560/2024/a/xml/cycle08/source 15:12 - 17-Mar-2025\nThe type and rule above prints on all proofs including departmental reproduction proofs. MUST be removed before printing.\nComments and suggestions.  We welcome your com-\nments about this publication and your suggestions for \nfuture editions.\nY ou can send us comments through IRS.gov/\nFormComments . Or, you can write to the Internal Revenue \nService, T ax Forms and Publications, 1111 Constitution \nAve. NW, IR-6526, Washington, DC 20224.\nAlthough we canвҖҷt respond individually to each com-\nment received, we do appreciate your feedback and will \nconsider your comments and suggestions as we revise \nour tax forms, instructions, and publications.  DonвҖҷt  send \ntax questions, tax returns, or payments to the above ad-\ndress.\nGetting answers to your tax questions.  If you have \na tax question not answered by this publication or the How \nT o Get T ax Help  section at the end of this publication, go \nto the IRS Interactive T ax Assistant page at IRS.gov/\nHelp/ITA  where you can find topics by using the search \nfeature or viewing the categories listed.\nGetting tax forms, instructions, and publications. \nGo to IRS.gov/Forms  to download current and prior -year \nforms, instructions, and publications.\nOrdering forms and publications.  Go to IRS.gov/\nOrderForms  to order current forms, instructions, and publi-\ncations; call 800 -829- 3676 to order prior -year forms and \ninstructions. The IRS will process your order for forms and \npublications as soon as possible. DonвҖҷt  resubmit requests \nyouвҖҷve already sent us. Y ou can get forms and publica-\ntions faster online.\nTax questions.  If you have a tax question not an-\nswered by this publication, check IRS.gov and How T o Get \nT ax Help  at the end of this publication.\n1.\nDefinitions\nYou Need To Know\nCertain terms used in this publication are defined below. \nThe same term used in another publication may have a \nslightly different meaning.\nAnnual additions.  Annual additions are the total of all \nyour contributions in a year, employee contributions (not \nincluding rollovers), and forfeitures allocated to a partici-\npant's account.\nAnnual benefits.  Annual benefits are the benefits to be \npaid yearly in the form of a straight life annuity (with no ex-\ntra benefits) under a plan to which employees don't con-\ntribute and under which no rollover contributions are \nmade.Business. A business is an activity in which a profit mo-\ntive is present and economic activity is involved. Service \nas a newspaper carrier under age 18 or as a public official \nisnвҖҷt a business.\nCommon- law employee.  A common -law employee is \nany individual who, under common law, would have the \nstatus of an employee. A leased employee can also be a \ncommon-law employee.\nA common -law employee is a person who performs \nservices for an employer who has the right to control and \ndirect the results of the work and the way in which it is \ndone. For example, the employer:\nвҖўProvides the employee's tools, materials, and work-\nplace; and\nвҖўCan fire the employee.\nCommon-law employees aren't self-employed and can't \nset up retirement plans for income from their work, even if \nthat income is self -employment income for social security \ntax purposes. For example, common -law employees who \nare ministers, members of religious orders, full -time insur-\nance salespeople, and U.S. citizens employed in the Uni-\nted States by foreign governments can't set up retirement \nplans for their earnings from those employments, even \nthough their earnings are treated as self -employment in-\ncome.\nHowever, an individual may be a common -law em-\nployee and a self -employed person as well. For example, \nan attorney can be a corporate common -law employee \nduring regular working hours and also practice law in the \nevening as a self -employed person. In another example, a \nminister employed by a congregation for a salary is a com-\nmon- law employee even though the salary is treated as \nself- employment income for social security tax purposes. \nHowever, fees reported on Schedule C (Form 1040), Profit \nor Loss From Business, for performing marriages, bap-\ntisms, and other personal services are self -employment \nearnings for qualified plan purposes.\nCompensation. Compensation for plan allocations is the \npay a participant received from you for personal services \nfor a year. Y ou can generally define compensation as in-\ncluding all the following payments.\n1.Wages and salaries.\n2.Fees for professional services.\n3.Other amounts received (cash or noncash) for per-\nsonal services actually rendered by an employee, in-\ncluding, but not limited to, the following items.\na.Commissions and tips.\nb.Fringe benefits.\nc.Bonuses.\nFor a self -employed individual, compensation means \nthe earned income, discussed later, of that individual.\n6 Chapter 1 Definitions Y ou Need T o Know Publication 560 (2024)\nPage 7 of 48  Fileid: вҖҰ cation-560/2024/a/xml/cycle08/source 15:12 - 17-Mar-2025\nThe type and rule above prints on all proofs including departmental reproduction proofs. MUST be removed before printing.\nCompensation generally includes amounts deferred at \nthe employee's election in the following employee benefit \nplans.\nвҖўSection 401(k) plans.\nвҖўSection 403(b) plans.\nвҖўSIMPLE IRA plans.\nвҖўSARSEPs.\nвҖўSection 457 deferred compensation plans.\nвҖўSection 125 cafeteria plans.\nHowever, an employer can choose to exclude elective \ndeferrals under the above plans from the definition of com-\npensation. The limit on elective deferrals is discussed in \nchapter 2 under Salary Reduction Simplified Employee \nPension (SARSEP) and in chapter 4.\nOther options.  In figuring the compensation of a par-\nticipant, you can treat any of the following amounts as the \nemployee's compensation.\nвҖўThe employee's wages as defined for income tax with-\nholding purposes.\nвҖўThe employee's wages you report in box 1 of Form \nW-2, Wage and T ax Statement.\nвҖўThe employee's social security wages (including elec-\ntive deferrals).\nCompensation generally can't include either of the fol-\nlowing items.\nвҖўNontaxable reimbursements or other expense allow-\nances.\nвҖўDeferred compensation (other than elective deferrals).\nSIMPLE plans.  A special definition of compensation \napplies for SIMPLE plans. See chapter 3 .\nContribution. A contribution is an amount you pay into a \nplan for all those participating in the plan, including \nself- employed individuals. Limits apply to how much, un-\nder the contribution formula of the plan, can be contrib-\nuted each year for a participant.\nDeduction. A deduction is the plan contribution you can \nsubtract from gross income on your federal income tax re-\nturn. Limits apply to the amount deductible.\nEarned income.  Earned income is net earnings from \nself- employment, discussed later, from a business in \nwhich your services materially helped to produce the in-\ncome.\nY ou can also have earned income from property your \npersonal efforts helped create, such as royalties from your \nbooks or inventions. Earned income includes net earnings \nfrom selling or otherwise disposing of the property, but it \ndoesn't include capital gains. It includes income from li-\ncensing the use of property other than goodwill.\nEarned income includes amounts received for services \nby self -employed members of recognized religious sects \nopposed to social security benefits who are exempt from \nself-employment tax.If you have more than one business, but only one has a \nretirement plan, only the earned income from that busi-\nness is considered for that plan.\nElective deferral.  An elective deferral is the contribution \nmade by employees to a qualified retirement plan.\nвҖўNon-owner employees: The employee salary reduc-\ntion/elective deferral contributions must be elected/\nmade by the end of the tax year and deposited into the \nemployeeвҖҷs plan account within 7 business days (safe \nharbor) and no later than 15 days.\nвҖўOwner/employees: The employee deferrals must be \nelected by the end of the tax year and can then be \nmade by the tax return filing deadline, including exten-\nsions.\nEmployer. An employer is generally any person for whom \nan individual performs or did perform any service, of what-\never nature, as an employee. A sole proprietor is treated \nas its own employer for retirement plan purposes. How-\never, a partner isn't an employer for ")
```

Controls\nVersion 8\nv8\nPage 2 \nCIS Controls v8CIS Controls Version 8\nAcknowledgments\nCIS would like to thank the many security experts who volunteer their time and talent to support the CIS CIS products represent the effort of a veritable army of volunteers from \nacross the industry, generously giving their time and talent in the name of a more secure online experience \nfor everyone.\nCreative Commons License\nThis work is licensed under a\xa0Creative Commons Attribution-NonCommercial-No Derivatives 4.0 International \nPublic License (the link can be found at https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc-nd/4. 0/legalcode.\nTo further clarify the Creative Commons license related to the CIS ControlsВ® content, you are authorized to \ncopy and redistribute the content as a framework for use by you, within your organization and outside of your \norganization, for non-commercial purposes only, provided that (i) appropriate credit is given to CIS, and (ii) \na link to the license is provided. Additionally, if you remix, transform, or build upon the CIS Controls, you may \nnot distribute the modified materials. Users of the CIS Controls framework are also required to refer to (http://\nwww.cisecurity.org/controls/) when referring to the CIS Controls\xa0in order to ensure that users are employing \nthe most up-to-date guidance. Commercial use of the CIS Controls is subject to the prior approval of the \nCenter for Internet Security, Inc. (CISВ®).\nMay 2021\nContents\nGlossary ............................................................................................................... iv\nAcronyms and Abbreviations ............................................................................... vii\nOverview ............................................................................................................... 1\nIntroduction 1\nThis Version of the CIS Controls 3\nThe CIS Controls Ecosystem (вҖңItвҖҷs not about the listвҖқ) 4\nHow to Get Started 5\nUsing or Transitioning from Prior Versions of the CIS Controls 5\nStructure of the CIS Controls 5\nImplementation Groups 6\nControl 01 Inventory and Control of Enterprise Assets ............................................................ 8\nWhy is this Control critical? A1\nAppendix B Controls and Safeguards Index ........................................................................... B1\nPage iii \nCIS Controls v8 Contents\nGlossary\nAdministrator accounts Dedicated accounts with escalated privileges and used for managing aspects of a \ncomputer, domain, or the whole enterprise information technology infrastructure. \nCommon administrator account subtypes include root accounts, local administrator \nand domain administrator accounts, and network or security appliance \nadministrator accounts.\nApplication A program, or group of programs, hosted on enterprise assets and designed for end-\nusers. Applications are considered a software asset in this document. Examples include \nweb, database, cloud-based, and mobile applications.\nAuthentication systems A system or mechanism used to identify a user through associating an incoming request \nwith a set of identifying credentials. The credentials provided are compared to those on \na file in a database of the authorized userвҖҷs information on a local operating system, user \ndirectory service, or within an authentication server. Examples of authentication systems \ncan include active directory, Multi-Factor Authentication (MFA), biometrics, and tokens.\nAuthorization systems A system or mechanism used to determine access levels or user/client privileges related \nto system resources including files, services, computer programs, data, and application \nfeatures. An authorization system grants or denies access to a resource based on the \nuserвҖҷs identity. Examples of authorization systems can include active directory, access \ncontrol lists, and role-based access control lists.\nCloud environment A virtualized environment that provides convenient, on-demand network access \nto a shared pool of configurable resources such as network, computing, storage, \napplications, and services. There are five essential characteristics to a cloud \nenvironment: on-demand self-service, broad network access, resource pooling, rapid \nelasticity, and measured service. Some services offered through cloud environments \ninclude Software as a Service (SaaS), Platform as a Service (PaaS), and Infrastructure as \na Service (IaaS).\nDatabase Organized collection of data, generally stored and accessed electronically from a \ncomputer system. Databases can reside remotely or on-site. Database Management \nSystems (DMSs) are used to administer databases, and are not considered part of a \ndatabase for this document.\nEnd-user devices Information technology (IT) assets used among members of an enterprise during work, \noff-hours, or any other purpose. End-user devices include mobile and portable devices \nsuch as laptops, smartphones and tablets, as well as desktops and workstations. For the \npurpose of this document, end-user devices are a subset of enterprise assets.\nEnterprise assets Assets with the potential to store or process data. For the purpose of this document, \nenterprise assets include end-user devices, network devices, non-computing/Internet of \nThings (IoT) devices, and servers, in virtual, cloud-based, and physical environments.\nExternally-exposed \nenterprise assetsRefers to enterprise assets that are public facing and discoverable through domain \nname system reconnaissance and network scanning from the public internet outside of \nthe enterpriseвҖҷs network.\nInternal enterprise assets Refers to non-public facing enterprise assets that can only be identified through network \nscans and reconnaissance from within an enterpriseвҖҷs network through authorized \nauthenticated or unauthenticated access.\nPage iv \nCIS Controls v8 Glossary\nLibrary Pre-written code, classes, procedures, scripts, configuration data, and more, \nused to develop software programs and applications. It is designed to assist \nboth the programmer and the programming language compiler in building and \nexecuting software. \nMobile end-user devices Small, enterprise issued end-user devices with intrinsic wireless capability, such as \nsmartphones and tablets. Mobile end-user devices are a subset of portable end-user \ndevices, including laptops, which may require external hardware for connectivity. For the \npurpose of this document, mobile end-user devices are a subset of end-user devices.\nNetwork devices Electronic devices required for communication and interaction between devices on a \ncomputer network. Network devices include wireless access points, firewalls, physical/\nvirtual gateways, routers, and switches. These devices consist of physical hardware, \nas well as virtual and cloud-based devices. For the purpose of this document, network \ndevices are a subset of enterprise assets.\nNetwork infrastructure Refers to all of the resources of a network that make network or internet connectivity, \nmanagement, business operations, and communication possible. It consists of hardware \nand software, systems and devices, and it enables computing and communication \nbetween users, services, applications, and processes. Network infrastructure can be \ncloud, physical, or virtual. \nNon-computing/Internet \nof Things (IoT) devicesDevices embedded with sensors, software, and other technologies for the purpose \nof connecting, storing, and exchanging data with other devices and systems over the \ninternet. While these devices are not used for computational processes, they support an \nenterpriseвҖҷs ability to conduct business processes. Examples of these devices include \nprinters, smart screens, physical security sensors, industrial control systems, and \ninformation technology sensors. For the purpose of this document, non-computing/IoT \ndevices are a subset of enterprise assets. \nOperating system System software on enterprise assets that manages computer hardware and software \nresources, and provides common services for programs. Operating systems are \nconsidered a software asset and can be single- and multi-tasking, single- and multi-\nuser, distributed, templated, embedded, real-time, and library.\nPhysical environment Physical hardware parts that make up a network, including cables and routers. The \nhardware is required for communication and interaction between devices on a network.\nPortable \nend-user devicesTransportable, end-user devices that have the capability to wirelessly connect to a \nnetwork. For the purpose of this document, portable end-user devices can include \nlaptops and mobile devices such as smartphones and tablets, all of which are a subset \nof enterprise assets.\nRemote devices Any enterprise asset capable of connecting to a network remotely, usually from public \ninternet. This can include enterprise assets such as end-user devices, network devices, \nnon-computing/Internet of Things (IoT) devices, and servers.\nRemote file systems Enable an application that runs on an enterprise asset to access files stored on a \ndifferent asset. Remote file systems often make other resources, such as remote non-\ncomputing devices, accessible from an asset. The remote file access takes place \nusing some form of local area network, wide area network, point-to-point link, or other \ncommunication mechanism. These file systems are often referred to as network file \nsystems or distributed file systems.\nPage v \nCIS Controls v8 Glossary\nRemovable media Any type of storage device that can be removed from a computer while the system \nis running and allows data to be moved from one system to another. Examples of \nremovable media include compact discs (CDs), digital versatile discs (DVDs) and \nBlu-ray discs, tape backups, as well as diskettes and universal serial bus (USB) drives.\nServers A device or system that provides resources, data, services, or programs to other devices \non either a local area network or wide area network. Servers can provide resources \nand use them from another system at the same time. Examples include web servers, \napplication servers, mail servers, and file servers.\nService accounts A dedicated account with escalated privileges used for running applications and other \nprocesses. Service accounts may also be created just to own data and configuration \nfiles. They are not intended to be used by people, except for performing administrative \noperations. \nServices Refers to a software functionality or a set of software functionalities, such as the \nretrieval of specified information or the execution of a set of operations. Services \nprovide a mechanism to enable access to one or more capabilities, where the access is \nprovided using a prescribed interface and based on the identity of the requestor per the \nenterpriseвҖҷs usage policies.\nSocial engineering Refers to a broad range of malicious activities accomplished through human interactions \non various platforms, such as email or phone. It relies on psychological manipulation to \ntrick users into making security mistakes or giving away sensitive information.\nSoftware assets Also referred to as software in this document, these are the programs and other \noperating information used within an enterprise asset. Software assets include operating \nsystems and applications.\nUser accounts An identity created for a person in a computer or computing system. For the purpose \nof this document, user accounts refer to вҖңstandardвҖқ or вҖңinteractiveвҖқ user accounts with \nlimited privileges and are used for general tasks such as reading email and surfing the \nweb. User accounts with escalated privileges are covered under administrator accounts.\nVirtual environment Simulates hardware to allow a software environment to run without the need to use \na lot of actual hardware. Virtualized environments are used to make a small number \nof resources act as many with plenty of processing, memory, storage, and network \ncapacity. Virtualization is a fundamental technology that allows cloud computing to work.\nPage vi \nCIS Controls v8 Glossary\nAcronyms and Abbreviations\nAAA Authentication, Authorization, \nand Auditing\nACL Access Control List\nAD Active Directory\nAoC Attestation of Compliance\nAPI Application Programming Interface\nBEC Business Email Compromise\nC2 Command and Control\nCCE Common Configuration Enumeration\nCDM Community Defense Model\nCIA Confidentiality, Integrity, and Availability\nCIS Center for Internet Security\nCIS-CAT CIS Configuration Assessment Tool\nCOTS Commercial off-the-Shelf\nCPE Common Platform Enumeration\nCREST Council of Registered\xa0Security\xa0Testers\nCSA Cloud Security Alliance\nCSP Cloud Service Provider\nCVE Common Vulnerabilities and Exposures\nCVSS Common Vulnerability Scoring System\nDBIR Data Breach Investigations Report\nDEP Data Execution Prevention\nDG Development Group\nDHCP Dynamic Host Configuration Protocol\nDKIM DomainKeys Identified Mail\nDLP Data Loss Prevention\nDMARC Domain-based Message Authentication, \nReporting, and Conformance\nDMS Database Management System\nDNS Domain Name System\nDPI Deep Packet Inspection\nEDR Endpoint Detection and Response\nEOL End of Life\nFFIEC Federal Financial Institutions \nExamination Council\nFISMA Federal Information Security \nModernization Act\nGRC Governance Risk and ComplianceHECVAT Higher Education Community Vendor \nAssessment Toolkit\nHIPAA Health Insurance Portability and \nAccountability Act\nHTTP Hypertext Transfer Protocol\nHTTPS Hypertext Transfer Protocol Secure\nIaaS Infrastructure as a Service\nIAM Identity and Access Management\nIDS Intrusion Detection System\nIG Implementation Group\nIOCs Indicators of Compromise\nIoT Internet of Things\nIP Internet Protocol\nIPS Intrusion Prevention System\nISAC Information Sharing and Analysis  \nCenter\nISO International Organization for \nStandardization\nIT Information Technology\nLotL Living off the Land\nMDM Mobile Device Management\nMFA Multi-Factor Authentication\nMITRE  \nATT&CKMITRE Adversarial Tactics, Techniques, \nand Common KnowledgeВ®\nMS-ISAC Multi-State Information Sharing and \nAnalysis Center\nNaaS Network-as-a-Service\nNCSA National Cyber Security Alliance\nNIDS Network Intrusion Detection System\nNIST National\xa0Institute of Standards \nand Technology\nOS Operating System\nOSS Open Source Software\nOVAL Open Vulnerability and \nAssessment Language\nOWASP Open Web Application Security Project\nPaaS Platform as a Service\nPAM Privileged Access Management\nPCI Payment Card Industry\nPage vii \nCIS Controls v8 Acronyms and Abbreviations\nSaaS Software as a Service\nSAFECode Software Assurance Forum for  \nExcellence in Code\nSCADA Supervisory Control and Data Acquisition\nSCAP Security Content Automation Protocol\nSIEM Security Information and \nEvent Management\nSIP System Integrity Protection\nSMS Short Messaging Service\nSOC Security Operations Center\nSOC 2 Service Organization Control 2\nSPAM Something Posing as Mail\nSPF Sender Policy Framework\nSQL Structured Query Language\nSSDF Secure Software \nDevelopment Framework\nSSH Secure Shell\nSSO Single Sign-On\nTelnet Teletype Network\nTLS Transport Layer Security\nTTPs Tactics, Techniques, and Procedures\nU.K. United Kingdom\nURL Uniform Resource Locator\nUSB Universal Serial Bus\nVPN Virtual Private Network\nWDEG Windows Defender Exploit Guard\nWPA2 Wi-Fi Protected Access 2\nXCCDF Extensible Configuration Checklist \nDescription Format\nPage viii \nCIS Controls v8 Acronyms and Abbreviations\nPage ix \nCIS Controls v8\nOverview\n\nOverview\nIntroduction\nThe CIS ControlsВ® started as a simple grassroots activity to identify the most common \nand important real-world cyber-attacks that affect enterprises every day, translate that \nknowledge and experience into positive, constructive action for defenders, and then \nshare that information with a wider audience. The original goals were modestвҖ”to help \npeople and enterprises focus their attention and get started on the most important \nsteps to defend themselves from the attacks that really mattered.\nLed by the Center for Internet SecurityВ® (CISВ®), the CIS Controls have matured into an \ninternational community of volunteer individuals and institutions that:\nвҖў Share insights into attacks and attackers, identify root causes, and translate that \ninto classes of defensive action\nвҖў Create and share tools, working aids, and stories of adoption and problem-solving\nвҖў Map the CIS Controls to regulatory and compliance frameworks in order to ensure \nalignment and bring collective priority and focus to them\nвҖў Identify common problems and barriers (like initial assessment and implementation \nroadmaps), and solve them as a community\nThe CIS Controls reflect the combined knowledge of experts from every part of the \necosystem (companies, governments, individuals), with every role (threat responders \nand analysts, technologists, information technology (IT) operators and defenders, \nvulnerability-finders, tool makers, solution providers, users, policy-makers, auditors, \netc.), and across many sectors (government, power, defense, finance, transportation, \nacademia, consulting, security, IT, etc.), who have banded together to create, adopt, and \nsupport the CIS Controls.\nEvolution of the CIS Controls\nThe CIS Controls started like many similar activities; we gathered experts together, \nand shared and argued until we reached an agreement. This can be very valuable, \ndepending on the people at the table and their experience. Through documenting and \nsharing the output, all enterprises can benefit from the work of people they cannot \nhire or even meet. You can improve the outcome (and your confidence in it) through \nselecting experts that represent a wide range of knowledge, bringing consistency to the \nprocess, and ensuring use of the best-available information (especially about attacks). \nIn the end, you are still depending on the good judgment of a relatively small group of \npeople, captured in an informal and narrative way.\nAt CIS, we have been on a multi-year path to bring more data, rigor, and transparency \nto the process of best practice recommendations (the CIS Benchmarksв„ў and the \nCIS Controls). All of these elements are essential to the maturation of a science to \nunderlie cyber defense; and, all are necessary to allow the tailoring and вҖңnegotiationвҖқ of \nsecurity actions applicable in specific cases, and as required through specific security \nframeworks, regulations, and similar oversight schemes.\nPage 1 \nCIS Controls v8 Overview\nIn the earliest versions of the CIS Controls, we used a standard list of publicly known \nattacks as a simple and informal test of the usefulness of specific recommendations. \nStarting in 2013, we worked with the Verizon Data Breach Investigations Report (DBIR) \nteam to map the results of their large-scale data analysis directly to the CIS Controls, \nas a way to match their summaries of attacks into a standard program for defensive \nimprovement.\nCIS has recently released the Community Defense Model (CDM), which is our most \ndata-driven approach so far. In its initial version, the CDM looks at the conclusions \nfrom the most recent Verizon DBIR, along with data from the Multi-State Information \nSharing and Analysis CenterВ® (MS-ISAC В®), to identify what we believe to be the \nfive most important types of attacks. We describe those attacks using the MITRE \nAdversarial Tactics, Techniques, and Common KnowledgeВ® (MITRE ATT&CKВ®) \nFramework in order to create attack patterns (or specific combinations of Tactics and \nTechniques used in those attacks). This allows us to analyze the value of individual \ndefensive actions (i.e., Safeguards1) against those attacks. Specifically, it also provides a \nconsistent and explainable way to look at the security value of a given set of defensive \nactions across the attackerвҖҷs life cycle, and provide a basis for strategies like defense-\nin-depth. The details of this analysis are available on the CIS website. The bottom \nline is that we have taken a major step towards identifying the security value of the \nCIS Controls, or any subset of them. While these ideas are still evolving, at CIS we \nare committed to the idea of security recommendations based on data, presented \ntransparently. For additional information, reference https://www.cisecurity.org/\ncontrols/v8/.\nThese activities ensure that the CIS Security Best Practices (which include the CIS \nControls and CIS Benchmarks) are more than a checklist of вҖңgood things to do, вҖқ or \nвҖңthings that could helpвҖқ; instead, they are a prescriptive, prioritized, highly focused \nset of actions that have a community support network to make them implementable, \nusable, scalable, and in alignment with all industry or government security \nrequirements.\n1 вҖңSafeguardsвҖқ were known as вҖңSub-ControlsвҖқ prior to Version 8 of the CIS Controls.\nPage 2 \nCIS Controls v8 Overview\nThis Version of the CIS Controls\nWhen we begin the work of a new version, we first sit down to establish вҖңdesign \nprinciplesвҖқ that will be used to guide the process. These serve as a decision \nвҖңtouchstoneвҖқ to remind us of what is really important, and of the goals of the CIS \nControls. While these have been fairly consistent since the earliest versions of the CIS \nControls, we have been refining our thinking over the last couple of versions to focus on \nthe role that the CIS Controls play in the total picture of enterprise security.\nOur design principles include:\nвҖў Offense Informs Defense\n вҖ“CIS Controls are selected, dropped, and prioritized based on data, and on \nspecific knowledge of attacker behavior and how to stop it\nвҖў Focus\n вҖ“Help defenders identify the most critical things they need to do to stop the most \nimportant attacks\n вҖ“Avoid being tempted to solve every security problemвҖ”avoid adding вҖңgood \nthings to doвҖқ or вҖңthings you could doвҖқ\nвҖў Feasible\n вҖ“All individual recommendations (Safeguards) must be specific and practical to \nimplement\nвҖў Measurable\n вҖ“All CIS Controls, especially for Implementation Group 1, must be measurable\n вҖ“Simplify or remove ambiguous language to avoid inconsistent interpretation\n вҖ“Some Safeguards may have a threshold\nвҖў Align\n вҖ“Create and demonstrate вҖңpeaceful co-existenceвҖқ with other governance, \nregulatory, process management schemes, framework, and structures\n вҖ“Cooperate with and point to existing, independent standards and security \nrecommendations where they exist, e.g., National Institute of Standards and \nTechnologyВ® (NISTВ®), Cloud Security Alliance (CSA), Software Assurance Forum \nfor Excellence in Code (SAFECode), ATT&CK, Open Web Application Security \nProjectВ® (OWASPВ®)\nIn addition, since Version 7, we have all seen significant changes in technology and the \ncybersecurity ecosystem. Movement to cloud-based computing, virtualization, mobility, \noutsourcing, Work-from-Home, and changing attacker tactics have been central in \nevery discussion. Physical devices, fixed boundaries, and discrete islands of security \nimplementation are less important, and so we reflect that in Version 8, through revised \nterminology and grouping of Safeguards. Also, to guide adopters in implementing \nVersion 8, CIS created a glossary to remove ambiguity of terminology. Some ideas \nhave been combined or grouped differently to more naturally reflect the evolution of \ntechnology, rather than how enterprise teams or responsibilities might be organized, \nand always referring back to our guiding principles. \nThe text of the CIS Controls document is just one step of a process to design, \nimplement, measure, report, and manage enterprise security. Taking this entire work \nstream into account as we write the CIS Controls, we can support the total enterprise \nmanagement process through: making sure that each Safeguard asks for вҖңone thing, вҖқ \nPage 3 \nCIS Controls v8 Overview\nwherever possible, in a way that is clear and requires minimal interpretation; that we \nfocus on measurable actions, and define the measurement as part of the process; and, \nthat we simplify the language to avoid duplication.\nAt CIS, we have always tried to be very conscious of the balance between addressing \ncurrent topics and the stability of an overall defensive improvement program. We have \nalways tried to focus on the foundations of good cyber defenseвҖ”and, always tried to \nkeep our eyes on emerging new defensive technologyвҖ”while avoiding the вҖңshiny new \ntoysвҖқ or complex technology that is out of reach for most enterprises.\nThe CIS Controls Ecosystem (вҖңItвҖҷs not about the listвҖқ)\nWhether you use the CIS Controls, and/or another way to guide your security \nimprovement program, you should recognize that вҖңitвҖҷs not about the list. вҖқ You can get a \ncredible list of security recommendations from many sourcesвҖ”it is best to think of the \nlist as a starting point. It is important to look for the ecosystem that grows up around \nthe list. Where can I get training, complementary information, explanations; how have \nothers implemented and used these recommendations; is there a marketplace of \nvendor tools and services to choose from; how will I measure progress or maturity; \nhow does this align with the myriad regulatory and compliance frameworks that \napply to me? The true power of the CIS Controls is not about creating the best list, it \nis about harnessing the experience of a community of individuals and enterprises to \nactually make security improvements through the sharing of ideas, tools, lessons, and \ncollective action.\nTo support this, CIS acts as a catalyst and clearinghouse to help us all learn from each \nother. Since Version 6, there has been an explosion of complementary information, \nproducts, and services available from CIS, and from the industry-at-large. Please \ncontact CIS for the following kinds of working aids and other support materials, \nhttps://www.cisecurity.org/c ontrols/v8/:\nвҖў Mappings from the CIS Controls to a very wide variety for formal Risk Management \nFrameworks (like NISTВ®, Federal Information Security Modernization Act (FISMA), \nInternational Organization for Standardization (ISO), etc.)\nвҖў Use cases of enterprise adoption\nвҖў A list of ongoing references to the CIS Controls in national and international \nstandards, state and national legislation and regulation, trade and professional \nassociations, etc.\nвҖў Information tailored for small and medium enterprises\nвҖў Measurement and metrics for the CIS Controls\nвҖў Pointers to vendor white papers and other materials that support the CIS Controls\nвҖў Documentation on alignment with the NISTВ® Cybersecurity Framework\nPage 4 \nCIS Controls v8 Overview\nHow to Get Started\nHistorically, the CIS Contro')
```

Annual Registration Fee.  .................................................................................................... 67 \n \n  \nCPPA  \n \nPage 3 of 67 CHAPTER 1.  CALIFORNIA CONSUMER PRIVACY ACT REGULATIONS \nARTICLE 1 . GENERAL PROVISIONS  \nВ§ 7000. Title and Scope . \n(a)  This Chapter shall be known as the California Consumer Privacy Act  Regulations . It may be \ncited as such and will be referred to in this Chapter as вҖңthese regulations.вҖқ These \nregulations govern compliance with the California Consumer Privacy Act and do not limit \nany other rights that consumers may have .  \n(b)  A violation of these regulations shall constitute a violation of the CCPA and be subject to the remedies provided for therein.  \nNote: Authority cited: Section s 1798.175 and  1798.185, Civil Code . Reference: Sections \n1798.100, 1798.105, 1798.106, 1798.110, 1798.115, 1798.120, 1798.121, 1798.125, 1798.130, \n1798.135, 1798.140, 1798.145, 1798.150, 1798.155, 1798.175, 1798.185, 1798.199.40, \n1798.199.45, 1798.199.50, 1798.199.55 and 1798.199.65, Civil Code.  \nВ§ 7001. Definitions . \nIn addition to the definitions set forth in Civil Code section 1798.140, for purposes of these regulations:  \n(a)  вҖңAgencyвҖқ means the California Privacy Protection Agency established by Civil Code section \n1798.199.10 et seq.  \n(b) вҖңAlternative Opt -out LinkвҖқ means the alternative opt -out link that a business may provide \ninstead of posting the two separate вҖңDo Not Sell o r Share My Personal InformationвҖқ and \nвҖңLimit the Use of My Sensitive Personal Information вҖқ links as set forth in Civil Code section \n1798. 135, subdivision ( a)(3), and specified in section 70 15. \n(c)   вҖңAttorney GeneralвҖқ means the California Attorney General or any officer or employee of \nthe California Department of Justice acting under the authority of the California Attorney \nGeneral.  \n(d)   вҖңAuthorized agentвҖқ means a natural person or a business entity that a consumer has authorized to act on their behalf subject to the requirements set forth in section 7063.  \n(e)   вҖңCategories of sourcesвҖқ means types  or groupings  of persons or entities from which a \nbusiness collects personal information about consumers , described with enough \nparticularity to provide consumers with a meaningful understanding of the type  of person \nor entity . They may  includ e the consumer directly, advertising networks, internet service \nproviders, data analytics providers, governm ent entities , operating systems and platforms, \nsocial networks , and data brokers . \n(f)   вҖңCategories of third partiesвҖқ means types or groupings of third parties with whom the \nbusiness shares  personal information , described with enough particularity to provide \nCPPA  \n \nPage 4 of 67 consumers with a meaningful understanding of the type of third party . They may include \nadvertising networks, internet service providers, data analytics providers, government \nentities, operating systems and platforms, social networks, and data brokers . \n(g)   вҖңCCPAвҖқ means the California Consumer Privacy Act  of 2018, Civil Code section  1798.100 et \nseq. \n(h)   вҖңCOPPAвҖқ means the ChildrenвҖҷs Online Privacy Protection Act, 15 U.S.C. sections 6501 to  \n6506  and 16 Code of Federal Regulations part 312.  \n(i)  вҖңDisproportionate effortвҖқ within the context of a business, service provider, contractor, or \nthird party  responding  to a consumer request means the time and /or resources expended \nby the business, service provider, contractor, or third party  to respond to the individualized \nrequest  significantly outweighs the reasonably foreseeable impact to the consumer by not \nresponding, taking into account applicable circumstances , such as the size of the business, \nservice provider, contractor, or third party , the nature of the request, and the technical \nlimitations impacting their  ability to respond . For example , responding to a consumer \nrequest to know may require disproportionate effort when the personal information that is \nthe subject of the request is  not in a searchable or readily -acces sible format, is maintained \nonly for legal or compliance purposes , is not sold or used for any commercial purpose , and \nthere is no reasonably foreseeable material impact to the consumer by not responding . By \ncontrast, the impact  to the consumer of denying a request to correct inaccurate \ninformation that the business uses and/or sells may outweigh the burden on the business, \nservice provider, contractor, or third party  in honoring the request when the reasonably \nforeseeable consequence of denying the request would be the denial of services or \nopportunities  to the consumer . A business, service provider, contractor, or third party  that \nhas failed to put in place adequate processes and procedures to receive and process \nconsumer requests in accordance with the CCPA and these regulations can not claim that \nresponding to a consumer вҖҷs request r equires disproportionate effort.  \n(j)   вҖңEmployment benefitsвҖқ means retirement, health, and other benefit programs, services, or \nproducts to which consumers  and their dependents  or their beneficiaries receive access \nthrough the consumerвҖҷs employer .  \n(k)   вҖңEmployment -related informationвҖқ means personal information that is collected by the \nbusiness about a natural person for the reasons identified in Civil Code section 1798.145, \nsubdivision (m)(1). The collection of employment -related information, including for the \npurpose of administering  employment benefits,  shall be considered a b usiness purpose .  \n(l)   вҖңFinancial incentiveвҖқ means a program, benefit, or other offering , including payments to \nconsumers , for the collection, retention, sale, or sharing of personal information . Price or \nservice differences are types  of financial incentive s. \n(m)  вҖңFirst partyвҖқ means a consumer -facing business with which  the consumer intends and \nexpects to interact.  \nCPPA  \n \nPage 5 of 67 (n)   вҖңFrictionless mannerвҖқ means a business вҖҷs processing of an opt -out preference signal that \ncomplie s with the requirements set forth in section 7025, subsection (f).  \n(o) вҖңInformation practicesвҖқ means  practices regarding the collection, use, disclosure, sale, \nsharing, and retention of personal information . \n(p) вҖңNonbusinessвҖқ means a person or entity that does not meet the definition of a вҖң businessвҖқ \nas defined in Civil Co de section 1798.140, sub division  (d). For example, non -profits and \ngovernment entities are nonbusinesses because вҖң businessвҖқ is defined , among other things,  \nto include only entities вҖңorganized or operated for the profit or financial benefit of its \nshareholders or other owners.вҖқ  \n(q)  вҖңNotice at C ollectionвҖқ means the notice given by a business to a consumer at or before the \npoint at which a business collect s personal information from the consumer as re quired by \nCivil Code section  1798.100, subdivision s (a) and  (b), and specified in these regulations.  \n(r)  вҖңNotice of R ight to LimitвҖқ means the notice given by a business informing consumers of \ntheir right to limit the use or disclosure of the consumerвҖҷs sensitive personal information as \nrequired by Civil Code sections  1798.121  and 1798.135 and specified in these regulations.  \n(s)  вҖңNotice of R ight to O pt-out of Sale/Sharing вҖқ means the notice given by a business \ninforming consumers of their right to opt- out of the sale or sharing of their personal \ninformat ion as required by Civil Code section s 1798.120 and 1798.135 and specified in \nthese regulations.  \n(t)   вҖңNotice of F inancial IncentiveвҖқ means the notice given by a business explaining each \nfinancial incentive or price or service difference as required by Civil Code section  1798.125, \nsubdivision (b), and specified in these regulations.  \n(u)   вҖңOpt -out preference signalвҖқ means a signal that is sent by a platform, technology, or \nmechanism, on behalf of the consumer , that communicates the consumer choice to opt-\nout of the sale and sharing of personal information and that complies with the \nrequirements set forth in section 7025, subsection (b) . \n(v)  вҖңPrice or service differenceвҖқ means (1) any difference in the price or rate charged for any \ngoods or services to any consumer  related to the collection, retention , sale , or sharing of \npersonal information , or (2) any difference in the level or quality of any goods or services \noffered to any consumer  related to the collection, retention , sale,  or sharing of personal \ninformation , including the denial of goods or services to the consumer.  \n(w) вҖңPrivacy policy ,вҖқ as referred to in Civil Code section s 1798.130, subdivision (a)(5),  and \n1798.135, subdivision (c)(2),  means the statement that a business shall make available to \nconsumers describing the businessвҖҷs online and offline  information practices , and the \nrights of consumers regarding their own personal information.  \n(x)   вҖңRequest to correctвҖқ means a consumer request that a  business correct inaccurate \npersonal information that it maintains about the consumer, pursuant to Civil Code section \n1798.106.  \nCPPA  \n \nPage 6 of 67 (y)  вҖңRequest to deleteвҖқ means a consumer request that a business delete pe rsonal information \nabout the consumer that the business has collected from the consumer, pursuant to Civil \nCode section 1798.105.  \n(z)   вҖңRequest to knowвҖқ means a consumer request that a business disclose personal information that it has collected about the  consumer pursuant to Civil Code sections  \n1798.110 or 1798.115. It includes a request for any or all of the following:  \n(1)  Specific pieces of personal information that a business has collected about the \nconsumer ;  \n(2)  Categories of personal information it has collected about the consumer;  \n(3)  Categories of sources from which the personal information is collected;  \n(4)  Categories of personal information that the business sold or disclosed for a business purpose about the cons umer;  \n(5)  Categories of third parties to whom the personal information was sold or disclosed for a  business purpose ; and  \n(6)  The business or commercial purpose for collecting or  selling personal \ninformation.  \n(aa) вҖңRequest to limitвҖқ means a consumer request that a  business limit the use and \ndisclosure of the consumerвҖҷs sensitive personal information, pursuant to Civil Code \nsection 1798.121, subdivision (a).  \n(bb) вҖңRequest to opt -in to sale/sharingвҖқ means an action demonstrating that the \nconsumer has consented to the businessвҖҷs sale or sharing of personal information about the consumer by a parent or guardian of a consumer less than 13 years of \nage or by a consumer at least 13 years of age . \n(cc)  вҖңRequest to opt -out of sale /sharing вҖқ means a consumer request that a business \nneither sell nor share the consumerвҖҷs personal information to third parties, \npursuant to Civil Code section 1798.120, subdivision (a).  \n(dd) вҖңRight to correctвҖқ means the consumerвҖҷs right to request  that a business correct \ninaccurate personal information that it maintains about the consumer as set forth \nin Civil Code section 1798.106.  \n(ee) вҖңRight to deleteвҖқ means the consumerвҖҷs right to request that a business delete any \npersonal information about the consumer that the business has collected from the consumer  as set forth in Civil Cod e section 1798.105.  \n(ff) вҖңRight  to knowвҖқ means the consumerвҖҷs right to request that a business disclose \npersonal information that it has collected , sold, or shared  about the consumer  as \nset forth in Civil Code sections 1798.110 and 1798.115.  \nCPPA  \n \nPage 7 of 67 (gg) вҖңRight  to limitвҖқ means the  consumer вҖҷs right to  request that a business limit the use \nand disclosure of a consumer вҖҷs sensitive personal information  as set forth  in Civil \nCode section 1798.121.  \n(hh) вҖңRight  to opt -out of sale /sharing вҖқ means the consumer вҖҷs right to direct a business \nthat sells or share s personal information about the consumer to third parties to \nstop doing so as set forth in Civil Code section 1798.120.  \n(ii) вҖңSignedвҖқ means that the written attestation, declaration, or permission has either \nbeen physically signed or provided electronically in accordance with the Uniform \nElectronic Transactions Act, Civil Code section 1633. 1 et seq . \n(jj) вҖңThird -party identity verification serviceвҖқ means a security process offered by an \nindependent third party that verifies the identity of the consumer making a request \nto the business . Third -party identity verification services are subject to the \nrequiremen ts set forth in  Article 5 regarding requests to delete , request s to correct, \nor request s to know . \n(kk) вҖңUnstructuredвҖқ as it relates to personal information means personal information \nthat is not or ganized in a pre -defined manner  and could not be retrieved or \norganized in a pre -defined manner without disproportionate effort on behalf of the \nbusiness, service provider, contractor, or t hird party. \n(ll) вҖңValue of the consumerвҖҷs dataвҖқ means the value provided to the business by the \nconsumerвҖҷs data  as calculated under section 7081 . \n(mm)  вҖңVerifyвҖқ means to determine that the consumer making a request to delete , \nrequest to correct, or request to know is the consumer about whom the business \nhas collected information , or if  that consumer is less than 13 years of age , the \nconsumerвҖҷs parent or legal guardian . \nNote: Authority cited: Sections 1798.175 and 1798.185, Civil Code . Reference: Sections \n1798.100, 1798.105, 1798.106, 1798.110, 1798.115, 1798.120, 1798.121, 1798.125, 1798.130, 1798.135, 1798.140, 1798.145, 1798.150, 1798.155, 1798.175, 1798.185, 1798.199.40, \n1798.199.45, 1798.199.50, 1798.199.55 and 1798.199.65, Civil Code.  \nВ§ 7002.  Restrictions on the Collection and Use of Personal Information.  \n(a) In accordance with Civil Code section 1798.100, subdivision (c), a businessвҖҷs collection, use, \nretention, and/or sharing of a consumerвҖҷs personal information shall be reasonably \nnecessary and proportionate to achieve:  \n(1) The purpose(s) for which the personal information was collected or processed, which shall comply with  the requirements  set forth in subsection (b); or \n(2) Another disclosed purpose that is compatible with the context in which the personal \ninformation was c ollected, which shall comply with  the requirements set forth in \nsubsection (c).  \nCPPA  \n \nPage 8 of 67 (b)  The purpose(s) for which the personal information was collected or processed shall be \nconsistent with the reasonable expectations of the consumer (s) whose personal \ninformation is collected or processed . The consumerвҖҷs (or consumersвҖҷ) reasonable \nexpectations concerning the purpose for which the ir personal information will be collected \nor processed shall be based on the following:  \n(1) The relationship between the consumer (s) and the b usiness. For example, if the \nconsumer is intentionally interacting with the business on its website to purchase a \ngood or service, the consumer likely expects that the purpose for collecting or processing the personal information is to  provide that good or service . By contrast, \nfor example, the consumer of a businessвҖҷs mobile flashlight application would  not \nexpect the business to collect the consumerвҖҷs geolocation information to provide the \nflashlight service.  \n(2) The type, nature, and amount of personal information that the business  seeks to \ncollect or process . For example, if a businessвҖҷs mobile communication application \nrequests access to the  consumerвҖҷs contact list in order to call a specific individual, the \nconsumer who is providing their contact list likely expects that the purpose of the \nbusinessвҖҷs use of that contact list will be to connect the consumer with the specific \ncontact they selected . Similarly, if a business collects the consumerвҖҷs fingerprint in \nconnection with setting up the security feature of unlocking the device using the \nfingerprint, the consumer likely expects that the businessвҖҷs use of the consumerвҖҷs \nfingerprint is only for the purpose of unlocking their mobile device.  \n(3) The source of the personal information and the businessвҖҷs method for collecti ng or \nprocessing it . For example, if the consumer is providing their personal information \ndirectly to the business  while using the business вҖҷs product or service, the consumer \nlikely expects that the business  will use the personal information to provide that \nproduct or service . However, the consumer may not expect that the business will use \nthat same personal information for a different product or service offered by the \nbusiness or the businessвҖҷs subsidiary.  \n(4) The specificity, explicitness, prominence , and clarity  of disclosures to the consumer (s) \nabout the purpose for collecting or processing the ir personal information, such as in \nthe Notice at Collection and in the marketing materials to the consumer (s) about the \nbusinessвҖҷs good or service . For example, the  consumer who receives a pop-up notice \nthat the business  wants to collect the consumerвҖҷs phone number to verify their \nidentity when they log  in likely expects that the business will use their phone number \nfor the purpose of verifying the consumerвҖҷs identity  and not for marketing purposes . \nSimilarly, the consumer may expect that a mobile application that markets itself as a \nservice that finds gas prices near  the consumer вҖҷs location  will collect and use the  \nconsumerвҖҷs  geolocation information  for that specific purpose when they are using \nthe service.  \n(5) The degree to which the involvement of service providers, contractors, third parties, \nor other entities in the collecti ng or processing of personal information is apparent to \nCPPA  \n \nPage 9 of 67 the consumer (s). For example, the consumer likely expects an online retailerвҖҷs \ndisclosure of the  consumerвҖҷs  name and address to a  delivery service provider in order \nfor that  service provider to deliver a purchased product,  because th at service \nproviderвҖҷs involvement  is apparent to the consumer . By contrast, the consumer may \nnot expect the disclosure of personal information to a service provider if the \nconsumer is not directly interacting with the service provider or the service providerвҖҷs \nrole in the processing is not apparent to the consumer.  \n(c)  Whether another disclosed purpose is compatible with the context in which the personal \ninformation was collected  shall be based on the following:  \n(1) At the time of collection of the personal information, the reasonable expectations of \nthe consumer(s)  whose personal information is collected or processed  concerning the \npurpose for which the ir personal information will be collected or processed, based on \nthe factors set forth in subsection (b).  \n(2) The other disclosed purpose for which the business  seeks to further collect or process \nthe consumerвҖҷs personal information, including whether it is  a business purpose \nlisted in Civil Code section 1798.140, subdivisions (e)(1) through (e)(8).  \n(3)  The strength of the link between subsection (c)(1) and subsection (c)(2). For example, \na strong link exists between the consumerвҖҷs reasonable expectations that the \npersonal information w ill be used to provide them with a requested service at the \ntime of collection, and the use of the information to repair errors that impair the intended functionality of that requested service . This would weigh in favor of \ncompatibility . By contrast, for example , a weak link exists between the  consumerвҖҷs \nreasonable  expectations that the personal information will  be collected to provide a \nrequested cloud storage service at the time of collection, and the use of the \ninformation to research and develop an unrelated facial recognition service.  \n(d) For each purpose identified in compliance with subsection (a)(1) or (a)(2), the collection, use, retention, and/or sharin g of a  consumerвҖҷs personal information to achieve that \npurpose shall be reasonably necessary and proportionate . The businessвҖҷs collection, use, \nretention, and/or sharing of a consumerвҖҷs personal information shall also be reasonably \nnecessary and proportionate to achieve any  purpose for which the business obtains the \nconsumerвҖҷs consent in compliance with subsection (e) . Whether a businessвҖҷs  collection, \nuse, retention, and/or sharing of a consumerвҖҷs personal information is reasonably necessary and p roportionate to achieve the  purpose identified  in compliance with \nsubsection (a)(1) or (a)(2 ), or any purpose for which the business obtains consent, shall be \nbased on the following:  \n(1) The minimum personal information that is necessary to achieve the pur pose  \nidentified in compliance with subsection (a)(1) or  (a)(2), or any purpose for which the \nbusiness obtains consent . For example, to complete an online purchase and send an \nemail confirmation of the purchase to the consumer, an online retailer may need the consumerвҖҷs order information, payment and shipping  information, and email address.  \nCPPA  \n \nPage 10 of 67 (2) The possible negative impacts  on consumers posed by the business вҖҷs collection or \nprocessing of the personal information . For example, a possible negative impact of \ncollecting precise geolocation information is that it may reveal other sensitive \npersonal information about the consumer,  such as health information based on visits \nto healthcare providers.  \n(3) The existence of additional safeguards for the personal information to specifically address the possible negative impacts on consumers considered by the business in \nsubsection (d)(2) . For example, a business  may consider encryption or automatic \ndeletion of personal information within a specific window of time as potential safeguards.  \n(e)  A business shall obtain the consumerвҖҷs consent in accordance with section 7004 before \ncollecting or processing personal information for any purpose that does not meet the \nrequirements set forth in subsection (a).  \n(f) A business shall not collect categories of pers')
```

All Rights Reserved.  Page ii \n \nDocument Changes  \nDate  Version  Description  \nOctober 2008  1.2 To introduce PCI DSS v1.2 as вҖңPCI DSS Requirements and Security Assessment Procedures,вҖқ eliminating \nredundancy between documents, and making both general and specific changes from PCI DSS Security Audit \nProcedures v1.1.
