All rights reserved.   Page 1 \n1 Introduction and PCI Data Security Standard Overview   \nThe Payment Card Industry Data Security Standard (PCI DSS) was developed to encourage and enhance payment card account data security \nand facilitate the broad adoption of consistent data security measures globally. PCI DSS provides a baseline of technical and operational \nrequirements designed to protect account data. While specifically designed to focus on environments with payment card account  data, PCI \nDSS can also be used to protect against threats and secure other elements in the payment ecosystem.  \nTable 1 shows the 12 principal PCI DSS requirements.  \nTable 1. Principal PCI DSS Requirements  \nPCI Data Security Standard вҖ“ High Level Overview  \nBuild and Maintain a Secure Network and Systems  1. Install and Maintain Network Security Controls . \n2. Apply Secure Configurations to All System Components . \nProtect Account Data  3. Protect Stored Account Data. \n4. Protect Cardholder Data with Strong Cryptography During \nTransmission Over Open, Public Networks . \nMaintain a Vulnerability Management Program  5. Protect All Systems and Networks from Malicious Software . \n6. Develop and Maintain Secure Systems and Software . \nImplement Strong Access Control Measures  7. Restrict Access to System Components and Cardholder Data by \nBusiness Need to K now. \n8. Identify Users and Authenticate Access to S ystem Components . \n9. Restrict Physical Access to C ardholder Data. \nRegularly Monitor and Test Networks  10. Log and Monitor All Access to System Components and Cardholder \nData. \n11. Test Security of S ystems and Networks Regularly . \nMaintain an Information Security Policy  12. Support Information Security with Organizational Policies and \nPrograms . \nThis document, the Payment Card Industry  Data Security Standard Requirements and Testing  Procedures, consists of the 12 PCI DSS \nprincipal requirements, detailed security requirements, corresponding testing procedures, and other information pertinent to each requirement. \nThe following sections provide detailed guidelines and best practices to assist entities to prepare for, conduct, and report the results of a PCI DSS assessment. The PCI DSS requirements and testing procedures begin on page 43. \n \n \nPayment Card Industry Data Security Standard: Requirements and Testing Procedures , v4.0  March 2022  \nВ© 2006 - 2022 PCI Security Standards Council, LLC . All rights reserved.   Page 2 \nPCI DSS comprises a minimum set of requirements for pr otecting account data and may be enhanced by additional controls and practices to \nfurther mitigate risks, and to incorporate local, regional, and sector laws and regulations. Additionally, legislation or regulatory requirements \nmay require specific protect ion of personal information or other data elements (for example, cardholder name).  \nLimitations  \nIf any of the requirements contained in this standard conflict with country, state, or local laws, the country, state, or loc al law will apply.  \nPCI DSS Resource s \nThe PCI Security Standards Council (PCI SSC) website ( www.pcisecuritystandards.org) provides the following additional resources to assist \norganizations with their PCI DSS assessments and validations:  \n\uf0a7 Document  Library, including:  \nвҖ“ PCI DSS  Summary of Changes  \nвҖ“ PCI DSS Quick Reference Guide  \nвҖ“ Information Supplements and Guidelines  \nвҖ“ Prioritized Approach for PCI DSS  \nвҖ“ Report on Compliance ( ROC) Reporting Template and Reporting Instructions  \nвҖ“ Self-Assessment Questionnaires (SAQs) and SAQ Instructions and Guidelines  \nвҖ“ Attestations of Compliance (AOCs ) \n\uf0a7 Frequently Asked Questions (FAQs)  \n\uf0a7 PCI for Small Merchants website  \n\uf0a7 PCI training courses and infor mational webinars  \n\uf0a7 List of Qualified Security Assessors (QSAs) and Approved Scanning Vendors ( ASVs ) \n\uf0a7 Lists of PCI approved devices, applications , and solutions  \n \n \nPayment Card Industry Data Security Standard: Requirements and Testing Procedures , v4.0  March 2022  \nВ© 2006 - 2022 PCI Security Standards Council, LLC . All rights reserved.   Page 3 \nThere are over 60 guidance documents and information supplements available on the PCI SSC website that provide specific guidance and \nconsiderations for PCI DSS. Examples include:  \n\uf0a7 Guidance for PCI DSS Scoping and Network Segmentation  \n\uf0a7 PCI SSC Cloud Computing Guidelines  \n\uf0a7 Multi -Factor Authentication Guidance  \n\uf0a7 Third -Party Security Assurance  \n\uf0a7 Effective Daily Log Monitoring  \n\uf0a7 Penetration Testing Guidance \n\uf0a7 Best Practices for Implementing a Security Awareness Program  \n\uf0a7 Best Practices for Maintaining PCI DSS Compliance  \n\uf0a7 PCI DSS for Large Organizations  \n\uf0a7 Use of SSL/Early TLS and Impact on ASV Scans  \n\uf0a7 Use of SSL/Early TLS for POS POI Terminal Connections  \n\uf0a7 Tokenization Product Security Guidelines  \n\uf0a7 Protecting Telephone- Based Payment Card Data  \nRefer to the Document Library at www.pcisecuritystandards.org for information about these and other resources.  \nIn addition, r efer to Appendix G  for definitions of PCI DSS terms.  Note : Information Supplements \ncomplement PCI DSS and identify \nadditional considerations and \nrecommendations for meeting PCI \nDSS requirements. Information \nSupplements do not supersede, \nreplace, or extend PCI DSS or any \nof its requirements.  \n \n \nPayment Card Industry Data Security Standard: Requirements and Testing Procedures , v4.0  March 2022  \nВ© 2006 - 2022 PCI Security Standards Council, LLC . All rights reserved.   Page 4 \n2 PCI DSS Applicability Informatio n \nPCI DSS is intended for all entities that store, process, or transmit cardholder data (CHD) and/or sensitive authentication data (SAD) or could \nimpact the security of the cardholder data environment (CDE). This includes all entities involved in payment c ard account processing вҖ”\nincluding merchants, processors, acquirers , issuers, and other service providers.  \nWhether any entity is required to comply with or validate their compliance to PCI DSS is at the discretion of those organizat ions that manage \ncomplia nce programs (such as payment brands  and acquirers). Contact the organizations of interest for any additional criteria.  \nDefining Account Data, Cardholder Data, and Sensitive Authentication Data  \nCardholder data and sensitive authentication data are consider ed account data and are defined as follows:  \nTable 2. Account Data  \nAccount Data  \nCardholder Data includes:  Sensitive Authentication Data includes : \nвҖў Primary Account Number (PAN)  \nвҖў Cardholder Name  \nвҖў Expiration Date  \nвҖў Service Code  вҖў Full track data (magnetic -stripe data or equivalent on a chip)  \nвҖў Card verification code  \nвҖў PINs/PIN blocks  \n \nPCI DSS requirements apply to entities with environments where account data (cardholder data and/or sensitive authentication data) is stored, \nprocessed, or transmitted, and entities with environments that can impact the security of the CDE. Some PCI DSS requirements may also \napply to entities with environments that do not store, process, or transmit account data вҖ“ for example, entities that outsource payment \noperations or management of their CDE1. Entities that outsource their payment environments or payment operations to third parties remain \nresponsible for ensuring that the account data is protected by the third party per applicable PCI DSS requirements .  \nThe primary account number (PAN) is the defining factor for cardholder data. The term account data therefore covers the following: the full \nPAN, any other elements of cardholder data that are present with the PAN, and any elements of sensitive authentic ation data.  \n \n \n1 In accordance with those organizations that manage compliance programs  (such as payment brands  and acquirers) ; entities should contact the organizations of \ninterest for more details.  \n \n \nPayment Card Industry Data Security Standard: Requirements and Testing Procedures , v4.0  March 2022  \nВ© 2006 - 2022 PCI Security Standards Council, LLC . All rights reserved.   Page 5 \nIf cardholder name, service code, and/or expiration date are stored, processed, or transmitted with the PAN, or are otherwise present in the \nCDE, they must be protected in accordance with the PCI DSS requirements applicable to cardholder data.   \nIf an entity stores, processes, or transmits PAN, then a CDE exists to which PCI DSS requirements will apply. Some requirements may not be \napplicable, for example if the entity does not store PAN, then the requirements relating to the protection of stored PAN in Requirement 3 will \nnot be applicable to the entity.  \nEven if an entity does not store, process, or transmit PAN, some PCI DSS requirements may still apply. Consider the following : \n\uf0a7 If the entity stores SAD, requirements specifically related to SAD storage in Requirement 3 will be applicable.  \n\uf0a7 If the entity engages third- party service providers to store, process or transmit PAN on its behalf, requirements related to the \nmanagement of service providers in Requirement 12 will be applicable.  \n\uf0a7 If the entity can impact the security of a CDE because the security of an entityвҖҷs infrastructure can affect how cardholder data is \nprocessed (for example, via a web server that controls the generation of a payment form or page) some requirements will be applicab le. \n\uf0a7 If cardholder data is only present on physical media (for example paper), requirements relating to the security and disposal of physical \nmedia in Requirement 9 will be applicable.  \n\uf0a7 Requirements related to an incident response plan are applicable to all entities, to ensure that there are procedures to follow in the \nevent of a suspected or actual breach of the confidentiality of cardholder data.  \nUse of Account Data, Sensitive Authentication Data, Cardholder Data, and Primary Account Number in PCI DSS  \nPCI D SS includes requirements that specifically refer to account data, cardholder data, and sensitive authentication data. It is i mportant \nto note that each of these types of data are different and the terms are not interchangeable. Specific references within r equirements to \naccount data, cardholder data, or sensitive authentication data are purposeful, and the requirements apply specifically to the type of data that is referenced.  \n \n \nPayment Card Industry Data Security Standard: Requirements and Testing Procedures , v4.0  March 2022  \nВ© 2006 - 2022 PCI Security Standards Council, LLC . All rights reserved.   Page 6 \nElements of Account Data and Storage Requirements  \nTable 3 identifies the elements  of cardholder and sensitive authentication data, whether storage of each data element is permitted or \nprohibited, and whether each data element must be rendered unreadableвҖ”for example, with strong cryptography вҖ”when stored. This \ntable is not exhaustive and is presented to illustrate only how the stated requirements apply to the different data elements.  \nTable 3. Account Data Element Storage Requirements  \n  Data Elements  Storage Restrictions  Required to Render Stored Data \nUnreadable  Account Data  Cardholder \nData  Primary Account Number (PAN) Storage is kept to a minimum as defined in \nRequirement 3.2  Yes, as defined in Requirement 3.5  \nCardholder Name  \nStorage is kept to a minimum as defined in \nRequirement 3.22 No Service Code  \nExpiration Date  \nSensitive \nAuthentication \nData  Full Track Data  \nCannot be stored after authorization as \ndefined in Requirement 3.3.13 Yes, data stored until authorization is \ncomplete must be protected with strong \ncryptography as defined in Requirement \n3.3.2  Card verification code  \nPIN/PIN Block  \n \nIf PAN is stored with other elements of cardholder data, only the PAN must be rendered unreadable according to PCI DSS Requir ement 3.5.1.  \nSensitive authentication data must not be stored after authorization, even if encrypted. This applies even for environments where there is no \nPAN present.  \n \n \n2 Where data exists in the same environment as PAN.  \n3 Except as permitte d for issuers and companies that support issuing services. Requirements for issuers and issuing services are separately defined in \nRequirement 3.3.3.   \n \n \nPayment Card Industry Data Security Standard: Requirements and Testing Procedures , v4.0  March 2022  \nВ© 2006 - 2022 PCI Security Standards Council, LLC . All rights reserved.   Page 7 \n3 Relationship between PCI DSS and PCI SSC Software Standards \nPCI SSC supports the use of secure payment software within cardholder data environments (CDE) via the Payment Application Dat a Security \nStandard (PA -DSS) and the Software Security Framework (SSF), which consists of the Secure Software Standard and the Secure Software \nLifecycle (Secure SLC) Standard. Software that is PCI SSC validated and listed provides assurance that the software has been developed \nusing secure practices and has met a defined set of software security requirements.  \nThe PCI SSC secure software programs include listings of payment  software and software \nvendors that have been validated as meeting the applicable PCI SSC Software Standards.  \n\uf0a7 Validated Software : Payment software listed on the PCI SSC website as a \nValidated Payment Application (PA -DSS) or Validated Payment Software (the \nSecure Software Standard) has been evaluated by a qualified assessor to confirm \nthe software meets the security requirements within that standard. The security \nrequirements in these standards are focused on protecting the integrity and \nconfidentiality of p ayment transactions and account data.  \n\uf0a7 Validated Software Vendors : The Secure SLC Standard defines security \nrequirements for software vendors to integrate secure software development practices throughout the entire software lifecycle. Software vendors that  have been \nvalidated as meeting the Secure SLC Standard are listed on the PCI SSC website as a Secure SLC Qualified Vendor.  \nFor more information about the SSF or PA -DSS, refer to the respective Program Guides  at www.pcisecuritystandards.org.  \nAll software that stores, processes, or transmits account data, or that could impact the security of account data or a CDE, i s in scope for an \nentityвҖҷs PCI DSS assessment. While the use of validated payment software supports the security of an entityвҖҷs CDE , the use of such software \ndoes not by itself make an entity PCI DSS compliant. The entityвҖҷs PCI DSS assessment should include verification that the sof tware is properly \nconfigured and securely implemented to support applicable PCI DSS requirements. Additionally, if PCI -listed payment software has been \ncustomized, a more in- depth review will be required during the PCI DSS assessment because the software may no longer be representative of \nthe version that was originally validated.  \nBecause security threats ar e constantly evolving, software that is no longer supported by the vendor (for example, identified by the vendor as \nвҖңend of lifeвҖқ) may not offer the same level of security as supported versions. Entities are strongly encouraged to keep their  software current \nand updated  to the latest software versions available.   \nEntities that develop their own software are encouraged to refer to PCI SSCвҖҷs software security standards and consider the requirements therein as best practices to use in their development environments. Secure payment software implemented in a PCI DSS compliant \nenvironment will help minimize the potential for security breaches leading to compromises of account data and fraud.  See Bespoke and \nCustom Software . Note : PA-DSS and the rel ated program will be \nretired in October 2022. Refer to the PCI SSC \nList of Validated Payment Applications for \nexpiry dates for PA -DSS validated \napplications . After the expiry date, applications \nare listed as вҖңAcceptable only for Pre- Existing \nDeployments.вҖқ Whether an entity can continue \nto use a PA -DSS application with an expired \nlisting is at the discretion of organizations that \nmanage compliance programs (such as \npayment brands  and acquirers ); entities should \ncontact the organizations of interest for more \ndetails.  \n \n \nPayment Card Industry Data Security Standard: Requirements and Testing Procedures , v4.0  March 2022  \nВ© 2006 - 2022 PCI Security Standards Council, LLC . All rights reserved.   Page 8 \nApplicability of PCI DSS to Payment Software  Vendors  \nPCI DSS may apply to a payment software vendor if the vendor  is also a service provider that  stores , process es, or transmit s account \ndata, or has  access to their customersвҖҷ account data вҖ”for example, in the role of a payment service provider  or via remote access to a \ncustomer environment . Software vendors to which PCI DSS may be applicable include those offering payment services,  as well as cloud \nservice providers offering payment terminals in the cloud, software as a service (SaaS), e- commerce in the cloud, and other cloud \npayment services.  \nBespoke and Custom Software \nAll bespoke and custom software that stores, processes, or transmits \naccount data, or that could impact the security of account data or a CDE, is in scope for an entityвҖҷs PCI DSS assessment.  \nBespoke and custom software that has been developed and maintained \nin accordance with one of PCI SSCвҖҷs Software Security Framework \nstandards (the Secure Software Standard or the Secure SLC standard) will support an entity in meeting PCI DSS Requirement 6.  \n \nSee Appendix F  for more details.  Note : PCI DSS Requirement 6 fully applies to bespoke \nand custom software that has not been developed and \nmaintained in accordance with one of PCI SSCвҖҷs Software \nSecurity Framework standards. Entities that use software \nvendors to develop bespoke or custom software that \ncould impact the security of account data or their CDE are \nresponsible for ensuring those software vendors develop \nthe software according to PCI DSS Requirement 6.  \n \n \nPayment Card Industry Data Security Standard: Requirements and Testing Procedures , v4.0  March 2022  \nВ© 2006 - 2022 PCI Security Standards Council, LLC . All rights reserved.   Page 9 \n4 Scope of PCI DSS Requirements  \nPCI DSS requirements apply to:  \n\uf0a7 The cardholder data environment (CDE), which is comprised of:  \nвҖ“ System components, people, and processes that store, process, and transmit cardholder data and/or sensitive authentication data, \nand,  \nвҖ“ System components that may not store, process, or transmit CHD/SAD but have unrestricted connectivity to system components \nthat store, process, or transmit CHD/SAD.  \nAND  \n\uf0a7 System  components, people, and processes that could impact the security of the CDE.4  \nвҖңSystem componentsвҖқ include network devices, servers, computing devices, virtual components, cloud components, and software. Examples \nof system components include but are not limited to:  \n\uf0a7 Systems that store, process, or transmit account data (for example, payment terminals, authorization systems, clearing systems, \npayment middleware systems, payment back -office systems, shopping cart and store front systems, payment gate way/switch systems, \nfraud monitoring systems).  \n\uf0a7 Systems that provide security services (for example, authentication servers, access control servers, security information and  event \nmanagement (SIEM) systems, physical security systems (for example, badge acc ess or CCTV), multi -factor authentication systems, \nanti-malware systems).  \n\uf0a7 Systems that facilitate segmentation (for example, internal network security controls).  \n\uf0a7 Systems that could impact the security of account data or the CDE (for example, name resolutio n, or e- commerce (web) redirection \nservers).  \n\uf0a7 Virtualization components such as virtual machines, virtual switches/routers, virtual appliances, virtual applications /desktops , and \nhypervisors.  \n\uf0a7 Cloud infrastructure and components, both external and on premises, and including instantiations of containers  or images, virtual private \nclouds, cloud- based identity and access management, CDEs residing on premises or in the cloud, service meshes with containerized \napplications , and container orchestration tools.  \n \n \n4 For additional guidance, refer to Information Supplement: Guidance for PCI DSS Scoping and Network Segmentation on the PCI SSC website.  \n \n \nPayment Card Industry Data Security Standard: Requirements and Testing Procedures , v4.0  March 2022  \nВ© 2006 - 2022 PCI Security Standards Council, LLC . All rights reserved.   Page 10 \n\uf0a7 Network components, including but not limited to network security controls, switches, routers, VoIP network devices, wireless access \npoints, network appliances, and other security appliances.  \n\uf0a7 Server types, including but not limited to web, application, database, authentication, mail, proxy, Network Time Protocol (NTP), and \nDomain Name System (DNS).  \n\uf0a7 End-user devices, such as computers, laptops, workstations, administrative workstations, tablets, and mobile devices.  \n\uf0a7 Printers, and multi -function devices that scan, print, and fax.  \n\uf0a7 Storage of account data in any format (for example, paper, data files, audio files, images, and video recordings).  \n\uf0a7 Applications, software, and software components, serverless applications, including all purchased, subscribed (for example, S oftware-\nas-a-Service), bespoke and custom software, including internal and external (for example, Internet) applications.  \n\uf0a7 Tools, code repositories, and systems that implement software configuration management or for deploymen')
```

li\nPart I: Foundation  пҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪ 1\nChapter 1:   AI Management System  пҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪ 3\nAI Management System  пҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪ 3\nComponent 1: Corporate Philosophical Framework  пҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪ 4\nComponent 2: Legal and Regulatory Environment  пҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪ 5\nComponent 3: AI Implementation Approach  пҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪ 5\nPhase A (AI Preparation)  пҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪ 6\nGoal and Objectives of Phase A  пҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪ 6\nSteps of Phase A (AI Preparation)  пҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪ 7\nPhase B: AI Management Framework  пҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪ 7\nGoal and Objectives of Phase B (AI Management Framework)  пҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪ 7\nPhase B-Part 1 (AI Governance) Objectives  пҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪ 7\nPhase B-Part 2 (Data Governance) Objectives  пҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪ 8\nPhase B-Part 3 (IT Governance) Objectives пҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪ 8\nSteps of Phase B (Part B-1: Establish AI Governance Controls) пҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪ пҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪ 17\nAnnex 3: AI Management System Products  пҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪ 19\nPart II: AI Ethics Framework  пҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪ 21\nChapter 2:   The Seven Ancient Greek Wisdom Principles  пҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪ 23\nPrinciple 1: Temperance  пҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪ 25\nPrinciple 2: Faith  пҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪ 26\nPrinciple 3: Justice  пҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪ 28\nPrinciple 4: Harmony  пҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪ 30\nPrinciple 5: Friendship 31\nPrinciple 6: Kalokagathia (Goodness and Kindness) 35\nOutcome, Results, and Outputs  пҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪ 37\nRecommendations 38\nChapter 3:   Corporate Philosophy Statements and Policies  пҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪ 41\nCPP01: AI Vision, AI Mission, and AI Values Statements  пҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪ 41\nCPP02: Conflict of Interest Policy  пҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪ 42\nCPP03: AI Ethical Standards Policy  пҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪ 44\nAnnex 1: Harmony Mnemonic 47Table of ConTenTs\nvCPP04: Corporate Ethics Policy  пҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪ пҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪ 51\nCPP07: Environment Protection Policy  пҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪ 52\nCPP08: Data Ethics Policy  пҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪ 53\nPart A: Principles of Data Ethics for AI пҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪ пҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪ пҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪ 129\nChapter 8:   Establish AI Governance Framework  пҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪ 131\nPhases of the AI Implementation Approach  пҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪ 131\nGoal and Objectives of Phase B (AI Management Framework, Part 1: AI Governance \nStructure)  пҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪ 132\nSteps of Phase B (AI Management Framework, Part 1: AI Governance Structure)  пҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪ 132\nStep B1-1: Ensure Corporate Governance Controls Operation  пҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪ 133\nStep B1-2: Establish AI Governance Roles and Responsibilities  пҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪ 134\nStep B1-3: пҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪ 134\nStep B1-4: Develop and Issue AI Strategy  пҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪ 137\nStep B1-5: Establish AI Third-Party Controls  пҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪ 138\nStep B1-6: Determine AI Ethical Principles and Values  пҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪ 139\nStep B1-7: Educate and Train Employees on AI пҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪ 140\nProducts of Phase B (AI Governance)  пҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪ 141\nPhase B (AI Governance) Recommended Assurance Actions  пҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪ 142\nConcluding Remarks  пҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪ 144\nAnnex 1: Insider Threats Action Plan  пҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪ 144\nAnnex 2: IT Sabotage Prevention Plan  пҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪ 146\nChapter 9:   Establish Data Governance Framework  пҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪ 149\nPhases of the AI Implementation Approach  пҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪ 149\nGoal and Objectives of Phase B (AI Management Framework, Part 2: Data Governance \nStructure)  пҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪ 150\nSteps of Phase B (AI Management Framework, Part 2: Data Governance Structure)  пҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪ 150\nStep B2-1: Establish Data Governance Function  пҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪ 151\nStep B2-2: Establish Data Governance Policies and Procedures 158\nAnnex 1: AI Data Protection Guidelines Develop AI System 202\nAssurance Stage: Steps D1вҖ“D4 203\nStep D1: Review the IT Operational Aspects of AI Systems AI Systems 205\nStep D3: Review the Auditability of AI Systems  пҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪ 206\nStep D4: Review Application Controls and Logging of AI Systems 207\nStep D5: Operate (Deploy) the AI System 207\nStep D6: Monitor Performance of AI Systems Drafting drawings 119\n4.1 Types of drawing 119\n4.2 Reference indicators  120\n4.3 Level of detail 120\n4.4 Drawings provided by the inventor 121\n5. Setting up an in-house patent review committee to  \nperiodically review invention disclosures and make \npatenting recommendations  154\n4. Professional ethics 155\nAnnexes  158\nAnnex A Examples of databases 158\nAnnex B Example of an invention disclosure form 159\nAnnex C WIPO resources and tools 162\nEndnotes  163\n 7Innovation is a worldwide phenomenon, occurring in all parts of the globe and constantly \nimproving our well-being and quality of life. Over the last decade, as shown in WIPOвҖҷs Global \nInnovation Index  (GII), innovation expenditures worldwide have been growing faster than GDP. \nIn high-income countries, private sector funding drove much of this growth in innovation \nexpenditure, while in middle- and low-income countries, the contribution of public funds in \nresearch and development expenditure, could be as high as 75 percent.\nIt is human nature to seek technical solutions whenever we encounter problems, whether in \nrelation to daily life needs or a quest to explore outer space. Therefore, the growing number on \nfilings of patent applications is an indicator of human inventiveness in very diverse technological \nfields. \nThe patent system offers inventors recognition for their creativity and the possibility of \nmaterial reward for their inventions. At the same time, the obligatory publication of patents and \npatent applications facilitates the spread of new technical knowledge and the acceleration of \ninnovation activities in society as a whole. \nIn reality, however, the potential benefits offered by the patent system have not been fully \nenjoyed by innovators in all parts of the world. While there may be many reasons for this, the \nopportunity to obtain the special skill set needed to draft a patent application that fully captures \nthe potential of a new invention is a challenge in many countries. \nInnovators need well-drafted patent applications if they want to secure the best possible \nprotection for their inventions and reduce the risk of rejection of applications. For third \nparties, well-drafted patents are just as important: they are not only a valuable source of new \nknowledge, they show the clear demarcation of the scope of the patent protection, helping to \navoid the inadvertent infringement of patent rights or to support a challenge to the validity of \na patent. For patent offices, receiving applications that are well drafted enables more efficient \nhandling of patent prosecution. \nThe first edition of the WIPO Patent Drafting Manual  was published in 2007. It was designed \nto help inventors and their advisors to acquire the technical skills needed to prepare and file \nwell-drafted patent applications. Since then, it has been valuable background reading material, \nsupporting the successful delivery of training activities organized by WIPO. In addition, the \nmanual has been widely used by other national and regional institutions in their patent drafting \ntraining activities. \nWIPO offers training programs with different content and of varying lengths, catering to the \nneeds of the targeted country and trainees. This extensive training experience has shown that \npatent drafting requires a combination of both theoretical knowledge and practical drafting \nskills. When participants have access to comprehensive background material that covers all \naspects of patent drafting theory, training workshops can focus on hands-on drafting exercises. \nIt is also clear that while the ability to draft proper claims is a core skill in the preparation of a \npatent application, the drafting of other parts of the application is equally important for the \nprotection and disclosure of inventions.Foreword\nWIPO Patent Drafting Manual: Second edition8 The general intellectual property capacities and technology bases of many developing \ncountries have evolved rapidly, so patent drafters need knowledge and skills suited to technical \ncomplexities and the demands of local innovators. There have also been important changes in \nnational patent laws and practices in some countries in recent years. \nThis second edition of the WIPO Patent Drafting Manual  has drawn from the lessons learned \nand has been adapted to todayвҖҷs users and their needs. In this new edition, the drafting of \nboth claims and descriptions are explained in detail. Additional explanations and examples of \nclaim formats that are typically used for chemical inventions (for example, Markush claims) or \ncomputer-implemented inventions are also included in the new edition. In order to improve the \nmanualвҖҷs readability, diagrams and charts that visualize the concept of patent claims have been \nadded. In addition, since patent drafters often find it necessary to amend their applications \nduring the patent examination phase, a new section dedicated to amendments of patent \napplications has been included. The order of the modules has also been rearranged so that the \nmanual follows the process of preparing, drafting, filing and prosecuting patent applications in \nchronological order. \nWe trust that the second edition of the WIPO Patent Drafting Manual  will be an effective tool \nfor those studying or teaching patent drafting. It is hoped that the new edition will continue to \nassist Member States that wish to enhance, refine and expand their capacity to assist inventors \nin protecting their intellectual property through carefully drafted patent applications.\nMarco M. AlemГЎn  \nAssistant Director General  \nWorld Intellectual Property Organization\n 9WIPO would like to express its great appreciation to Pascual Segura (Spain) and Kay Konishi \n(Japan), who thoroughly reviewed the first edition of the WIPO Patent Drafting Manual , identified \nand suggested areas for improvement, and made contributions to the text to update and \nsupplement the contents of the first edition. As an experienced patent attorney and a qualified \ntutor for teaching patent drafting, the expertise they provided to trainees from different \nbackgrounds was indispensable in the successful completion of the second edition. Valuable \ncontributions and suggestions were also made by Anton Blijlevens (New Zealand), Pablo \nPaz (Argentina), Karl\xa0Rackette (Germany), Robert Sayre (United States of America). Thanks \nare due to the various WIPO officials who peer reviewed the draft content, in particular to \nTomoko Miyamoto for the hard work and coordination which were key for this work to be \nfinished successfully.\nSince the second edition of the WIPO Patent Drafting Manual  is largely based on its first \nedition, the contribution of Thomas Ewing (United States of America), who was the principal \nauthor of the first edition, is fully acknowledged. Equally, WIPO acknowledges the valuable \ncontributions made by the following experts and institutions throughout the preparation of \nthe first edition: Markus Engelhard (Germany), Takashi Fujita (Japan), ValГ©rie Gallois (France), \nWendy Herby (United States of America), Albert Jacobs (United States of America), Karuna Jain \n(India), Emmanuel Jelsch (Switzerland), Samuel Le Cacheux (France), Carlos Olarte (Colombia), \nKarl Rackette (Germany), Sorin Schneiter (Switzerland), Kanika Radhakrishnan (India and United \nStates of America), Douglas\xa0Weinstein (United States of America) and the Geneva International \nAcademic Network.Acknowledgements\n10 Module I  \nIntellectual property: \nan introduction\n1. Intellectual property types\nIntellectual property (IP) is the name given to patents, trademarks, copyrights, industrial \ndesigns and other types of intangible property that arise from creations of the mind and which, \nin their broadest sense, have no physical form.\nFigure\xa01:  Various types of properties and their values and results\n \nLike all types of property, IP is often the result of investment, is owned and may generate \nincome. For this reason, IP is considered an asset. Intellectual property differs from tangible \nproperty because it has no physical form and comes into being because of human intelligence, \ncreativity and imagination.\nEach type of IP has its own unique laws. It is sometimes divided into two general categories: \nindustrial property and copyright.\nIndustrial property  refers to assets created primarily for the advancement of technology, \nindustry and trade, such as patents (inventions), industrial designs, trademarks, service marks \nand geographical indications.1\nPatents\nA patent is a legal document granting its holder the exclusive right to control the use of an \ninvention, as defined in the patentвҖҷs claims, within a limited geographical area and time by \nstopping others from, among other things, making, using or selling the invention without \nauthorization. For example, patents could be granted for a battery that efficiently stores solar \nenergy, a vaccine to protect against malaria or a new compound for transforming fish bones \ninto agricultural fertilizer.\nIndustrial designs\nIndustrial design protection allows its owner to control the exploitation of the ornamental \nshapes associated with products, such as the stylish shape of a new sports car, the distinctive \nplastic casing of a certain type of computer or the shape of a soft drink bottle.Property\n вҖ“Real\n вҖ“Personal\n вҖ“Capital\n вҖ“IntellectualValue creation activity\n вҖ“Product creation\n вҖ“Investment\n вҖ“Improvement\n вҖ“Sale\n вҖ“Rent or licensingResults\n вҖ“Revenues/profits\n вҖ“Employment\n вҖ“Appreciation\n вҖ“Solution of needs\nModule I Intellectual property: an introduction 11 Trademarks\nA trademark allows its owner to assure the public of the origin of the goods. Examples of \ntrademarks include the distinctive names of products, such as NandoвҖҷsВ® or Coca ColaВ®, or a \nlogo, such as the Mercedes BenzВ® triad symbol.\nService marks\nA service mark is a form of trademark that allows its owner to assure the public of the origin of a \nservice, such as вҖңCheques for TwoВ®.вҖқ\nCopyright  refers to original expressions and вҖңworks of authorship.вҖқ The person who creates \na copyrighted work is called an author . Examples of copyrighted works include paintings, \nphotographs, music, dance, poetry, literature, etc. Copyright also applies to original expressions \nassociated with technology, such as computer software, technical specifications and \nrelated documentation.\nOne difference between copyright and industrial property is that, generally, copyright does \nnot need to be registered with a government authority to be effective as protection against \nunauthorized use. Industrial property rights, however, must be expressly granted by and \nregistered with a government authority if they are to be recognized and enforced.\nIn theory, anyone can draft a patent application for this purpose. In practice, however, \nprofessional patent attorneys and technical professionals known as patent agents  or patent \nengineers  usually write patent applications and file them with the relevant government \nauthorities, because such applications can be technically and procedurally complex.\n 2. More patent basics\nPatents may be granted to protect inventions in all fields of technology that are new, involve an \ninventive step and are capable of industrial application.2 The patent has to be for an invention \nthat works or вҖ“ as some countries define it вҖ“ is capable of being вҖңreduced to practice.вҖқ In general, \na patent application must disclose the invention sufficiently clearly and completely that a person \nskilled in the art can carry out the invention. Thus a clever notion that cannot presently be put \ninto actual practice (e.g., a time machine) cannot be patented.\nDifferent countries may use different definitions of patentable вҖңinventions.вҖқ For example, \ninventions must generally be technical in nature, but not all jurisdictions share the same \ndefinition of what is вҖңtechnicalвҖқ and what is not.\nIn many countries, the term for which a patent is available is 20\xa0years from the date on which \nthe application was filed. In principle, a patent gives its owner the right to exclude others from \nmaking, using, offering for sale or selling the invention or importing the patented invention into \na country where the patent has been granted. In other words, a patent provides a property right \nthat allows the owner to say who cannot  use the invention protected by the patent. Although \nthere are certain exceptions and limitations to patent rights, in general, anyone who is neither \nthe patent owner nor licensed by the patent owner and who nonetheless manufactures, uses, \nimports, offers for sale or sells a product or performs a process covered by the patent is known \nas an infringer . An infringer can be sued in court to force them to stop the infringement and to \npay the owner damages.\nPatents are territorial  вҖ“ that is, they have effect only in countries where they have been applied \nfor and granted. Each country has the sovereign right to grant or refuse to grant a patent. \nIn some regions, a group of nations have agreed by treaty to provide for common filing and \nexamination of patent applications. For example, in Europe, the European Patent Convention \nestablished the European Patent Office (EPO), which examines European patent applications \nand grants European patents, while the African Intellectual Property Organization (OAPI) grants \nregional patents that are enforceable in all OAPI member states.\nModule\xa0VIII  provides more information about patent filing procedures.\nWIPO Patent Drafting Manual: Second edition12 Key words\n вҖ“Intellectual property\n вҖ“Industrial property\n вҖ“Patent\n вҖ“Industrial design\n вҖ“Trademark\n вҖ“Service mark\n вҖ“Copyright\n вҖ“Invention\n вҖ“Territorial\n вҖ“Infringement  \nSelf-test\n \x85What is intellectual property (IP)? \n \x85Why is IP an asset?\n \x85What is the difference between industrial property and copyright?\n \x85Give an example of each type of IP that you can see in the room where you are now.\n \x85Can a software code be protected by copyright?\n \x85A patent generally gives its owner the right to exclude others from making, using or selling \nthe invention defined in the patentвҖҷs claims. True or false?\n \x85Once a patent has been issued in one country, it is entitled to recognition all over the world. \nTrue or false?\n \x85Who are patent infringers?  \nThis manual explores how patents are applied for and registered. The objective of this manual \nis to help you to develop a general understanding of the skills needed to draft a patent \napplication, file it and work with patent authori  ties to have it issued. Since national/ regional \nlaws and practices may vary significantly, you must also seek out and understand the specific \nrequirements for the jurisdiction(s) to which you and your client(s) are subject. \nNote that, in this manual, the term patent drafter  is used not in the legal and tech  nical sense \nof a professional certified by national authorities to represent patent applicants in defined \ncircumstances but rather as a generic term to mean anyone who drafts a patent application \n(includ  ing an inventor a patent agent and a patent attorney).\n 13Module II  \nPatents\n 1. Detailed overview of patents\nA patent confers a limited exclusive right granted by a government for an invention. In the \npast, rulers awarded patents for almost any good or service whether or not an invention was \ninvolved. For example, a king might bestow a patent on salt to a trusted ally. In modern times, \ngovernments have reduced the scope of patents to protect inventions only. The Republic \nof Venice created one of the first patent systems of the modern era. The original term for a \npatent was set at 14 years вҖ“ twice the length of the average apprenticeship. The term was later \nextended to 17 years following the grant of the patent. At present, the term of patent protection \navailable in most countries is set at 20 years from the date of the applicationвҖҷs filing, in principle.\n\t\t1.1\t What\tis\ta\tpatent?\nA patent generally grants the patent owner the exclusive right to control who makes, uses, \nsells, offers for sale and/or imports any product or process defined by the patentвҖҷs claims. \nPatent claims  are sets of sentences that define the invention being protected. To obtain a \npatent, claims must typically be for an invention that is new (novel), involves inventive step (is \nnonobvious) in view of the вҖңprior artвҖқ and is industrially applicable (useful). Prior art  is a technical \nterm that generally refers to all the knowledge available to the public at the time of filing of the \npatent application.\nThere are numerous other requirements that must also be met to obtain a patent. For example, \nthe subject matter of the invention must be patentable under the applicable law, and the patent \napplication must disclose the invention sufficiently clearly and completely. We will explain these \nlegal requirements for obtaining a patent in detail in section 2  of this module.\nProfessional tip\nYou must file a patent application before publicly disclosing any important research \nresults that may lead to a valuable product or technology. This caution applies especially \nto research institutions, where earliest publication of academic works is considered of \nutmost importance.\nTo ensure that publication in a review journal or presentation at a conference will not \ndestroy the novelty of a patent application in countries where patent protection is sought, \nacademic research institutions may set up an internal publication clearance procedure \nthat reviews researchersвҖҷ journal and conference submissions.\nIn many countries, patents are granted under substantive examination systems  in which a patent \napplication is thoroughly reviewed by a government-employed patent examiner. Among other \nthings, the patent examiner will compare the related prior art against the applicationвҖҷs claims to \ndetermine whether the claimed invention provides a legally sufficient advancement of that art.\nSome countries have registration systems  in which an applicant receives a patent once certain \nformalities have been completed but without any such substantive examination. In such \nWIPO Patent Drafting Manual: Second edition14 systems, the validity of the patent in view of prior art is not assessed until or unless the patent is \nlater challenged in court.\nA patent is said to be valid  once it has been granted, provided that it has not been successfully \nchallenged in a court or before the relevant patent office. A patent is said to be invalid  when \nit is rejected or cancelled because the invention is not new in view of the prior art or for other \nreasons. Some of the worldвҖҷs patent systems hold that patents are deemed to be valid unless \nproven otherwise. This is especially true in the substantive examination systems, where an \nimpartial government official has examined a patent application and relevant prior art before \ngranting a patent.\nThe worldвҖҷs patent laws typically recognize patent protection for different types of invention. \nMany patent systems aim to provide uniform treatment for all inventions, regardless of the type \nof invention. When people speak of patents, they usually mean вҖңpatents of invention,вҖқ which \nare known as utility patents  in the United States of America. These patents protect machines, \nprocesses, chemical compositions and the other kinds of invention that are valuable because of \ntheir usefulness.\nSome countries offer protection for inventions by means of utility model registrations , \nwhich are also known as innovation patents, utility innovations or short-term patents. The \nrequirements for the registration of utility models are typically less stringent than those for \nobtaining a patent of invention.\nSince the knowledge and skills required for drafting utility model applications are similar to those for \ndrafting patent applications, this manual is also relevant to utility model registrations.\nThis manual relates most directly to patents of invention or utility patents.3\nIn practice, protection for utility models is typically sought for innovations of an incremental \nnature that might not satisfy the criteria for patents of invention (e.g., they may not \ndemonstrate the necessary inventive step). The term of protection for utility models is \nsignificantly shorter than that for patents. Some patent offices do not conduct substantive \nexamination of utility model applications prior to their registration; consequently, the precise \nnature of the right granted will be in question until a dispute arises between the owner and \nanother party. In some countries, utility model protection can be obtained only for certain fields \nof technology and only for products not processes.\n\t1.2\t What\tcan\tbe\tthe\tsubject\tof\ta\tpatent?\nLet us look at U.S. Patent No.\xa06,434,955\xa0B1, issued on August\xa020, 2002, bearing the title вҖңElectro-\nAdsorption Chiller: A Miniaturized Cooling Cycle with Applications from Microelectronics to \nConventional Air-Conditioning.вҖқ\nThe abstract for this patent reads:\nA novel modular and miniature chiller is proposed that symbiotically combines \nabsorption and thermoelectric cooling devices. The seemingly low efficiency of each \ncycle individually is overcome by an amalgamation with the other. This electro-adsorption \nchiller incorporates solely existing technologies. It can attain large cooling densities \nat high efficiency, yet is free of moving parts and comprises harmless materials. The \ngoverning physical processes are primarily surface rather than bulk effects, or involve \nelectron rather than fluid flow. This insensitivity to scale creates promising applications \nin areas ranging from cooling personal computers and other micro-electronic appliances, \nto automotive and room air-conditioning.\nWhile the patent examiner assigned to review this application ultimately found it patentable, \nthey nevertheless reviewed nearly 15\xa0pieces of prior art and used 2\xa0pieces of this prior art in \nrejecting the claims of the application as originally filed. The issued patent has 19\xa0claims in two \nsets, with one set of 11\xa0device claims followed by a second set of 7\xa0method claims (')
```

As\nAI becomes  more deeply integrated into YouTube\'s\nframework, questions arise about bias, privacy, and harmful\ncontent, emphasizing the need for responsible innovation,\nethical guida nce, and user empower ment in navigating this\nintricate ter rain.\nThroughout this book, we will explor e into both the\nopportunities and challenges presented by AI on YouTube.\nThrough case studies, futur e projections, and ethical\ndiscussions, we aim to provide a compr ehensive\nunderstanding of AI\'s inп¬Ӯuence on digital media and its\nramiп¬Ғcations for cr eators, users, and society as a whole.\nAs we navigate this landscape, let us remain mindful of our\nethical obligations and the profound impact of technology\non our lives. Let this exploration spark meaningful\nconversations, foster informed decision-making, and inspir e\ncollective eп¬Җorts to shape a digital futur e that is fair,\ninclusive, and empowering for everyone.\nThank you for joining me on this journey of discovery .\nTogether, let us uncover the transfor mative potential of AI\non YouTube and work towar ds a more vibrant, diverse, and\nresponsible digital media envir onment.\nWarm regards,\nChidiebere Iroegbu\nINTRODUCTION\nWelcome to "The Rise of AI in YouTube,"  a detailed\nexploration into the dynamic relationship between Artiп¬Ғcial\nIntelligence (AI) and one of the world\'s largest online video\nplatfor ms, YouTube. In this book, we embark on a journey to\nunveil the signiп¬Ғcant changes, ethical considerations, and\nfutur e prospects brought about by the integration of AI into\nYouTube\'s ecosystem.\nYouTube stands as a cultural power house, fundamentally\naltering how we consume, produce, and exchan ge video\ncontent in the digital era. With billions of users and a vast\narray of content covering various genres and subjects,\nYouTube has cemented its position as a cornerstone of\ncontemporary media  consumption, inп¬Ӯuencing\nentertainment, lear ning, and cultural dialogues worldwide.\nAt the core of YouTube\'s success lies its embrace of AI\ntechnologies, which drive numer ous featur es and\nfunctionalities that enhance user experiences, optimize\ncontent delivery, and propel platfor m expansion. From\npersonalized content suggestions and AI-assisted content\ncreation to automated content moderation and audience\ninteraction, AI has seamlessly woven itself into the fabric of\nthe YouTube experience, reshaping how users engage with\nand e xplor e video content online.\nHowever, the integration of AI into YouTube presents\nchallenges and ethical dilemmas. Concer ns such as\nalgorithmic bias, privacy breaches, and the proliferation of\nharmful content under score the comple xities of AI\nimplementation on the platfor m. Further more, ethical\nconsiderations regarding AI-Generated Content,\nrecommendation algorith ms, and content moderation raise\nissues of accountability, transpar ency, and user conп¬Ғdence.\nIn this book, we explor e deep into the intric acies of\nnavigating the intersection of AI and YouTube, investigating\nthe opportu nities, risks, and obligations linked with AI\nintegration on the platfor m. Through thorough analyses,\ncase studies, and futur e projections, we examine how AI is\nreshaping content creation, consumption trends, and\nplatfor m dynamics on Y ouTube.\nFrom successful implementations to setbacks in AI adoption,\nethical dilemmas, regulatory hurdles, and forthcoming\ntrends, our aim is to oп¬Җer a compr ehensive understanding of\nthe transfor mative inп¬Ӯuence of AI on YouTube and its\nramiп¬Ғcations for cr eators, users, and society as a whole.\nUltimately, this book acts as a guide for navig ating the\nelaborate conver gence of AI and YouTube, providing\ninsights, viewpoints, and recommendations for promoting\nresponsible innovation, ethical leadership, and inclusive\ndigital envir onments in the evolving realm of online video .\nJoin us as we embark on this exploration of вҖңThe Rise of AI\nin YouTubeвҖқ and unravel the captivating dynamics shaping\nthe futur e of digital media.\nCHAPTER ONE\nTHE GROWTH OF Y OUTUBE AND AI FUSION\nYouTube, the giant of online video sharing, has experienced\na remark able journey since its establishment in 2005. What\ninitially started as a modest platfor m for amateur content\nhas now become a global sensation, signiп¬Ғcantly altering\nthe way content is consu med and created. At the heart of\nthis evolution lies the fusion of Artiп¬Ғcial Intelligence (AI),\nwhich has revolutionized every facet of the YouTube\nexperience.\n1.1 The Origin Story of Y ouTube\nTo understanding the importance of AI fusion, it\'s crucial to\nexplor e into YouTube\'s beginnings. Founded by Steve Chen,\nChad Hurley, and Jawed Karim, YouTube emer ged as a\nplatfor m for sharing short, amateur videos. Its user-friendly\ninterface and straightforwar dness quickly attracted  millions\nof users, propelling it into a cultural phenomenon. However,\nas the platfor m expanded, so did the comple xities of\nmanaging its extensive content library and catering to\ndiverse user pr eferences.\n1.2 The Ascendancy of AI\nAs YouTube\'s  popularity soared, there arose a need for more\nsophisticated tools to manage content and enha nce user\nexperience. This demand coincided with the ascent of AI\ntechnologies, particularly  in Machine Learning and Data\nAnalytics. YouTube seized upon these advancements,\nrecognizing AI\'s potential to r evolutionize its platfor m.\n1.3 AI\'s Role in Content Oversight\nOne of the initial applic ations of AI on YouTube was in\ncontent moderation. With millions of videos uploaded daily,\nmanually reviewing each piece of content for guideline\nviolations was impractical. AI algorithms enabled YouTube to\nautomate this process, п¬Ӯagging potentially problematic\ncontent for human review. This not only sped up the\nmoderation process but also enhanced its accuracy,\ncreating a safer and mor e hospitable envir onment for users.\n1.4 AI and V ideo Suggestions\nArguably the most visibl e impact of AI on YouTube is its\nrecommendation system.  Utilizing extensive data on user\npreferences and viewing patter ns, AI algorithms craft\npersonalized suggestions for each user. These suggestions\naren\'t random but are carefully tailor ed to optimize user\nengagement metrics such as watch time and Click-Through\nRates. Through continual learning and adaptation, AI\nensur es that suggestion s remain pertinent and enticing,\nfostering a deeper connection between users and content.\n1.5 The Rise of AI-Generated Content\nBeyond suggestions, AI is increasingly contributing to\ncontent creation. From automated video editing tools to AI-\ngenerated music compositions, creators are leveraging AI to\nstreamline their workп¬Ӯow s and push creative boundaries.\nFurther more, AI-Generated Content extends beyond\ntraditional human creators, with the emer gence of virtual\ninп¬Ӯuencers and AI-Driven avatars captivating audiences\nworldwide. This blending of human and AI-Generated\nContent marks a new era of creativity and innovation on\nYouTube.\n1.6 Hurdles and Ethical Considerations\nNevertheless, the integration of AI on YouTube isn\'t without\nchallenges and ethical considerations. The opaque natur e of\nAI algorithms raises concer ns regarding accountability and\ntranspar ency, particularly concer ning content moderation\nand recomm endation systems. Moreover, the potential for\nalgorithmic biases to propagate misinfor mation and\nreinfor ce echo chambers underscor es the necessity for\nincreased scrutiny and oversight.\n1.7 F uture Outlook\nDespite these challenges, the futur e of AI integration on\nYouTube appears promising. As AI technologies advance, we\nanticipate further innovations in content moderation,\nrecommendation systems, and content creation tools.\nMoreover, the democrati zation of AI through open-sour ce\nplatfor ms and collaborative initiatives has the potential to\nempower creators from all backgr ounds to harness AI\'s\ntransfor mative capabilities.\nIn conclusion , the fusion of AI has fundamentally altered the\nYouTube landscape, evolving it from a simple video -sharing\nplatfor m into a vibran t ecosystem of creativity and\ninnovation. As we navigate this intersection of AI and\nYouTube, it\'s crucial to remain mindful of the opportunities\nand obstacles that lie ahead.\nCHAPTER TWO\nINSIGHTS INTO Y OUTUBE\'S RECOMMENDA TION SYSTEM\nYouTube\'s recommendation system stands as a\nsophisticated mechanism power ed by AI, signiп¬Ғcantly\ninп¬Ӯuencing the browsing experience of countless users each\nday. At its core lies a comple x set of algorithms crafted to\nanalyze user behavior, understand preferences, and oп¬Җer\ntailor ed content suggest ions. Unraveling the workings of\nthese AI algorithms is key to understanding the dynamics of\nYouTube\'s r ecommendation landscape.\n2.1 Decoding Y ouTube\'s Recommendation Algorithm\nYouTube\'s recommendati on algorithm relies on machine\nlearning techniques, continuously learning from user\ninteractions. Its foundation comprises neural networks\ntrained on extensive datasets including user engagement\nmetrics, video details, and viewer feedback. These networks\nprocess inputs like watch history, likes, dislik es, and\ncomments to predict the videos most likely to interest a\nspeciп¬Ғc user .\n2.2 Balancing Personalization and Discovery\nA primary goal of YouTube\'s recommendation algorithm is to\nbalance personalization with serendipity . Personalization\ntailors content to individual interests, enhancing\nengagement and satisfaction. Yet, excessive perso nalization\nrisks creating echo chambers, limiting exposur e to new\nideas. Serendipity injects variety into recomme ndations,\nintroducing users to diverse content. Striking this balance\nposes an ongoing challenge for YouTube\'s algorithm\nengineers.\n2.3 Importance of Engagement Metrics\nYouTube\'s recommendation algorithm heavily relies on\nengagement metrics to gauge video relevance and appeal.\nMetrics like watch time, Click- Through Rate, likes, and\ncomments oп¬Җer insights into user preferences and\nsatisfaction levels. Videos  with strong engagemen t signals\nare more likely to reach a wider audience, contri buting to\ntheir popular ity. Howeve r, over-reliance on these  metrics\ncan promote  clickbait content, prompting YouTube to reп¬Ғne\nits algorithm continually to prioritize quality content.\n2.4 Considering External F actors\nWhile user interactions primarily drive YouTube\'s\nrecommendation algorith m, external factors such as trends\nand cultural events also play a role. Videos related to\nbreaking news or viral topics may receive temporary boosts\nin recomme ndations. Content creators can strategically\ncapitalize on external factors to enhance their videos\'\nvisibility, adding comple xity to the recomm endation\nprocess.\n2.5 T ackling Bias and F airness\nA signiп¬Ғcan t concer n with YouTube\'s recomm endation\nalgorithm is the risk of bias and discrimination. Resear ch\nindicates AI Algorithms can exhibit biases based on factors\nlike race and gender, leading to disparities in recommended\ncontent. YouTube implements measur es to address\nalgorithmic bias, including audits, diversity training, and\nuser feedback mechanisms. However, achieving  fairness\nremains an ongoing challenge r equiring continuous eп¬Җorts.\n2.6 T ransparency and Accountability\nTranspar ency and accountability are crucial for user trust in\nYouTube\'s recommendati on algorithm. While the platfor m\nprovides general guidelines, the inner working s of the\nsystem remain largely opaque. Enhancing transpar ency\nthrough detailed explanations and giving users more contr ol\nover r ecommendations can foster trust and understanding.\n2.7 F uture Innovations\nAs AI advances, YouTube\'s recommendation algorithm holds\npotential for further innovation. Natural Language\nProcessing can enhance content analysis accuracy, while\nreal-time user feedback can reп¬Ғne recomme ndations.\nPrivacy -preserving AI techniques can ensur e personalized\nrecommendations without compr omising user privacy,\npaving the way for a more ethical recomm endation\necosystem.\nIn summary, YouTube\'s recommendation algorithm is a\nfusion of AI, Data Science, and User Experience, shaping\nonline video  consumption. Understanding this algorithm\nprovides insights into content discovery on one of the\nlargest digital platfor ms. However, navigating AI-Driven\nrecommendations requir es vigilance regarding ethical and\nsocietal implications.\nCHAPTER THREE\nVIRTUAL PERSONALITIES: THE RISE OF DIGIT AL INFL UENCERS\nWithin the dynamic realm  of Social Media, a new breed of\ninп¬Ӯuencers has emer ged, transcending the limitations of\nphysical existence вҖ“ virtua l inп¬Ӯuencers, power ed by Artiп¬Ғcial\nIntelligence. These digital avatars wield substantial\ninп¬Ӯuence over online audiences, challenging the distinction\nbetween human and Machine- Generated Content.\n3.1 The Origins of V irtual Inп¬Ӯuencers\nVirtual inп¬Ӯuencers п¬Ғnd their roots in early experiments with\nComputer -Generated Imagery (CGI) and virtual characters\nwithin the entertainment industry . However, it wasn\'t until\nthe rise of platfor ms like Instagram and YouTube that they\ngained traction. Pioneering п¬Ғgures like Lil Miquela captur ed\naudiences with their lifelike appearance and curated\ncontent. Since then, a plethora of virtual inп¬Ӯuencers has\nemer ged, each with its unique personality and niche\nfollowing.\n3.2 The Allure of V irtual Inп¬Ӯuencers\nWhat distinguishes virtual inп¬Ӯuencers is their ability to\nembody aspirational ideals unconstrained by physical\nlimitations. Unlike human  inп¬Ӯuencers, they remain  immune\nto aging, fatigue, or personal contr oversies, providing a\nconsistent and idealized image to their followers. This\nidealized representation, coupled with their enigm atic aura,\nappeals to audiences seeking escapism and novelty in a\ndigitized world.\n3.3 The T echnology behind V irtual Inп¬Ӯuencers\nSophisticated AI technologies form the backbone of virtual\ninп¬Ӯuencers, including computer vision, Natural Language\nProcessing, and Generative Adversarial Networks  (GANs).\nThese algorit hms enable virtual inп¬Ӯuencers to engage with\nusers, create compelling content, and even simulate human\nemotions. Advancements in deep learning have facilitated\nthe development of hyper -realistic avatars, blurring the lines\nbetween virtual and r eal.\n3.4 Addressing Ethical and Authenticity Concerns\nThe ascent of virtual inп¬Ӯuencers has spark ed ethical and\nauthenticity debates in inп¬Ӯuencer mark eting. Critics argue\nthat they perpetuate unrealistic beauty standar ds and\ncommodif y authenticity . Further more, the undisclosed\nnatur e of their AI-Drive n personas raises transparency\nissues. However, advocates see them as a fresh approach,\noп¬Җering brands creativ e contr ol and reduci ng risks\nassociated with human inп¬Ӯuencers.\n3.5 P artnerships and Collaborations\nDespite ethical debates, virtual inп¬Ӯuencers are sought-af ter\ncollaborators for brands seeking novelty and digital appeal.\nThey seamlessly integrate into branded content\npartnerships, providing brands with creative freedom and\nengagement potential. Moreover, they transcend\ngeographical barriers, appealing to global brands aiming for\ndiverse audiences.\n3.6 The F uture of V irtual Inп¬Ӯuencers\nAs AI advances, virtual inп¬Ӯuencers are poised for further\ndevelopment, with potential for even more realistic avatars\nand immersive storytelling. Democratization of AI tools\nempowers creators to develop their virtual inп¬Ӯuencers,\nchallenging established players. However, ethical, legal, and\nsocietal considerations are crucial in navigating their role in\nthe digital landscape.\n3.7 Impact on T raditional Inп¬Ӯuencers\nThe rise of virtual inп¬Ӯuencers disrupts the inп¬Ӯuencer\nmark eting ecosystem, presenting opportunities and\nchallenges for human inп¬Ӯuencers. While virtual inп¬Ӯuencers\noп¬Җer controlled alternatives, human inп¬Ӯuencers emphasize\nauthenticity and relatabili ty. Traditional inп¬Ӯuencers adapt by\ndiversif ying content, prioritizing authenticity, and leveraging\ntheir unique e xperiences.\nIn summary , virtual inп¬Ӯuencers redeп¬Ғne inп¬Ӯuencer\nmark eting, blurring reality and virtuality . As AI evolves, they\nwill shape consumer trends, brand perceptions, and digital\ncultur e. However, responsible consideration of ethical, legal,\nand societal implications is essential for a sustainable digital\nfutur e.\nCHAPTER FOUR\nAI\'S INFL UENCE ON CONTENT CREA TION AND CONSUMPTION D YNAMIC S\nArtiп¬Ғcial Intelligence (AI) has not only transfor med the way\ncontent is consumed but also how it\'s produced. This\nsection e xplor es AI\'s gr oundbr eaking eп¬Җects on both content\ncreation and consumptio n behaviors, delving into how AI-\nDriven tools and algorithms are reshaping the digital media\nscene.\n4.1 Empowering Content Creation with AI T ools\nAI has demo cratized content creation by providing creators\nwith robust tools to streamline their processes and unleash\ntheir creativity . From AI-Generated text and image synthesis\nto automated video editing and voice modulation, AI tools\noп¬Җer eп¬ғciency and scalability like never befor e. These\nresour ces empower creators to craft high-quality  content\nswiftly and aп¬Җordably, leveling the п¬Ғeld and fostering\ndiversity and innovation in content cr eation.\n4.2 Personalized Content Recommendations\nOn the consumption end, AI Algorithms have revolutionized\nhow users explor e and engage with content online.\nPlatfor ms like YouTube, Netп¬Ӯix, and Spotif y leverage\nsophisticated Data Analysis and Machine Learning to deliv')

best \npr ac tices. educe ambiguity  and impr o v e agen t decision-making,  r esulting in smoo ther  \nw orkflo w  e x ecution and f e w er  err or s.Best pr actices f or  agen t instructionsU se e xisting documen
