sfy | \nHand aviiqineudu? hag qideedarh \n\nThe Single Biggest Secret to \nMembership and Subscription Growth \nFor Associations, SAAS, Publishers, Digital Access, \nSubscription Boxes and all Membership and \nSubscription-Based Businesses \nROBERT SKROB \n\nRetention Point \nThe Single Biggest Secret to Membership and Subscription Growth \nв„ў and В© 2018 by Membership Services, Inc. \nAll rights reserved. \nNo part of this work may be reproduced in any form, or by any means \nwhatsoever, without written permission from Membership Services, Inc., \nexcept in the case of brief quotations embodied in critical articles and \nreviews. \nPublished by Membership Services, Inc. \nRetention Pointв„ў, Member Leaderв„ў and Member On Rampв„ў are \ntrademarks of Membership Services, Inc. \nISBN-13: 978-0-692-09455-6 \nLCCN: 2018903622 \nThis publication is designed to provide accurate and authoritative \ninformation in regard to the subject matter covered. It is sold with the \nunderstanding that the publisher is not engaged in rendering legal, \naccounting or other professional services. If legal advice or other expert \nassistance is required, the services of a competent professional should be \nsought. \n\nTable of Contents \nMembership Growth Comes From Retention, \nNot From Recruiting New Members \nThe Slight Change That Stops Your Members \nFrom Quitting and Grows Your Recurring Subscription \nMembership Revenue \nFinally Revealed, How to Turn Membership Quitters \nInto Lifers Who Grow Your Revenue by Becoming \nMembers of Your Vibrant Tribe \nGetting Your New Members to the Retention Point Is \nthe Secret for Fastest Membership Growth \nFive Mistakes Membership Marketers \nMake That Prevent Them From \nAchieving Membership Growth \nHow Member Leaders Design a Member On Ramp \nto Accelerate the Retention Point \nHow to Become a Member Leader to Improve \nYour Retention by 85% (or More!) .......cccccseceeeteeeeees \nHow the Largest Direct Response Marketing Company \nCommunicates With Its New MembefrsS..............00ccccee \nHow Charity: Water Transforms Donors Into \nMein OiSnerg tie 8 teers meetin ieee Go RR i \nFree Trial Membership Secrets - Generate More Free \nTrials and Convert More Free Trials Into Long-Term \nPOAC MDENS TID On enceesicn sine Miciaioles a Snelecateairacatae Sil \n\n12 \n13 \n17 Three SAAS Customer Success Mistakes When \nOn-Boarding New Members and How You Can \nAvoid a Similar High Churn Rate Fate wee \nHow FabFitFun Delivers the Value That Gets Members \nEngaged and Retains Them for Years ..............ccceeeee \nThe Easy Steps to Get Your Members to the \nRetentighthOMlers cae eee te eee ee \nYour Selling Begins After Your New Member \nJoins Your MembersMip PROGh AMY x eccacee eae \nA Confused Mind Causes a Canceled Member, \nClarity Gets Your Member to the Retention Point........... \nPut Your Fastest Win First to Get Your Members to \nthe Retention Point and Transform Them Into Lifers...... \nHow to Use Your Member On Ramp to Increase \nNew: MemberSales:.....fa i. SGU: ets) 5 hs See. \nWhat's Next: Build Your Own Member On Ramp to \nGet More New Members to the Retention Point.............. \nHow Many Members Must You Lose Before \nYou Fix Your Member Experience? ..............ccccccssseees. hal \n\nCHAPTER 1 \nMembership Growth Comes From Retention, \nNot From Recruiting New Members \nItвҖҷs 2:37 a.m., and IвҖҷm at an altitude of 38,000 feet, \nsomewhere over the Arabian Sea. I boarded this flight in \nMumbai about 30 minutes ago. ItвҖҷll be 27 hours before \nPm home in Florida, if all goes well. \nPm returning from a client engagement with a \npublisher in India. Focused on helping the team get and \nkeep more new members. I'll tell you about it in just a bit. \nWhat I will reveal to you within the pages you are \nholding has completely transformed my life. And if you \ninvest an hour of your time to read this book, I expect it \nwill transform your life by teaching you how to build a \nprogram that stops your members from quitting, grows \nto exceed your wildest dreams and generates reliable, \nsustainable recurring subscription revenue. LetвҖҷs end the \nfrustration of losing members as fast as you gain them. ItвҖҷs \ntime to break through those member number plateaus and \nscale-up your membership revenue. \nTwenty years ago, my wife and I lived in a little \nyellow house across the street from a drug dealer. Two \ndrug dealers actually. One had a dog, until the cops shot \nthe animal because it attacked them when they came to \narrest its owner. But thatвҖҷs a story for another day. \n\nRobert Skrob \nMy wife and I lived in that little yellow house with \nour 18-month-old daughter. Sadly, it was a step up from \nwhere we had lived before. \nWe didnвҖҷt have a lot of options. We owed more \nmoney in credit card debt than we earned in a year \nof work. Our minimum monthly payments across the \ndifferent cards were more than we made in a month. And \nwhen my wife had our daughter, she had to quit her job \nbecause after the cost of daycare, sheвҖҷd earn less than \n$4.00 an hour working full time as head cashier at The \nHome Depot. \nToday we live in a beautiful home. We have a \ngorgeous back yard with a pool and a fountain where | \ncan listen to water flowing over rocks as I relax in a chaise \nlounge reading a book and smoking a Partagas #10 cigar. \nA treat I donвҖҷt enjoy frequently enough. \nMy clients travel to see me from across America \nand around the world. And in some rare cases, IвҖҷve been \nbribed to travel to their offices. Today IвҖҷm returning from \nMumbai. ItвҖҷs a long trip, but not so bad in business class. \nItвҖҷs a lot like sitting in my reading chair at homeвҖ”if my \nhome was a metal tube seven miles up in the sky. \nNonetheless, Pll have a rare 27-hour period when \nI won't be disturbed. My meals will be brought to me. \nThereвҖҷs a lavatory just 10 steps away. Drinks are available \nfor the asking. \nWithin an hour of reading, IвҖҷm going to reveal to you \nthe membership retention strategies that got my daughter, \nmy wife and me out of that little yellow house and into the \nhome we enjoy today. \n\nRetention Point \nMy goal over the next 27 hours is to create a draft \nbook manuscript that lays it all out for you and makes it \neasy for you to follow. Most of all, Pll keep it brief! My \nego wants me to write another 75,000-word book, ask \nmy agent to sign me up with another publishing deal with \na five-figure advance and market my way to another best \nseller. \nInstead, IвҖҷm going to write a book YOU want. For \nyou, IвҖҷm investing the next 27 hours into creating a tool \nyou can consume within an hour that will enable you to \nexperience a transformation similar to the one I help create \nfor my clients. And when I get back to the comfort of my \nhome, Pll edit, cut and remove all the blabbering excess \nto distill this book down to the ideas you can implement \nright now. Grab yourself a cold drink and within an hour \nof reading, youвҖҷll know exactly what it takes to stop your \nmembers from quitting and to grow your membership by \nbecoming a Member Leader. \n\n , _ \nir | (eae \n: } ee 6 ee . \nSoe eee ee eee, \nwitвҖҳ aehGnuz to Cobh aust osu r ee a es 43 < yee, вҖҳreese \nTia Cee areas a ae tae \nAsn gs bi ade Eee a ale ce os wen - \n7вҖқ ol apr Oe S a Bai) bee Sh 7 \n_ OCs 2a! ergetew hel \na) ie Le en sy oS ae \na> = ; : \n= oe > . 4 7 2S) \ne;) aa - В« В° yy \n~~ - 2 as В© \n<< (eva as er ot \n~ eas Bes Hi qe \n,eF=aep : \n\nCHAPTER 2 \nThe Slight Change That Stops Your Members \nFrom Quitting and Grows Your Recurring \nsubscription Membership Revenue \nI had a decision to make. Although I had gone to school \nto become a CPA, I took a job as a bookkeeper at a \nconsulting company to membership associations. Just five \nyears later, in 1999, married, with our daughter, Samantha, \n4 years old and our son, Robert William, a newborn, I had \nto make a big decision. It would determine the course of \nmy life. \nDo I go back to a career as a CPA, or do I double \ndown on this membership stuff? My role in the company \nhad grown considerably over the last few years. And now, \nI was forced to make a decision. Buy the company or move \non to another opportunity, possibly back to pursuing the \nCPA career I'd previously left behind. \nMembership is a great business model in concept. You \nget a customer and your customer renews each year. While \nworking for that association consulting firm, I discovered \ndirect response copywriting. I wrote my first membership \nsales letter in 1996, sitting at my coffee table with a copy of \nDan KennedyвҖҷs book How to Write a Sales Letter next to \nme on the couch. I would read a chapter and then do what \nit said, writing a section of the sales letter on my laptop, \nwhich back then was the size of a briefcase and 10 times \nheavier. \n\nRobert Skrob \nIt worked; I got new members. And I began writing \nsales letters for more than 37 different membership \norganizations over the next few years. I bought the \ncompany, and it began to grow, for a time. \nThe internet revolution was getting started, \nchallenging the status quo. Web-based companies began \ndelivering what membership associations had been \ndelivering for the previous five decades. And they were \ndoing it better. Although IвҖҷd become great at getting new \nmembers, they were quitting just as fast. \nEverything turned around for me when I stopped \nchasing new members to focus on creating member \nrelationships instead. \nI moved the finish line. For me the goal stopped \nbeing a new member sign-up. The new goal was getting a \n12-month member. \nIn years past I would focus a lot of time and energy on \ngetting a new member. Then when someone said вҖңyes,вҖқ I'd \ncelebrate. Then move on to getting the next new member. \nCan you imagine if you did this in dating? YouвҖҷd be \ngoing on a first date every night. Happy you got there, but \nthen instantly moving on to the next first date. \nInstead I focused on the first year of membership. At \nthat time I was selling annual memberships. Thus, members \nhad to pay annual dues to become a member. I moved my \nfinish line out a year, to when that new member renewed \nhis dues, called вҖңconversion.вҖқ How many first-year \nmembers convert? became the key question. \nThis meant my work wasnвҖҷt complete until a member \nrenewed one year later. And what we found is that if a \n6 \n\nRetention Point \nmember retains membership and engagement for a year, \nthereвҖҷs a 90% chance heвҖҷll be your member for life. \nIn the years since, вҖҷve expanded beyond membership \nassociations into for-profit membership programs, \nlaunching my own membership in 2006 and selling it in \n2012. Pve helped to grow more than 1,000 membership \nprograms in the last two decades. ve worked with \nprograms that use various offers: free trials to get new \nmembers; monthly continuity from $9.97 to more than \n$4,500.00; and annual membership dues from $20.00 to \n$20,000.00. \nThe key to retention for all of these programs is what \nhappens AFTER your new member joins. Handling your \nnew member correctly can increase your new member \nlifetime value by three to ten times. This completely \nchanges the economics and stability in your business. \nWith a higher lifetime value, you can invest more to \nget new members. This increased marketing spend gives \nyou far greater reach than your competitors. You are able \nto grow faster and reach people youвҖҷd never be able to \nreach otherwise. Plus, with more members, you get more \nreferrals, the other services you offer get higher purchases \nand you have the opportunity to attract sponsors and other \noutside money to fuel your business. \nThis change allows me to triple, quadruple and even \nquintuple a membership program within a year. Few \nbelieve it before it happens. After years of running your \nown membership, you may find it impossible to imagine \nsomeone like me, someone who knows nothing about \nyour membership, being able to walk in and triple your \nmembership over the next 12 months. \n\nRobert Skrob \nApproach this book with an open mind. I am writing \nthis to share everything you need to transform your life the \nway these secrets have transformed mine. And IвҖҷve distilled \nit to a one-hour read. \nMy kids are in college now. My daughter is pursuing \nher doctoral degree in science education at Florida State \nUniversity. My son is studying business in the honors \nprogram at the University of Florida. My wife and I are \nnow happy empty nesters, living in our beautiful home \nwith a resort-like pool just steps from the living room, \nin a safe neighborhood. We have long since paid off our \nvehicles and credit cards. All because of what I will reveal \nto you in this book. \nThat is the real power of this information. To \ntransform your life from frustration and fear of high churn \nrates into peace of mind and security because you have a \nvibrant tribe of members who would miss you and come \nlooking for you if your communications disappeared for \neven one month. \nMost Membership Marketers churn through \nnew members, hoping some stick, because they donвҖҷt \nunderstand the difference between customers and members. \nMember Leaders get members to the Retention Point by \nbuilding relationships with members, one by one, at scale, \nthrough their automated Member On Ramp system. \nA small change of perspective will open you up to a \nhuge amount of growth. If you'll give me an hour to learn \nhow to transform your membership program from a series \nof вҖңone night standsвҖқ into a tribe of lifetime relationships, \nPll give you the next 26 and a half hours to distill what Pve \nlearned over the last two-plus decades. \n\nCHAPTER 3 \nFinally Revealed, How to Turn Membership \nQuitters Into Lifers Who Grow Your Revenue by \nBecoming Members of Your Vibrant Tribe \nYou know those members of yours who love what you \ndeliver? They canвҖҷt get enough, they tell everyone they \nknow about you and they buy everything you offer. \nThese are the вҖңLifers.вҖқ Then there are the вҖңQuitters.вҖқ \nThe Quitters stop opening your email, donвҖҷt use what \nyou provide and then they quit (sometimes asking for a \nrefund). This program reveals how to create more Lifers \nand repel the Quitters before they cost you time and \nmoney on wasted fulfillment. \nThe Retention Point is the moment when your \nmembers become so emotionally invested in what you \ndeliver, they become Lifers. By reading the rest of this \nbook over the next 57 minutes, youвҖҷll understand the exact \nsteps to identify your Retention Point. Why it happens and \nhow to make it happen more often, on purpose. \nYou'll discover the thinking that prevents \nMembership Marketers from ever achieving maximum \ngrowth within their programs. And what Member Leaders \ndo differently to create programs that far outstrip all \nexpectations. \n\nRobert Skrob \nYou'll also discover: \nThe five fallacies of membership retention that most \nsubscription businesses implement that actually \nINCREASE member churn rates. \nFive case studies of subscription business \nturnarounds (or successful launches) including a \npublisher, a subscription box, SAAS, an association \nand a charity/nonproftt. \nSimple tactics you can swipe and deploy within \nyour membership program to improve retention. \nHow to achieve 90% to 98% annual renewal \npercentages, even if you believe this is completely \nimpossible for your business. \nThe single biggest myth subscription companies \nbelieve that kills membership growth. \n10 \n\nCHAPTER 4 \nGetting Your New Members to the Retention \nPoint Is the Secret for Fastest Membership \nGrowth \nMost Membership Marketers think they may have one or \ntwo competitors in their niche that are selling a similar \nproduct to a select group of customers. Fact is, your \ncompetition is a lot bigger, a lot better financed and way \nahead of you. \nYou are competing with the best attention seekers in \nthe world. Right now there are hundreds of engineers in \nSilicon Valley trying to engineer apps and games to make \nthem so addictive that users never quit. You also have \nnews producers at Fox News and MSNBC along with \ncountless bloggers trying to capture and keep consumersвҖҷ \nattention to generate ad revenue. And depending on who \nyou believe, you have thousands of Russians trying to \ndistract attention away from whatвҖҷs real as well. \nAdded to all this, youвҖҷve got entertainment executives \ntrying to write stories that captivate viewers. And if you \nown an upstart platform thatвҖҷs trying to get viewers to \nвҖңcut the cordвҖқ and give up their cable television to become \ndependent on your programming, you'll spend some time \nand energy figuring out whatвҖҷs addictive. \nYou'll be fascinated by Netflix data published \nby Wired magazine that shows when a series becomes \naddictive. \n\nRobert Skrob \nrea \nLength of Season (in minutes) вҖ” \n= This is the вҖңHooked Episode,вҖқ the episode after which at least 70 percent of viewers will finish the season. \nSource. вҖңNetti Chill, and Binge> Wired Maga 217, page 19 \nThe chart shows the length of each series in minutes \ntogether with cubes representing each episode. The light \ngray cubes represent the вҖңhooked episode.вҖқ This hooked \nepisode is the episode after which 70% of the viewers \ncomplete the entire series. While some viewers complete \nthe series within a week, others savor the show over several \nmonths. Yet after the вҖңhooked episode,вҖқ 70% stick with \nthe series until the end. \nThis brings up an important question for you: At \nwhat point do your new subscriber members become \nhooked? \nYou see, this is the exact same pattern you have with \nyour Lifers; theyвҖҷve become so emotionally invested that \n\nRetention Point \ntheyвҖҷve reached the Retention Point. This is the point \nwhere 70% to 80% become subscribers for life. \nHowever, you have many times that number of \nsubscribers who start but then disengage within the first \nfew weeks or days. \nMembership Marketers leave the Retention Point \nto chance. Few even track the numbers necessary to \ndetermine where their Retention Point is. \nArmed with the Netflix data, producers and writers \nare writing the first episodes of their programs to move \nthat вҖңhooked episodeвҖқ earlier in the series. ThereвҖҷs detailed \nanalysis about what elements get viewers hooked, such as \nhaving one well-developed main character versus several \ncharacters in the program. Does there need to be a fast- \ndriving plot where a lot happens, or should there be a \nmystery where facts are slowly revealed to the audience? \nThe quicker a viewer gets hooked, the more people \nwho start the series will stay to the end. They are so \nengaged that they talk about the show with their friends, \npost about it on social media and tweet to the producers \nand actors. This leads to the producer receiving plenty of \nmoney to film the next season. \nSame with your Retention Point. Getting your \nsubscribers to the Retention Point faster is the secret to \nslashing churn rates. \nMoving your Retention Point earlier in your \nsubscriber life cycle is the key to long-term growth. The \nRetention Point is when your subscriber decides that he \nwants to be a member of your tribe, that heвҖҷs engaged and \nheвҖҷs going to do what it takes to succeed. Your subscriber \n13 \n\nRobert Skrob \nrarely has a visible success that you or I could recognize. \nInstead, he makes a decision. \nIve dedicated my career to helping my clients \naccelerate the Retention Point. And you are holding the \nresult of more than two decades of work in nonprofit, \nfor-profit, publishing, products, monthly continuity billing \nand annual memberships, all focused on engineering the \ncombination of value, communication and community \nnecessary to get your members to that Retention Point. \nYou move subscribers to the Retention Point by \n1. inspiring them to improve themselves, \n2. giving clarity over their pathway to improvement, \n3. helping them take the first step and \n4, revealing your unique personality. \nWhen you are intentional about these components of your \nnew subscriber welcome, you can move your Retention \nPoint to immediately after the sale. \nThis is also an important answer to your question \nвҖңHow long should each piece of content be?вҖқ ThereвҖҷs a lot \nof speculation that you should keep your videos shorter to \nincrease consumption. \nYet the Netflix data illustrates that the hooked \nepisode has no correlation with the length of the episode \nor the season. Once they are hooked, 70% to 80% of \nviewers will stay to the end. \nвҖңWhat is the optimal length?вҖқ is the wrong question. \nRather, вҖңWhat is the hooked point and how do we move \nthat earlier so more viewers stay to the end?вҖқ is the right \nquestion to answer. \n\nRetention Point \nLet me summarize a few key points to make sure they \nare entirely clear: \n1. The Retention Point is when your subscribers \nbecome Lifers, so emotionally invested in what you \ndeliver that 70% to 80% become subscribers for \nlife. \n2. Most Membership Marketers leave the Retention \nPoint to chance. \n3. Getting your subscribers to the Retention Point \nfaster is the secret to slashing churn rates. \n4. ItвҖҷs surprisingly easy to get a lot more of your new \nmembers to the Retention Point when you try. \nWhen you move beyond focusing on getting new \nmembers to instead measuring and focusing on getting \nnew members to the Retention Point, your membership \nsubscription numbers can triple, grow by a factor of five or \neven more. \nMembership Marketers fail because they believe \nthe wrong things about growing membership. Member \nLeaders succeed because they focus on building \nrelationships with members, getting them to the Retention \nPoint of engagement and making members eagerly want to \nbelong for life. \nIS \n\no aes tent zi \n? 0 eye \n9 вҖ”вҖ” = \nors a wile dom \nh eared \nrs) @teseeem Ce \na a Senecgee sears? aes = \ni @ a ee eee | wea nw ; \n=) nS eiih an Inege aie a ree OE Baa, s jyeestriactina ot \nfee yt pa ps he 6 \nAe ASIN T UR AME 8 a. Peek -V.Apaiteert 4 \nsome вҖңar \neu 2 ggncnl al le medal a \nwale ie Pp Potton bah \nee) ae _ ae Paes! . \na er at tl Ye et : \nx > gael tas ee: : \na 2 etnies nial ed ewan \n| ee ne a 4 \n* \na. = \nae \n\nCHAPTER 5 \nFive Mistakes Membership Marketers \nMake That Prevent Them From \nAchieving Membership Growth \nIt sounds simple enough. Create a subscription program. \nInstead of making one sale, get your customer to sign up \nfor a series of purchases. Boost customer value from day \none! And better yet, call your customers вҖңmembersвҖқ and \nimply that they are receiving something valuable thatвҖҷs not \navailable to just anyone with a pulse and a credit card. \nThe trouble is that customers donвҖҷt feel an obligation \nto retain their вҖңmembership.вҖқ In fact, the opposite is true, \nand they drop out as soon as they find a cheaper version \nof what you deliver. The bonus you dangled in front of \nthem to get them to join worked in that it got them to \njoin, but everything else you did ran them off as fast as \nthey signed up. \nMembers dropping out of monthly subscription \nprograms, continuity programs or memberships has \nreached epidemic proportions. In fact, the average \nretention rate has gotten so bad that many consider it a \nsuccess if you keep just 50% of your new customers for \nthree or four months. While 50% may be better than the \naverage, itвҖҷs far from an acceptable way to try to build a \nbusiness. \nThe number of press releases touting the benefits of \nthe membership model for businesses seems to grow each \nity \n\nRobert Skrob \nday, and yet more and more people who have tried this \nmodel are abandoning it. ItвҖҷs just too hard. \nAt least itвҖҷs too hard following the Membership \nMarketing model. \nThereвҖҷs a missing piece, not included in any of the \nbooks, that makes these programs work. ItвҖҷs the difference \nbetween a Member Marketer and a Member Leader. \nItвҖҷs something that nonprofit trade and professional \nassociations have been doing for decades. And itвҖҷs the \nreason they keep their members for several decades while \nso many marketers are lucky to keep their members for a \nfew weeks. \nIf youвҖҷve been frustrated by slow membership \ngrowth, itвҖҷs not your fault. All of the training out there is \non how to become a Membership Marketer. Today you are \nreading how to become a Member Leader. \nBut first allow me to break down a few Membership \nMarketing mistakes that could be preventing you from \nachieving the growth you need. \nMEMBERSHIP MARKETING MISTAKE #1: \nGive Members More Value \n\nRetention Point \nYou've likely heard that you keep your members by giving \nthem more value. And if you are seeing members quit, one \nof the first things most people will tell you is to deliver \nmore value to your members. \nHeck, this is an epidemic even within the nonprofit \nassociation world, and youвҖҷd think of all people, they \nwould know better. \nHereвҖҷs a simple example. If you deliver a 12-page \nnewsletter each month, double it to 24 pages and youвҖҷll \ncut your churn rate in half, right? No, more value doesnвҖҷt \nequal more retention. This is the same whether you are \ndelivering subscription boxes, SAAS, digital access or \nanything else. \nValue is like water. Too much water is just as bad as \ntoo little. Floods, mud slides and tsunamis are three quick \nexamples of the destruction that too much water creates. \nItвҖҷs the same with value. \nOe Retention PointвҖҷ Accelerator \nis like water: \ntoo much is just as bad as too little. \n19 \n\nRobert Skrob \nWhen you deliver too much value, your member becomes \noverwhelmed. Your member starts feeling bad that he isnвҖҷt \nusing what he purchased, and so he wants to quit. Does \nit matter that other members are easily able to consume \nwhat this new member received? While thatвҖҷs nice for your \nother members, it does nothing to retain the guy who feels \noverwhelmed. \nMEMBERSHIP MARKETING MISTAKE #2: \nSend Members a Gift of Food to Make \nThem Feel Obligated to Stay \nBelieve me, I wish it were this simple. ve never seen \nanyone improve their retention by sending a box of \ncookies, brownies or even ShariвҖҷs Berries. (Although IвҖҷve \nbeen successfully bribed by boxes of Partagas #10 cigars.) \nHereвҖҷs a quick story that'll help you see this from \nyour memberвҖҷs perspective. \nItвҖҷs 3:00 p.m. and you are STARVING. You skipped \nbreakfast and got so busy midday that you havenвҖҷt been \nable to eat lunch yet. You feel sick, have been lightheaded \nfor more than two hours and are ready to eat anything \nthat stands still. \nYou walk into a restaurant and the place is empty. \nAfter all, itвҖҷs 3:00 in the afternoon. Finally you get the \nattention of a hostess who seats you, and then you wait \nand wait for a server. \n\nRetention Point \nYour server walks up and says, вҖңWow, you look \nhungry. Here are the entrГ©es that come out of the kitchen \nthe fastest this time of day, #1, #2 and #3. Would you like \nto order one of those or look at the rest of the menu while \nI get you a drink?вҖқ \nHow does this make you feel? Awesome, right? \nSomeone has recognized you are in pain and has offered \nyou the single fastest way to relieve that pain. This server \nwould get a big tip. \nWhat if you are in this exact situation again, but \ninstead the server walks up to your table with a piece of \nlemon meringue pie and says, вҖңHowdy, darlinвҖҷ, hereвҖҷs a \npiece of pie we had left over from lunch. I hope you enjoy \nit. What can I get you to drink?вҖқ Argh, you havenвҖҷt eaten \nanything yet today. Pie is just going to make you feel sicker \nand hungrier. Pie isnвҖҷt what you want. She could bring you \nthree pieces of pie and it wouldnвҖҷt help. \nPu Retention Point Accelerator \nSolve problems \nto deliver member \nZk \n\nRobert Skrob \nYour member didnвҖҷt join to get a box of cookies. He \njoined to solve a burning problem in his life. The faster \nyou help your member solve that problem, the quicker \nyou'll create a long-lasting relationship. \nWhat if instead, after your server brings your meal, \nyou eat and feel satisfied? Then your server comes by your \ntable and says, вҖңWow, you ate that fast. DarlinвҖҷ, we had \na couple of pieces of pie left over from lunch; would you \nlike a slice of apple or a slice of pecan pie on me as a thank \nyou for dropping in today? Now how do you feel about \nthe gift? \nThere is one place where an unexpected gift can give \nyou a boost in member retention. Once you have made \na promise to solve a problem, then delivered on that \npromise, a gift of food IS a plus that builds good will. \nUntil then, itвҖҷs just empty calories that do nothing for your \nmember or you. \nMEMBERSHIP MARKETING MISTAKE #3: \nUse вҖңPain of DisconnectвҖқ Benefits to \nStop Them From Quitting \nImagine your electricity is shut offвҖ”not only for a few \nhours, but you have made the choice to shut it off forever. \nIt wonвҖҷt be long before the food in your refrigerator starts \n\nRetention Point \nto spoil, cold showers become unbearable and life without \nheat and air conditioning is really uncomfortable. If you \nare married, a few hours, days or weeks without electricity \nwill put a strain on that relationship, especially if you are \nthe one who has made the choice to shut off the power \nand you plan on sticking to it. \nThe dream of most Membership Marketers is to \nprovide members with a benefit thatвҖҷs so valuable that they \nincorporate it into their daily activities and canвҖҷt imagine \nlife without it. Better still, itвҖҷs something that when itвҖҷs \nremoved from their lives, they feel a huge sense of loss or it \nbecomes a disruption. Losing this benefit is as unthinkable \nas living without electricity. \nThese are вҖңpain of disconnectвҖқ or вҖңGolden \nHandcuffsвҖқ benefits. Most often they fail at improving \nretention rates. Sure, your best members get value from \nthe program. Unfortunately, though, the majority of your \nnew members never implement the program, they never \ntry your вҖңpain of disconnectвҖқ benefits and they have вҖңone \nfoot out the doorвҖқ before they ever get started because \nthey sense that you are trying to вҖңt")
```

AnnotatedDocument(extractions=[Extraction(extraction_class='architecture', extraction_text='In the context of natural language processing and LLMs, a prompt is an input provided to the model to generate a response or prediction.', char_interval=CharInterval(start_pos=4000, end_pos=4137), alignment_status=<AlignmentStatus.MATCH_EXACT: 'match_exact'>, extraction_index=1, group_index=0, description=None, attributes=None), Extraction(extraction_class='strategy', extraction_text='Prompts can be used to achieve various kinds of understanding and generation tasks such as text summarization, information extraction, question and answering, text classification, language or code translation, code generation, and code documentation or reasoning.', char_interval=CharInterval(start_pos=4177, end_pos=4443), alignment_status=<AlignmentStatus.MATCH_EXACT: 'match_exact'>, extraction_index=2, group_index=1, description=None, attributes=None), Extraction(extraction_class='strategy', extraction_text='If you are trying to generate output that is robust to a variety of inputs, then it is important to include edge cases in your examples. Edge cases are inputs that are unusual or unexpected, but that the model should still be able to handle.', char_interval=CharInterval(start_pos=18044, end_pos=18287), alignment_status=<AlignmentStatus.MATCH_EXACT: 'match_exact'>, extraction_index=1, group_index=0, description=None, attributes=None), Extraction(extraction_class='strategy', extraction_text='System prompting sets the overall context and purpose for the language model. It defines the вҖҳbig pictureвҖҷ of what the model should be doing, like translating a language, classifying a review etc.', char_interval=CharInterval(start_pos=18495, end_pos=18694), alignment_status=<AlignmentStatus.MATCH_EXACT: 'match_exact'>, extraction_index=2, group_index=1, description=None, attributes=None), Extraction(extraction_class='strategy', extraction_text='Contextual prompting provides specific details or background information relevant to the current conversation or task. It helps the model to understand the nuances of whatвҖҷs being asked and tailor the response accordingly.', char_interval=CharInterval(start_pos=18697, end_pos=18922), alignment_status=<AlignmentStatus.MATCH_EXACT: 'match_exact'>, extraction_index=3, group_index=2, description=None, attributes=None), Extraction(extraction_class='strategy', extraction_text='Role prompting assigns a specific character or identity for the language model to adopt.', char_interval=CharInterval(start_pos=18925, end_pos=19014), alignment_status=<AlignmentStatus.MATCH_EXACT: 'match_exact'>, extraction_index=4, group_index=3, description=None, attributes=None)], text='Prompt  \nEngineering\nAuthor: Lee Boonstra\n\nPrompt Engineering\nFebruary 20252Acknowledgements\nContent contributors\nMichael Sherman\nYuan Cao\nErick Armbrust\nAnant Nawalgaria\nAntonio Gulli\nSimone Cammel\nCurators and Editors\nAntonio Gulli\nAnant Nawalgaria\nGrace Mollison \nTechnical Writer\nJoey Haymaker\nDesigner\nMichael Lanning \n\nIntroduction  6\nPrompt engineering  7\nLLM output configuration 8\nOutput length  8\nSampling controls 9\nTemperature  9\nTop-K and top-P 10\nPutting it all together 11\nPrompting techniques  13\nGeneral prompting / zero shot 13\nOne-shot & few-shot  15\nSystem, contextual and role prompting 18\nSystem prompting  19\nRole prompting 21\nContextual prompting 23Table of contents\n\nStep-back prompting  25\nChain of Thought (CoT) 29\nSelf-consistency  32\nTree of Thoughts (ToT) 36\nReAct (reason & act) 37\nAutomatic Prompt Engineering 40\nCode prompting  42\nPrompts for writing code 42\nPrompts for explaining code  44\nPrompts for translating code 46\nPrompts for debugging and reviewing code 48\nWhat about multimodal prompting? 54\nBest Practices  54\nProvide examples 54\nDesign with simplicity  55\nBe specific about the output 56\nUse Instructions over Constraints 56\nControl the max token length  58\nUse variables in prompts 58\nExperiment with input formats and writing styles 59\nFor few-shot prompting with classification tasks, mix up the classes 59\nAdapt to model updates 60\nExperiment with output formats  60\nJSON Repair  61\nWorking with Schemas  62\nExperiment together with other prompt engineers  63\nCoT Best practices  64\nDocument the various prompt attempts 64\nSummary  66\nEndnotes  68\nPrompt Engineering\nFebruary 20256Introduction\nWhen thinking about a large language model input and output, a text prompt (sometimes \naccompanied by other modalities such as image prompts) is the input the model uses \nto predict a specific output. You donвҖҷt need to be a data scientist or a machine learning \nengineer вҖ“ everyone can write a prompt. However, crafting the most effective prompt can be \ncomplicated. Many aspects of your prompt affect its efficacy: the model you use, the modelвҖҷs \ntraining data, the model configurations, your word-choice, style and tone, structure, and \ncontext all matter. Therefore, prompt engineering is an iterative process. Inadequate prompts \ncan lead to ambiguous, inaccurate responses, and can hinder the modelвҖҷs ability to provide \nmeaningful output.You donвҖҷt need to be a data \nscientist or a machine learning \nengineer вҖ“ everyone can write \na prompt.\nPrompt Engineering\nFebruary 20257When you chat with the Gemini chatbot,1 you basically write prompts, however this \nwhitepaper focuses on writing prompts for the Gemini model within Vertex AI or by using  \nthe API, because by prompting the model directly you will have access to the configuration \nsuch as temperature etc.\nThis whitepaper discusses prompt engineering in detail. We will look into the various \nprompting techniques to help you getting started and share tips and best practices to \nbecome a prompting expert. We will also discuss some of the challenges you can face  \nwhile crafting prompts.\nPrompt engineering\nRemember how an LLM works; itвҖҷs a prediction engine. The model takes sequential text as \nan input and then predicts what the following token should be, based on the data it was \ntrained on. The LLM is operationalized to do this over and over again, adding the previously \npredicted token to the end of the sequential text for predicting the following token. The next \ntoken prediction is based on the relationship between whatвҖҷs in the previous tokens and what \nthe LLM has seen during its training. \nWhen you write a prompt, you are attempting to set up the LLM to predict the right sequence \nof tokens. Prompt engineering is the process of designing high-quality prompts that guide \nLLMs to produce accurate outputs. This process involves tinkering to find the best prompt, \noptimizing prompt length, and evaluating a promptвҖҷs writing style and structure in relation \nto the task. In the context of natural language processing and LLMs, a prompt is an input \nprovided to the model to generate a response or prediction.\nPrompt Engineering\nFebruary 20258These prompts can be used to achieve various kinds of understanding and generation \ntasks such as text summarization, information extraction, question and answering, text \nclassification, language or code translation, code generation, and code documentation \nor reasoning.\nPlease feel free to refer to GoogleвҖҷs prompting guides2,3 with simple and effective \nprompting examples.\nWhen prompt engineering, you will start by choosing a model. Prompts might need to be \noptimized for your specific model, regardless of whether you use Gemini language models in \nVertex AI, GPT, Claude, or an open source model like Gemma or LLaMA.\nBesides the prompt, you will also need to tinker with the various configurations of a LLM.\nLLM output configuration\nOnce you choose your model you will need to figure out the model configuration. Most LLMs \ncome with various configuration options that control the LLMвҖҷs output. Effective prompt \nengineering requires setting these configurations optimally for your task.\nOutput length\nAn important configuration setting is the number of tokens to generate in a response. \nGenerating more tokens requires more computation from the LLM, leading to higher energy \nconsumption, potentially slower response times, and higher costs.\nPrompt Engineering\nFebruary 20259Reducing the output length of the LLM doesnвҖҷt cause the LLM to become more stylistically \nor textually succinct in the output it creates, it just causes the LLM to stop predicting more \ntokens once the limit is reached. If your needs require a short output length, youвҖҷll also \npossibly need to engineer your prompt to accommodate.\nOutput length restriction is especially important for some LLM prompting techniques, like \nReAct, where the LLM will keep emitting useless tokens after the response you want.\nBe aware, generating more tokens requires more computation from the LLM, leading \nto higher energy consumption and potentially slower response times, which leads to \nhigher costs.\nSampling controls\nLLMs do not formally predict a single token. Rather, LLMs predict probabilities for what the \nnext token could be, with each token in the LLMвҖҷs vocabulary getting a probability. Those \ntoken probabilities are then sampled to determine what the next produced token will be. \nTemperature, top-K, and top-P are the most common configuration settings that determine \nhow predicted token probabilities are processed to choose a single output token.\nTemperature\nTemperature controls the degree of randomness in token selection. Lower temperatures \nare good for prompts that expect a more deterministic response, while higher temperatures \ncan lead to more diverse or unexpected results. A temperature of 0 (greedy decoding) is \nPrompt Engineering\nFebruary 202510deterministic: the highest probability token is always selected (though note that if two tokens \nhave the same highest predicted probability, depending on how tiebreaking is implemented \nyou may not always get the same output with temperature 0).\nTemperatures close to the max tend to create more random output. And as temperature gets \nhigher and higher, all tokens become equally likely to be the next predicted token.\nThe Gemini temperature control can be understood in a similar way to the softmax function \nused in machine learning. A low temperature setting mirrors a low softmax temperature (T), \nemphasizing a single, preferred temperature with high certainty. A higher Gemini temperature \nsetting is like a high softmax temperature, making a wider range of temperatures around \nthe selected setting more acceptable. This increased uncertainty accommodates scenarios \nwhere a rigid, precise temperature may not be essential like for example when experimenting \nwith creative outputs.\nTop-K and top-P\nTop-K and top-P (also known as nucleus sampling)4 are two sampling settings used in LLMs \nto restrict the predicted next token to come from tokens with the top predicted probabilities.  \nLike temperature, these sampling settings control the randomness and diversity of \ngenerated text.\nвҖў Top-K sampling selects the top K most likely tokens from the modelвҖҷs predicted \ndistribution. The higher top-K, the more creative and varied the modelвҖҷs output; the \nlower top-K, the more restive and factual the modelвҖҷs output. A top-K of 1 is equivalent to \ngreedy decoding.\nPrompt Engineering\nFebruary 202511вҖў Top-P  sampling selects the top tokens whose cumulative probability does not exceed \na certain value (P). Values for P range from 0 (greedy decoding) to 1 (all tokens in the \nLLMвҖҷs vocabulary).\nThe best way to choose between top-K and top-P is to experiment with both methods (or \nboth together) and see which one produces the results you are looking for. \nPutting it all together\nChoosing between top-K, top-P, temperature, and the number of tokens to generate, \ndepends on the specific application and desired outcome, and the settings all impact one \nanother. ItвҖҷs also important to make sure you understand how your chosen model combines \nthe different sampling settings together.\nIf temperature, top-K, and top-P are all available (as in Vertex Studio), tokens that meet \nboth the top-K and top-P criteria are candidates for the next predicted token, and then \ntemperature is applied to sample from the tokens that passed the top-K and top-P criteria. If \nonly top-K or top-P is available, the behavior is the same but only the one top-K or P setting \nis used. \nIf temperature is not available, whatever tokens meet the top-K and/or top-P criteria are then \nrandomly selected from to produce a single next predicted token.\nAt extreme settings of one sampling configuration value, that one sampling setting either \ncancels out other configuration settings or becomes irrelevant.\nPrompt Engineering\nFebruary 202512вҖў If you set temperature to 0, top-K and top-P become irrelevantвҖ“the most probable \ntoken becomes the next token predicted. If you set temperature extremely high (above \n1вҖ“generally into the 10s), temperature becomes irrelevant and whatever tokens make \nit through the top-K and/or top-P criteria are then randomly sampled to choose a next \npredicted token.\nвҖў If you set top-K to 1, temperature and top-P become irrelevant. Only one token passes the \ntop-K criteria, and that token is the next predicted token. If you set top-K extremely high, \nlike to the size of the LLMвҖҷs vocabulary, any token with a nonzero probability of being the \nnext token will meet the top-K criteria and none are selected out.\nвҖў If you set top-P to 0 (or a very small value), most LLM sampling implementations will then \nonly consider the most probable token to meet the top-P criteria, making temperature and \ntop-K irrelevant. If you set top-P to 1, any token with a nonzero probability of being the \nnext token will meet the top-P criteria, and none are selected out.\nAs a general starting point, a temperature of .2, top-P of .95, and top-K of 30 will give you \nrelatively coherent results that can be creative but not excessively so. If you want especially \ncreative results, try starting with a temperature of .9, top-P of .99, and top-K of 40. And if you \nwant less creative results, try starting with a temperature of .1, top-P of .9, and top-K of 20. \nFinally, if your task always has a single correct answer (e.g., answering a math problem), start \nwith a temperature of 0.\nNOTE:  With more freedom (higher temperature, top-K, top-P, and output tokens), the LLM \nmight generate text that is less relevant.\nWARNING:  Have you ever seen a response ending with a large amount of filler words? This \nis also known as the "repetition loop bug", which is a common issue in Large Language \nModels where the model gets stuck in a cycle, repeatedly generating the same (filler) word, \nphrase, or sentence structure, often exacerbated by inappropriate temperature and top-k/\nPrompt Engineering\nFebruary 202513top-p settings. This can occur at both low and high temperature settings, though for different \nreasons. At low temperatures, the model becomes overly deterministic, sticking rigidly to the \nhighest probability path, which can lead to a loop if that path revisits previously generated \ntext.  Conversely, at high temperatures, the model\'s output becomes excessively random, \nincreasing the probability that a randomly chosen word or phrase will, by chance, lead back \nto a prior state, creating a loop due to the vast number of available options.  In both cases, \nthe model\'s sampling process gets "stuck," resulting in monotonous and unhelpful output \nuntil the output window is filled. Solving this often requires careful tinkering with temperature \nand top-k/top-p values to find the optimal balance between determinism and randomness.\nPrompting techniques\nLLMs are tuned to follow instructions and are trained on large amounts of data so they can \nunderstand a prompt and generate an answer. But LLMs arenвҖҷt perfect; the clearer your \nprompt text, the better it is for the LLM to predict the next likely text. Additionally, specific \ntechniques that take advantage of how LLMs are trained and how LLMs work will help you get \nthe relevant results from LLMs\nNow that we understand what prompt engineering is and what it takes, letвҖҷs dive into some \nexamples of the most important prompting techniques.\nGeneral prompting / zero shot\nA zero-shot5 prompt is the simplest type of prompt. It only provides a description of a task \nand some text for the LLM to get started with. This input could be anything: a question, a \nstart of a story, or instructions. The name zero-shot stands for вҖҷno examplesвҖҷ.\nPrompt Engineering\nFebruary 202514LetвҖҷs use Vertex AI Studio (for Language) in Vertex AI,6 which provides a playground to test \nprompts. In Table 1, you will see an example zero-shot prompt to classify movie reviews. \nThe table format as used below is a great way of documenting prompts. Your prompts will \nlikely go through many iterations before they end up in a codebase, so itвҖҷs important to keep \ntrack of your prompt engineering work in a disciplined, structured way. More on this table \nformat, the importance of tracking prompt engineering work, and the prompt development \nprocess is in the Best Practices section later in this chapter (вҖңDocument the various prompt \nattemptsвҖқ).  \nThe model temperature should be set to a low number, since no creativity is needed, and we \nuse the gemini-pro default top-K and top-P values, which effectively disable both settings \n(see вҖҳLLM Output ConfigurationвҖҷ above). Pay attention to the generated output. The words \ndisturbing  and masterpiece  should make the prediction a little more complicated, as both \nwords are used in the same sentence.\nPrompt Engineering\nFebruary 202515Name 1_1_movie_classification\nGoal Classify movie reviews as positive, neutral or negative.\nModel gemini-pro\nTemperature 0.1 Token Limit 5\nTop-K N/A Top-P 1\nPrompt Classify movie reviews as POSITIVE, NEUTRAL or NEGATIVE. \nReview: "Her" is a disturbing study revealing the direction \nhumanity is headed if AI is allowed to keep evolving, \nunchecked. I wish there were more movies like this masterpiece. \nSentiment:\nOutput POSITIVE\nTable 1. An example of zero-shot prompting\nWhen zero-shot doesnвҖҷt work, you can provide demonstrations or examples in the prompt, \nwhich leads to вҖңone-shotвҖқ and вҖңfew-shotвҖқ prompting. General prompting / zero shot\nOne-shot & few-shot\nWhen creating prompts for AI models, it is helpful to provide examples. These examples can \nhelp the model understand what you are asking for. Examples are especially useful when you \nwant to steer the model to a certain output structure or pattern. \nA one-shot prompt , provides a single  example,  hence the name one-shot. The idea is the \nmodel has an example it can imitate to best complete the task.\nA few-shot prompt 7 provides multiple  examples to the model. This approach shows the  \nmodel a pattern that it needs to follow. The idea is similar to one-shot, but multiple examples \nof the desired pattern increases the chance the model follows the pattern.\nPrompt Engineering\nFebruary 202516The number of examples you need for few-shot prompting depends on a few factors, \nincluding the complexity of the task, the quality of the examples, and the capabilities of the \ngenerative AI (gen AI) model you are using. As a general rule of thumb, you should use at \nleast three to five examples for few-shot prompting. However, you may need to use more \nexamples for more complex tasks, or you may need to use fewer due to the input length \nlimitation of your model.\nTable 2 shows a few-shot prompt example, letвҖҷs use the same gemini-pro model \nconfiguration settings as before, other than increasing the token limit to accommodate the \nneed for a longer response.\nGoal Parse pizza orders to JSON\nModel gemini-pro\nTemperature 0.1 Token Limit 250\nTop-K N/A Top-P 1\nPrompt Parse a customer\'s pizza order into valid JSON:\nEXAMPLE:\nI want a small pizza with cheese, tomato sauce, and pepperoni.\nJSON Response:\n```\n{\n"size": "small",\n"type": "normal",\n"ingredients": [[ "cheese", "tomato sauce ", "peperoni "]]\n}\n```\nContinues next page...\nPrompt Engineering\nFebruary 202517Prompt EXAMPLE:\nCan I get a large pizza with tomato sauce, basil and mozzarella \n{\n"size": "large",\n"type": "normal",\n"ingredients ": [["tomato sauce ", "bazel", "mozzarella "]]\n}\nNow, I would like a large pizza, with the first half cheese and \nmozzarella. And the other tomato sauce, ham and pineapple.\nJSON Response:\nOutput ```\n{\n"size": "large",\n"type": "half-half ",\n"ingredients": [["cheese", "mozzarella"], ["tomato sauce", \n"ham", "pineapple"]]\n}\n```\nTable 2. An example of few-shot prompting\nWhen you choose examples for your prompt, use examples that are relevant to the task you \nwant to perform. The examples should be diverse, of high quality, and well written. One small \nmistake can confuse the model and will result in undesired output.\nIf you are trying to generate output that is robust to a variety of inputs, then it is important to \ninclude edge cases in your examples. Edge cases are inputs that are unusual or unexpected, \nbut that the model should still be able to handle.\nPrompt Engineering\nFebruary 202518System, contextual and role prompting\nSystem, contextual and role prompting are all techniques used to guide how LLMs generate \ntext, but they focus on different aspects:\nвҖў System prompting  sets the overall context and purpose for the language model. It \ndefines the вҖҳbig pictureвҖҷ of what the model should be doing, like translating a language, \nclassifying a review etc.\nвҖў Contextual prompting  provides specific details or background information relevant to \nthe current conversation or task. It helps the model to understand the nuances of whatвҖҷs \nbeing asked and tailor the response accordingly.\nвҖў Role prompting  assigns a specific character or identity for the language model to adopt. \nThis helps the model generate responses that are consistent with the assigned role and its \nassociated knowledge and behavior.\nThere can be considerable overlap between system, contextual, and role prompting. E.g. a \nprompt that assigns a role to the system, can also have a context.\nHowever, each type of prompt serves a slightly different primary purpose:\nвҖў System prompt: Defines the modelвҖҷs fundamental capabilities and overarching purpose.\nвҖў Contextual prompt: Provides immediate, task-specific information to guide the response. \nItвҖҷs highly specific to the current task or input, which is dynamic.\nвҖў Role prompt: Frames the modelвҖҷs output style and voice. It adds a layer of specificity \nand personality.\nPrompt Engineering\nFebruary 202519Distinguishing between system, contextual, and role prompts provides a framework for \ndesigning prompts with clear intent, allowing for flexible combinations and making it easier to \nanalyze how each prompt type influences the language modelвҖҷs output.\nLetвҖҷs dive into these three different kinds of prompts.\nSystem prompting\nTable 3 contains a system prompt, where I specify additional information on how to return the \noutput. I increased the temperature to get a higher creativity level, and I specified a higher \ntoken limit. However, because of my clear instruction on how to return the output the model \ndidnвҖҷt return extra text.\nGoal Classify movie reviews as positive, neutral or negative.\nModel gemini-pro\nTemperature 1 Token Limit 5\nTop-K 40 Top-P 0.8\nPrompt Classify movie reviews as positive, neutral or negative. Only \nreturn the label in uppercase.\nReview: "Her" is a disturbing study revealing the direction \nhumanity is headed if AI is allowed to keep evolving, \nunchecked. It\'s so disturbing I couldn\'t watch it.\nSentiment:\nOutput NEGATIVE\nTable 3. An example of system prompting\nSystem prompts can be useful for generating output that meets specific requirements. The \nname вҖҳsystem promptвҖҷ actually stands for вҖҳproviding an additional task to the systemвҖҷ. For \nexample, you could use a system prompt to generate a code snippet that is compatible with \na specific programming language, or you could use a system prompt to return a certain \nstructure. Have a look into Table 4, where I return the output in JSON format.\nPrompt Engineering\nFebruary 202520Goal Classify movie reviews as positive, neutral or negative, return JSON.\nModel gemini-pro\nTemperature 1 Token Limit 1024\nTop-K 40 Top-P 0.8\nPromptClassify movie reviews as positive, neutral or negative. Return \nvalid JSON:\nReview: "Her" is a disturbing study revealing the direction \nhumanity is headed if AI is allowed to keep evolving, \nunchecked. It\'s so disturbing I couldn\'t watch it.\nSchema:\n```\nMOVIE:\n{\n"sentiment": String "POSITIVE" | "NEGATIVE" | "NEUTRAL",\n"name": String\n}\nMOVIE REVIEWS:\n{\n"movie_reviews": [MOVIE]\n}\n```\nJSON Response:\nOutput ```\n{\n  "movie_reviews": [\n    {\n      "sentiment": "NEGATIVE",\n      "name": "Her"\n    }\n  ]\n}\n```\nTable 4. An example of system prompting with JSON format\nPrompt Engineering\nFebruary 202521There are some benefits in returning JSON objects from a prompt that extracts data. In \na real-world application I donвҖҷt need to manually create this JSON format, I can already \nreturn the data in a sorted order (very handy when working with datetime objects), but most \nimportantly, by prompting for a JSON format it forces the model to create a structure and \nlimit hallucinations.\nSystem prompts can also be really useful for safety and toxicity. To control the output, simply \nadd an additional line to your prompt like: вҖҳYou should be respectful in your answer.вҖҷ.\nRole prompting\nRole prompting is a technique in prompt engineering that involves assigning a specific role to \nthe gen AI model. This can help the model to generate more relevant and informative output, \nas the model can craft its responses to the specific role that it has been assigned.\nFor example, you could role prompt a gen AI model to be a book editor, a kindergarten \nteacher, or a motivational speaker. Once the model has been assigned a role, you can then \ngive it prompts that are specific to that role. For example, you could prompt a teacher to \ncreate a lesson plan that they can then review. Have a look into Table 5, where the model \ntakes the role of a travel guide.\nPrompt Engineering\nFebruary 202522Goal Act as travel guide and provide 3 travel suggestions\nModel gemini-pro\nTemperature 1 Token Limit 1024\nTop-K 40 Top-P 0.8\nPrompt I want you to act as a travel guide. I will write to you \nabout my location and you will suggest 3 places to visit near \nme. In some cases, I will also give you the type of places I \nwill visit.\nMy suggestion: "I am in Amsterdam and I want to visit \nonly museums."\nTravel Suggestions:\nOutput 1. Rijksmuseum: World-renowned museum, known for Dutch masterpieces \nlike RembrandtвҖҷs вҖңThe Night Watch.вҖқ DonвҖҷt miss the incredible collection of \npaintings and artifacts showcasing Dutch art and history.\n2. Van Gogh Museum: Immerse yourself in the captivating works of Vincent \nvan Gogh. The museum houses the largest collection of his paintings and \ndrawings, including вҖңThe Starry NightвҖқ and вҖңSunflowers.вҖқ\n3. Stedelijk Museum Amsterdam: Discover modern and contemporary art \nfrom around the world. The museumвҖҷs collection includes works by Picasso, \nKandinsky, and Rietveld, housed in a striking modern building.\nTable 5. An example of role prompting\nThe above example shows an example of taking the role of a travel agent. When you \nwould change the role to a teacher of geography, you would notice that you will receive a \ndifferent response.\nDefining a role perspective for an AI model gives it a blueprint of the tone, style, and focused \nexpertise youвҖҷre looking for to improve the quality, relevance, and effectiveness of your \noutput. \nPrompt Engineering\nFebruary 202523Here are some styles you can choose from which I find effective:\nConfrontational, Descriptive, Direct, Formal, Humorous, Influential, Informal, \nInspirational, Persuasive\nLetвҖҷs change our prompt in Table 6 to include a humorous and inspirational style.\nPrompt I want you to act as a travel guide. I will write to you about \nmy location and you will suggest 3 places to visit near me in \na humorous style.\nMy suggestion: "I am in Manhattan."\nTravel Suggestions:\nOutput 1. Behold the Empire State of Mind: Ascend to the dizzying heights of the Empire \nState Building and bask in the glory of Manhattan\'s skyline. Prepare to feel like \nKing Kong atop the Big Apple, minus the giant ape-sized banana.\n2. Get Artsy-Fartsy at MoMA: Unleash your inner art aficionado at the Museum \nof Modern Art (MoMA). Gaze upon masterpieces that will boggle your \nmind and make you question whether your stick-figure drawings have any \nartistic merit.\n3. Shop вҖҳTil You Drop on Fifth Avenue: Indulge in some retail therapy on the \niconic Fifth Avenue. Brace yourself for sticker shock as you window-shop (or \nactually shop) at designer boutiques that will make your wallet cry. But hey, \nyouвҖҷre in Manhattan, so you might as well embrace the fabulousness!\nTable 6. An example of role prompting with a humorous tone and style\nContextual prompting\nBy providing contextual prompts, you can help ensure that your AI interactions are as \nseamless and efficient as possible. The model will be able to more quickly understand your \nrequest and be able to generate more accurate and relevant responses, as you can see in the \nexample of Table 7.\nPrompt Engineering\nFebruary 202524Goal Suggest articles for a blog about retro games\nModel gemini-pro\nTemperature 1 Token Limit 1024\nTop-K 40 Top-P 0.8\nPrompt Context: You are writing for a blog about retro 80\'s arcade \nvideo games.\nSuggest 3 topics to write an article about with a few lines of \ndescription of what this article should contain.\nOutput 1. **The Evolution of Arcade Cabinet Design:** \nThis article would explore the evolution of arcade cabinet designs, from the \nearly wood and metal cabinets of the 1970s to the sleek, neon-lit designs of \nthe 1980s and beyond.\n2. **Blast From The Past: Iconic Arcade Games of The 80вҖҷs:** \n- This article would feature a glimpse into the history, impact, and legacy \nof some of the most iconic and influential arcade games of the 1980s, \nhighlighting their innovations, popular mechanics, and enduring charm.\n3. **The Rise and Retro Revival of Pixel Art:** \n- This article would delve into the evolution of pixel art as a defining visual \nstyle of early arcade games, tracing its roots, exploring its techniques, and \ndiscussing the resurgence of pixel art in modern games and digital art.\nTable 7. An example of contextual prompting\nPrompt Engineering\nFebruary 202525Step-back prompting\nStep-back8 prompting is a technique for improving the performance by prompting the LLM \nto first consider a general question related to the specific task at hand, and then feeding the \nanswer to that general question into a subsequent prompt for the specific task. This вҖҳstep \nbackвҖҷ allows the LLM to activate relevant background knowledge and reasoning processes \nbefore attempting to solve the specific problem.\nBy considering the broader and underlying principles, LLMs can generate more accurate and \ninsightful responses. Step-back prompting encourages LLMs to think critically and apply their \nknowledge in new and creative ways. It changes the final prompt doing the task by utilizing \nmore knowledge in the LLMвҖҷs parameters than would otherwise come into play when the LLM \nis prompted directly.\nIt can help to mitigate biases in LLM responses, by focusing on general principles instead of \nspecific details, step-back prompting.\nLetвҖҷs have a look into these examples to understand how step-back prompting can improve \nthe results. LetвҖҷs first review a traditional prompt (Table 8) before we compare it to a step \nback prompt (Table 9):\nPrompt Engineering\nFebruary 202526Goal Write a storyline for a level of a first-person shooter video game.\nModel gemini-pro\nTemperature 1 Token Limit 1024\nTop-K 40 Top-P 0.8\nPrompt Write a one paragraph storyline for a new level of a first-\nperson shooter video game that is challenging and engaging.\nOutput The level begins with the playerвҖҷs squad being ambushed in a dense urban area \nby a relentless enemy faction. Players must fight their way through a maze of \nback alleys and dilapidated buildings, utilizing stealth and precision to eliminate \nenemy forces and avoid detection. The player must use their environment to their \nadvantage, setting traps, utilizing cover, and making calculated moves to survive. \nAlong the way, they uncover intel suggesting the enemy is planning a devastating \nattack, and the player must race against time to gather evidence and relay it back \nto their headquarters before the enemy can carry out their plans.\nTable 8. A traditional prompt before we compare it with a step back prompt\nWhen you set the temperature to 1, you might end up with all kinds of creative writings for a \nstoryline, b')
```

Rakesh K umar\nGupta), Wife (Sneha Singh), Daughter (Natasha Gupta), and\nmy siblingsвҖҷ families\nSelenium open-source community\nF o r e w o r d\nIn some ways, you can view writing code as an act of\ncollaborative art. Just as no two people would say the same\nthing if asked to describe a sunrise, those same people will\nwrite code in diп¬Җer ent ways, even if the goal is same for\nboth of them. The pr ocess of taking a description of what we\nwant to do and converting it into code is a creative one.\nAlthough we might write code on our own as a hobby, in\nprofessional settings we work in teams, with each person on\nthe team working on the part of the tapestry of the\napplication that suits their strengths. Not only is the process\nartistic, but also collaborative.\nThe expression вҖңif the only tool you have is a hammer,\neverything looks like a nailвҖқ is frequently used in software\ndevelopment circles. ThatвҖҷs becau se, just as with any\nartistic ende avor, to develop stable software proп¬Ғciently,\none needs to lear n about the tools that can be used, and the\ntechniques that can be applied. ItвҖҷs not enough to know how\nto use a tool, we need to know when  to use it. So, not only is\nsoftware development a collaborative artistic endeavor, but\nalso one wher e we need to know tools and techniques in\norder to work eп¬Җectively .\nBut thereвҖҷs a problem. How do we learn a new tool or\ntechnique? How do we begin to understand how and wher e\nto use it? After all, weвҖҷve been writing software for a long\ntime, so there must be a large body of tools and techniques\nto draw fr om.\nLetвҖҷs take the п¬Ғeld of writing web apps. Tim Berners-L ee\npublished the п¬Ғrst website while working at CERN in\nDecember 1990. Originally, web pages were static and\nsimple. When Netsca pe introduce d Javascript to the world,\nthese simple, static pages became a little more interesting.\nWhen Microsoft introduced XmlHT TPRequest, it became\npossible for pages to request information from web servers\nwithout needing to reload. In 1999, Darci DiNucci introduced\nthe world to the phrase вҖңWeb 2.0вҖқ, but it didnвҖҷt become a\nwidespr ead phrase until the early 2000s.\nThe early 2000s was when we saw an explosion of creativity\non the Web. Sites suddenly went from being relatively static\nto being incredibly dynamic. The technologies powering\nWeb 2.0 enabled sites like Google Maps to exist. And that\nexplosion of creativity continues to this day, with websites\nbecoming ever mor e comple x, dynamic, and capable.\nAs the W eb has gr own, so has the tooling.\nIn 2004, Jason Huggins started the Selenium project. In\n2011, we released Seleni um 2, and Selenium 3 followed in\n2016. Most recently, Selenium 4 was released in 2021, a full\nten years after the world  was introduced to the WebDriver\nAPIs. Over those many years, the tool has grown and\nadapted to the changing world around it, and the\ncommunity has gather ed a wealth of knowledge and\nexperience, not only abou t Selenium, but also how to use it,\nand get the most out of it.\nThis brings me to the book.\nYou hold in your hands a book that is ostensibly about\nSelenium. Except itвҖҷs about far more than that. You see, in\nthis book, Robin takes us on a journey from neophyte to\nmaster tester, introducing us not only to the tool that is\nSelenium, but also to the conte xt in which it can be used.\nThis book provides a distillation of many of the current вҖңbest\npracticesвҖқ that many teams have adopted when using\nSelenium, particularly the Java bindings. Those are practices\nthat have evolved over many years, and have been shown\nto be eп¬Җective time and again.\nJust as a paintbrush is most useful when there is a canvas\nand paint, so Selenium is most useful when used with test\nframeworks, design patter ns, and logging. And just as there\nare many ways in which a paintbrush can apply paint to the\ncanvas, so Selenium has many interesting capabilities and\nAPIs. In this metap hor, Robin is the art teacher, wher ein he\nstarts gently, introducing us to Selenium through its IDE,\nand then builds upon that, chapter after chapter . Each step\nof the way, he provides supporting information and\nconcepts.\nSo, even if this book is about Selenium Test Automation,\nwhen youвҖҷve п¬Ғnished reading it, youвҖҷll be familiar with\ndiп¬Җer ent kinds of tests, testing in general, design patter ns,\nand other tools and frameworks which can be used with\nSelenium.\nWhen youвҖҷve п¬Ғnished reading it, youвҖҷll be better able to\nparticipate in the wildly entertaining collaborative art\nproject we call software development, and thatвҖҷs a\nwonderful thing.\nSimon Mavi Stewart\nCreator of WebDriver\nSelenium Project Lead 2009-2021\nCoeditor of the WebD river and WebDriver Bidi W3C\nspeciп¬Ғcations\nLondon, January 2024\nAbout the Author\nRobin  is a versatil e engineerin g leader with over 15 years\nof experienc e in software delivery across startup s, scale-\nups, and enterprises. With a metrics-driven approach, he\nhas elevated  the engineering maturity of Dev/QA /DevOps\nteams for diverse domains such as BFSI, EdTech, Retail, and\nDeveloper Experience. Experienced in multiple tech stacks,\nRobin\'s hand s-on leader ship style yields positive  results.\nBeyond work, he mentors at ADPList and Plato, contributes\nto open-sour ce projects like Selenium, created TestZeus (the\nonly open-sour ce test automation framework for Salesfor ce),\nand has author ed courses. He is also recognized as a\nspeak er at international events such as Dreamforce (by\nSalesfor ce) and Selenium Confer ence. Balancing\nresponsibilities as a dad at home and M.O.M (Manager of\nManagers) at the oп¬ғce, Robin excels in both personal and\nprofessional r ealms.\nFor mor e details, visit www .robin-gupta.com\nAbout the Technical Reviewer\nShreya Singh Patel has been on an automation adventur e\nfor nearly a decade, building rock-solid testing frameworks\nwith Selenium  and Java like a tech architect conjuring bug-\nresistant fortresses. But her repertoir e doesn\'t end there!\nShe works on API automation, mobile automation, database,\nand load testing. Every master ed technology adds another\nvibrant thr ead to her ever -expanding skillset.\nShe has been  working in the IT industry for over 7 years on\nvarious automation projects, also witnessing the rise in\ndemand for automation in each industry, not just IT .\nBut she is not just about conquering bugs, she\'s about\nlighting the way for other s. Witnessing the "Aha!" moments\nwhen her mentor ed manual testers transfor m into\nautomation heroes fuels her passion more than any\nsquashed superbug. She currently mentors colleagues,\nguiding them through code and frameworks until they write\ntheir own automation victories.\nShe actively volunteers as Internship Operation Head for\nboth summer and winter internships at Internity Foundation,\na startup. Her passion for mentorship shines through as she\nguides interns, sharing her knowledge and empowering\nthem to build their own careers in the exciting world of\nautomation.\nThat\'s why this book review opportunity feels like destiny .\nIt\'s a chance for her to share her hard-won knowledge,\nacting as a friendly translator through the sometimes-\nconfusing world of Selenium for eager beginners. Imagine\nher as a bridge, connecting comple x code to wide-eyed\nminds, and empowering them to embark on their own\ntesting adventur es.\nA c k n o w l e d g e m e n t s\nFirst and foremost, I want to express my gratitude to the\nteam at Orange AVA. Without their help and support, this\nbook would not have been possible. I also want to thank\nShreya Singh Patel, who perfor med the technical review\nand, parado xically, acted as the tester of a book on the\ntopic of test automation.\nAndrew Knight (passionat ely called The Automatio n Panda)\nand Ivan Harris (CPTO at Provar) have patiently answer ed\nmy questions about writing a book - thank you, folks.\nSpecial recognition goes to Tristan Lombar d (a.k.a Stage\nMom) for keeping me on track during the writing process\nand guiding me to do the right things, not the easy things.\nThis book has hidden treasur es across the chapte rs in the\nform of quotes from industry leaders and open-sour ce\ncontributors. I want to thank these luminaries for giving\naway these pearls of wisdom.\nA big thanks to Simon Stewart (creator of WebDriver) for\nwriting the forewor d to this book and lighting the path for\nmany engineers lik e me.\n2023 was a rough year for me, so I want to express my\ngratitude to my wife for supporting me at every stage of this\nwriting jour ney.\nPreface\nOnce upon a time, in a land not so far away, I was chatting\nwith my friend about a certiп¬Ғcation exam for a test\nautomation tool that I had taken. He was telling me about\nthis new thing called Selenium. I was entirely skeptical of\nthis new open-sour ce technology, believing it was a fad\nbound to fade away . Lo and behold, Selenium has not only\nturned me around but also raised the bar for browser\nautomation. Since its inception in 2004, Selenium has stood\ntall for an eternity in the technology domain, wher e trends\nrise and fall every six months.\nSelenium is a powerful tool that allows you to automate web\nbrowser interactions, essentially teaching your computer to\nact like a robot. It is like having your own personal minion to\ncarry out your tasks, including software testing, allowing\nyou to focus on the more creative and strategic aspects of\nsoftware delivery .\nWhether you are a seasoned tester or just dipping your toes\ninto the world of browser automation, buckle up and get\nready for an adventur e. In this book, we will explore the ins\nand outs of test automation using Selenium. This book is\norganized into 11 chapters, as outlined below :\nChapter 1 acts as an onboar ding ramp to the Selenium\necosystem by covering the introduction to Selenium and\ndetailed hands-on sectio ns with Selenium IDE. This way\nreaders can accelerate their learning journey while staying\nin the low-code mode.\nChapter 2 delves into the basics of Software Quality, Test\nAutomation strategy, and Reporting. Befor e we dive deep\ninto the ocea n of Selenium test automation, it is imperative\nthat we take a holy dip into the world of software testing\nand quality . This ensur es that we do not waste our time\nautomating the wrong tests and can really deliver on the\npromise of testing via automation.\nChapter 3 covers one of the most important topics in test\nautomation вҖ“ Locator strategies. Additionally, readers learn\ninteractions with various web elements (such as clicking\nlinks and buttons), for m submissions, and п¬Ғle uploads.\nChapter 4 explores advanced techniques for using\nSelenium WebDriver . With our newfound grip on Selenium\nWebDriver basics, readers also learn about various methods\nto tackle synchronization and wait strategies. This chapter\nsheds light on Shadow DOM, automation for Animations,\nand design patter ns such as Page Objects and Screenplay\npatter ns.\nChapter 5 acts as the backbon e for our learning\nexperience, as we build out a full-п¬Ӯedged Test Automation\nframework. Here, readers also learn about a very important\ntesting framework вҖ“ TestNG, for adding tests in an\nextensible way to the test automation framework.\nChapter 6 explor es Distributed Test Automation,\nemphasizing the importance of scaling tests via Selenium\nGrid acr oss machines and br owsers.\nChapter 7 provides readers insight into the п¬Ғeld of test\nautomation for Software as A Service (SAAS) applications.\nReaders also learn about automating non-functional tests\nlike Security and Performance, using our beloved tool\nSelenium.\nChapter 8 raises the bar of Selenium test automation with\nBehavior Driven Development (BDD). WouldnвҖҷt it be great if\nnon-technical users could read and execute our Selenium\ntests?
