Way Forward\nIntroduction\nStructur e\nUsage of AI in T est Automation\nPrompt Engineering\nImpact of AI in the п¬Ғeld of T est Automation\nGit Basics\nKey Concepts of Git\nEssential Git Commands and their Usage\nGit Workп¬Ӯow\nOpen-Sour ce Community of Selenium\nTechnical Contributions\nBeyond T ech: Enriching the Community in V aried W ays\nConclusion\nIndex\nCHAPTER 1\nIntroduction to Selenium Test\nAutomation\nIntroduction\nAs of 2023, there are approximately 1.5 billion websites on the world\nwide web, with around 200 million being very active. In this vast and\never-expanding digital landscape, Selenium Test Automation has\nemer ged as an increasingly popular and indispensable tool in the\nrealm of web application  automation. As technolog y continues  to\nevolve and the demand for eп¬ғcient, reliable software intensiп¬Ғes,\nSelenium shines as a crucial resour ce for devel opers and quality\nassurance professionals. This п¬Ғrst chapter serves as your portal to\ncompr ehending how Selenium can transfor m your testing approach,\nenhancing speed, accuracy, and versatility .\nOur journey begins with an introduction to Selenium, highlighting its\nsigniп¬Ғcance and why it has become the go-to choice for automated\ntesting in diverse sectors such as healthcar e, education, and п¬Ғnance.\nThe true elegance of Selenium resides in its capability to emulate\nhuman interactions in a native fashion.\nThen we will discuss Selenium IDE in detail, a browser extension that\nallows users  to record, edit, and replay tests without writing any\ncode. This way we can get a п¬Ӯavor of Selenium, without getting into\ntoo much detail. We will examine its limitations, as well as its\nadvantages, and compar e it with Selenium W ebDriver .\nBy the end of this chapter, you will have a foundation in Selenium\nTest Automation, equipped with the knowledge to start your journey.\nSo, letвҖҷs embark on this exciting trail together and discover how\nSelenium can revolutionize the way you approach software test\nautomation.\nNote:  The statistics at the beginning of this section are inspir ed from\nhttps://www .inter netlivestats.com/total-number -of-websites/ .\nStructure\nIn this chapter, we will discuss the following topics:\nIntroduction to Selenium\nAdvantages of Selenium\nGetting started with Selenium\nSelenium IDE\nIntroduction to Selenium\nSelenium is a browser automation tool that can help in automating\nvarious repetitive tasks such as web crawling, scraping, portal\nadministration and most importantly software testing. The Selenium\nfamily of tools comprises thr ee components:\nSelenium WebDriver : Selenium WebDriver refers to both the\nlanguage bindings and the implementations of the individual\nbrowser -contr olling code. This is commonly referred to as just\nWebDriver . Selenium WebDriver uses a вҖңWebDriver вҖқ interface to\ninteract with web browsers using language-speciп¬Ғc bindings\n(like Java, Python, and .NET). This interface deп¬Ғnes a set of\nmethods to navigate between pages, interact with page\nelements (such as clicking buttons or п¬Ғlling in text п¬Ғelds), and\nperfor m other tasks. One of the key beneп¬Ғts of using Selenium\nWebDriver is that it allows you to write tests in a way that\nclosely resembles how a human user interacts with a website.\nThis makes it easier to write tests that accurately simulate real-\nworld usage scenarios. Overall, Selenium WebDriver is a\npowerful tool for automating web application testing that oп¬Җers\na wide range of capabili ties and п¬Ӯexibility for developers and\ntesters. We will cover  WebDriver in detail in Chapter 3: Selenium\nWebDriver Basics .\nSelenium IDE: Selenium IDE (Integrated Development\nEnvir onment) is a browse r-based tool that allows you to create,\nrecord, and playback automated tests for web applications. It is\nan open-sour ce project that is available as a Firefox plugin and\nas a Chr ome e xtension.\nSelenium IDE provides a user-friendly interface that allows users\nto record interactions with a web application and convert them\ninto automated tests. This is done by recording user\ninteractions, such as clicks and keystr okes, and generating test\nscripts in a for mat called JSON.\nSelenium IDE has several featur es that make it a useful tool for\nautomated testing. For example,  it allows you to add assertions\nto your tests to verify that speciп¬Ғc elements on a web page are\npresent or have certain properties. It also allows  you to deп¬Ғne\nvariables and use п¬Ӯow contr ol statements, making it possible to\nwrite comple x tests.\nOne of the advantages of using Selenium IDE is that it allows\nnon-technical users to create automated tests without having to\nwrite code. However, it also provides an option to export the\nrecorded test script to diп¬Җer ent programming languages, such\nas Java or Python, so that technical users can customize and\nextend the tests as needed.\nOverall, Selenium IDE is a useful tool for creating simple\nautomated tests for web applications. While it may not have the\nsame level of functio nality and п¬Ӯexibility as Selenium\nWebDriver, it can still be a valuable tool for teams  that are just\nstarting with automated testing or for non-technical users who\nneed to cr eate and run tests quickly and easily .\nSelenium GRID : Selenium Grid is a tool that allows you to run\nSelenium tests in parallel across multiple machines and\nbrowsers. It is a server that enables you to distribute your tests\nacross diп¬Җer ent machines, thereby reducing the time requir ed to\nrun the tests and increasing the eп¬ғciency of your testing\nprocess. Selenium Grid works by creating a hub that manages\nthe distribution of tests  to multiple nodes. Each node is a\nmachine that has a speciп¬Ғc conп¬Ғguration, such as a speciп¬Ғc\noperating system, browser version, or screen resolution. By\nadding nodes with diп¬Җer ent conп¬Ғgurations to the grid, you can\ntest your web application across multiple envir onments,\nensuring that it works correctly on all of them. When a test is\nexecuted on the Selenium Grid, it is sent to the hub, which then\nforwar ds it to an availa ble node that matches the desir ed\nconп¬Ғguration. The node then runs the test and sends the results\nback to the hub. This process can be repeated with multiple\nnodes, allowing you to run tests in parallel and signiп¬Ғcantly\nreducing the time requir ed to complete the testing process.\nSelenium Grid supports various programming langu ages such as\nJava, Python, Ruby, and C#. It also supports diп¬Җer ent browsers\nincluding Google Chrome, Mozilla Firefox, Safari, and Edge. If\nyou want to scale by distributing and running tests on several\nmachines and manage multiple envir onments from a central\npoint, making it easy to run the tests against a vast combination\nof browsers/O S, then you want to use Selenium Grid. We will\ncover GRID in detail in Chapter 6: Distributed T est Automation .\nSelenium: The Origin Story\nIn 2004, Jason Huggins  and his team were tasked with п¬Ғlling\ntimesheets at their organization. This timesheet portal had a caveat,\nevery time a new row had to be added, the web application would\nmake a server call increasing the wait time for the user. Jason\nthought of automating the application with JavaScript as he observed\nthe task to be repetitive and manually time-consuming. He wrote a\nsmall utility to accomplish the task and it gained popularity within his\nteam as it was simple to use and easily saved the user time and\neп¬Җort with using the timesheet application. As this utility was gaining\nmomentum, Jason made a jok e mocking a competitor saying that you\ncan cure mercury poisoning by taking selenium supplements. The\nothers that received the email took the name and ran with it. And\nsince then, the pr oject has been called Selenium.\nSelenium has been used for over 20 years and is based on industry\nstandards, backed by Microsoft, Google, Apple, and Mozilla. This\nextensive experience has enabled Selenium to develop a robust\nand п¬Ӯexible tool that can handle the complexitie s of the modern\nweb. This п¬Ӯexibility enables developers to write solid tests that can\nhandle the advanced features of the modern web. Selenium is also\nopen-source, which means that it is free to use and ')

```json
AnnotatedDocument(extractions=[], text=' \n  \n \n \n \n \n \n \n \n \n \n \n \n \n \n \nAuditing S tandards \nof the Public Company Accounting Oversight \nBoard  \n \nThis booklet displays PCAOB au diting standards for audits of financial statements for fiscal years ending \non or after June 15, 2025 through Dec. 14, 2025 (that is, beginning on or after June 16, 2024 through     \nDec. 14, 2024), including amendments related to:  \n \n1) The auditorвҖҷs use of confirmation which are effective for fiscal years ending on or after June 15, \n2025 (that is, for fiscal years beginning on or after June 16, 2024) (PCAOB Release No. 2023 -008, \nSEC Release No. 34 -99060); and  \n2) The supervision of audits involving other auditors and dividing responsibility for the audit with \nanother accounting firm which are effective for fiscal years ending on or after December 15, \n2024 (that is, for fiscal years beginning on or after December 16,  2023) (PCAOB Release No. \n2022 -002, SEC Release No. 34 -95488).  \n \nThis booklet was prepared by staff of the Office of the Chief Auditor from the auditing standards on the \nPCAOB\'s website. In the event of typographical or other technical errors in the standards presented in \nthis document, the rule text that the PCAOB Boar d adopted, and the Securities and Exchange \nCommission ("SEC") approved as presented in the relevant SEC rule release, shall govern.  \n \nAuditing Standards\nAuditing Standards  \nThe Sarbanes-Oxley Act of 2002, as amended, directs the Board to establish, by rule, auditing and related  \nprofessional practice standards for registered public accounting firms to follow in the preparation of audit\nreports for public companies and other issuers, and broker-dealers.  \nThe following is a list of PCAOB auditing standards for audits of financial statements for fiscal years ending  \non or after June 15, 2025 (that is, beginning on or after June 16, 2024). Downloadable PDF booklets of the\nauditing standards that are effective for audits of that and other periods are also available:  \nPCAOB auditing standards for audits of financial statements for fiscal years ending on Dec. 15,  \n2024 through Jun. 14, 2025  \nPCAOB auditing standards for audits of financial statements for fiscal years ending on Dec. 15,  \n2020 through Dec. 14, 2024  \nPCAOB auditing standards for audits of financial statements for fiscal years ending Dec. 15, 2017  \nthrough Dec. 14, 2020  \nPCAOB auditing standards, as reorganized beginning Dec. 31, 2016, for audits of financial  \nstatements for fiscal years ending before Dec. 15, 2017  \nFor periods not listed above, applicable auditing standards are available in the \narchive\n.\nFind analogous auditing standards of the ASB and the IAASB using the \nFind an Analogous Standard\nreference tool.\nGeneral Auditing Standards\n1000 General Principles and Responsibilities\nAS 1001: Responsibilities and Functions of Responses to the Risks of Material Misstatement\nFor FYE on and after 6/15/2025-12/14/2025\n(that is, fiscal years beginning on and after 6/16/2024-12/14/2024)\n3\nAuditing Standards\nAS 2305: Substantive Analytical Procedures\nAS 2310: The AuditorвҖҷs Use of Confirmation\nAS 2315: Audit Sampling\n2400 Audit Procedures for Specific Aspects of the Audit\nAS 2401: Consideration of Fraud in a Accompanying Audited Financial Statements\nAS 2705: Required Supplementary Information\nAS 2710: Other Information in Documents Containing Audited Financial Statements\n2800 Concluding Audit Procedures\nFor FYE on and after 6/15/2025-12/14/2025\n(that is, fiscal years beginning on and after 6/16/2024-12/14/2024)\n4\nAuditing Standards\nAS 2801: Subsequent Events\nAS 2805: Management Whether a Previously Reported Material Weakness Continues to Exist\nFor FYE on and after 6/15/2025-12/14/2025\n(that is, fiscal years beginning on and after 6/16/2024-12/14/2024)\n6\nAS 1001: Responsibilities and Functions of the Independent Auditor\nAS 1001: Responsibilities and Functions of the\nIndependent Auditor\n \nAmendments: \nAmending releases and related SEC approval orders\n.02 \n \nDistinction Between Responsibilities of Auditor and Management\n.04 \n \nProfessional Qualifications\n.10 \n \nResponsibility to the Profession\n.01\n        \nThe objective of the ordinary audit of financial statements by the independent auditor is the\nexpression of an opinion on the fairness with which they present, in all material respects, financial\nposition, results of operations, and its cash flows in conformity with generally accepted accounting\nprinciples. The auditor\'s report is the medium through which he expresses his opinion or, if circumstances\nrequire, disclaims an opinion. In either case, he states whether his audit has been made in accordance with\nthe standards of the PCAOB. These standards require him to state whether, in his opinion, the financial\nstatements are presented in conformity with generally accepted accounting principles and to identify\nthose circumstances in which such principles have not been consistently observed in the preparation of\nthe financial statements of the current period in relation to those of\n \nthe preceding period.\nDistinction Between Responsibilities of Auditor and\nManagement\n.02        The auditor has a responsibility to plan and perform the audit to obtain reasonable assurance \nabout whether the financial statements are free of material misstatement, whether caused by error or\nfraud.\n Because of the nature of audit evidence and the characteristics of fraud, the auditor is able to\nobtain reasonable, but not absolute, assurance that material misstatements are detected.\n The auditor\nhas no responsibility to plan and perform the audit to obtain reasonable assurance that misstatements,\nwhether caused by errors or fraud, that are not material to the financial statements are detected.\n \n.03\n        \nThe financial statements are management\'s responsibility. The auditor\'s responsibility is to express\nan opinion on the financial statements. Management is responsible for adopting sound accounting policies\nand for establishing and maintaining internal control that will, among other things, initiate, record,Summary Table of Contents\n21\nFor FYE on and after 6/15/2025-12/14/2025\n(that is, fiscal years beginning on and after 6/16/2024-12/14/2024)\n7\nAS 1001: Responsibilities and Functions of the Independent Auditor\nprocess, and report transactions (as well as events and conditions) consistent with management\'s\nassertions embodied in the financial statements. The entity\'s transactions and the related assets, liabilities,\nand equity are within the direct knowledge and control of management. The auditor\'s knowledge of these\nmatters and internal control is limited to that acquired through the audit. Thus, the fair presentation of\nfinancial statements in conformity with generally accepted accounting principles\n is an implicit and\nintegral part of management\'s responsibility. The independent auditor may make suggestions about the\nform or content of the financial statements or draft them, in whole or in part, based on information from\nmanagement during the performance of the audit. However, the auditor\'s responsibility for the financial\nstatements he or she has audited is confined to the expression of his or her opinion on them.\n \nProfessional Qualifications\n.04        The professional qualifications required of the independent auditor are those of a person with the \neducation and experience to practice as such. They do not include those of a person trained for or\nqualified to engage in another profession or occupation. For example, the independent auditor, in\nobserving the taking of a physical inventory, does not purport to act as an appraiser, a valuer, or an expert\nin materials. Similarly, although the independent auditor is informed in a general manner about matters of\ncommercial law, he does not purport to act in the capacity of a lawyer and may appropriately rely upon\nthe advice of attorneys in all matters of law.\n \n.05\n        \nIn the observance of the standards of the PCAOB, the independent auditor must exercise his\njudgment in determining which auditing procedures are necessary in the circumstances to afford a\nreasonable basis for his opinion. His judgment is required to be the informed judgment of a qualified\nprofessional person.\n \nDetection of Fraud\n[.06-.09]         [Paragraphs deleted.]\nResponsibility to the Profession\n[.10]         [Paragraph deleted.] \n.11 \nThe auditor should be aware of and consider auditing interpretations applicable to his or her audit.\nIf the auditor does not apply the auditing guidance included in an applicable auditing interpretation, the\nauditor should be prepared to explain how he or she complied with the provisions of the auditing standard\naddressed by such auditing guidance.\nNote: The term "auditing interpretations," as used in this paragraph, refers to the publications\nentitled "Auditing Interpretation" issued by the American Institute of Certified Public Accountants\'\nAuditing Standards Board as in existence on April 16, 2003, and in effect.3\nFor FYE on and after 6/15/2025-12/14/2025\n(that is, fiscal years beginning on and after 6/16/2024-12/14/2024)\n8\nAS 1001: Responsibilities and Functions of the Independent Auditor\nFootnotes (AS 1001 -  Responsibilities and Functions of the Independent Auditor):\n See AS 2105, Consideration of Materiality in Planning and Performing an Audit . The auditor\'s \nconsideration of illegal acts and responsibility for detecting misstatements resulting from illegal acts is\ndefined in AS 2405, \nIllegal Acts by Clients\n. For those illegal acts that are defined in that section as having\na direct and material effect on the determination of financial statement amounts, the auditor\'s\nresponsibility to detect misstatements resulting from such illegal acts is the same as that for error or\nfraud.\n \nSee\n paragraphs .10 through .13 of AS 1015, \nDue Professional Care in the Performance of Work\n.\n \n The responsibilities and functions of the independent auditor are also applicable to financial\nstatements presented in conformity with a comprehensive basis of accounting other than generally\naccepted accounting principles; references in this section to financial statements presented in\nconformity with generally accepted accounting principles also include those presentations.\n \nCopyright В© 2002, American Institute of Certified Public Accountants, Inc.1\n2\n3\nFor FYE on and after 6/15/2025-12/14/2025\n(that is, fiscal years beginning on and after 6/16/2024-12/14/2024)\n9\nAS 1005: Independence\nAS 1005: Independence\nAmendments: \nAmending releases and related SEC approval orders\n \n.01\n   \n     \n In a\nll matters relating to the assignment, an independence in mental attitude is to be maintained\nby the auditor or auditors.\n.02\n        \n The statement in the preceding paragraph requires that the auditor be independent; aside from\nbeing in public practice (as distinct from being in private practice), he must be without bias with respect to\nthe client since otherwise he would lack that impartiality necessary for the dependability of his findings,\nhowever excellent his technical proficiency may be. However, independence does not imply the attitude of\na prosecutor but rather a judicial impartiality that recognizes an obligation for fairness not only to\nmanagement and owners of a business but also to creditors and those who may otherwise rely (in part, at\nleast) upon the independent auditor\'s report, as in the case of prospective owners or creditors.\n.03\n        \nIt is of utmost importance to the profession that the general public maintain confidence in the\nindependence of independent auditors. Public confidence would be impaired by evidence that\nindependence was actually lacking, and it might also be impaired by the existence of circumstances which\nreasonable people might believe likely to influence independence. To \nbe\n independent, the auditor must be\nintellectually honest; to be \nrecognized\n as independent, he must be free from any obligation to or interest\nin the client, its management, or its owners. For example, an independent auditor auditing a company of\nwhich he was also a director might be intellectually honest, but it is unlikely that the public would accept\nhim as independent since he would be in effect auditing decisions which he had a part in making. Likewise,\nan auditor with a substantial financial interest in a company might be unbiased in expressing his opinion\non the financial statements of the company, but the public would be reluctant to believe that he was\nunbiased. Independent auditors should not only be independent in fact; they should avoid situations that\nmay lead outsiders to doubt their independence.\n.04\n        \nThe profession has established, through the AICPA\'s Code of Professional Conduct, precepts to\nguard against the \npresumption\n of loss of independence. "Presumption" is stressed because the possession\nof intrinsic independence is a matter of personal quality rather than of rules that formulate certain\nobjective tests. Insofar as these precepts have been incorporated in the profession\'s code, they have the\nforce of professional law for the independent auditor.\n.05\n        \nThe Securities and Exchange Commission (SEC) has also adopted requirements for independence\nof auditors who report on financial statements filed with it.\n.06\n        \nThe independent auditor should administer his practice within the spirit of these precepts and\nrules if he is to achieve a proper degree of independence in the conduct of his work.\n.07\n        \nTo emphasize independence from management, many corporations follow the practice of having\nFor FYE on and after 6/15/2025-12/14/2025\n(that is, fiscal years beginning on and after 6/16/2024-12/14/2024)\n10\nAS 1005: Independence\nthe independent auditor appointed by the board of directors or elected by the stockholders.\nCopyright В© 2002, American Institute of Certified Public Accountants, Inc.\nFor FYE on and after 6/15/2025-12/14/2025\n(that is, fiscal years beginning on and after 6/16/2024-12/14/2024)\n11\nAS 1010: Training and Proficiency of the Independent Auditor\nAS 1010: Training and Proficiency of the Independent\nAuditor\nAmendments: \nAmending releases and related SEC approval orders\n.01\n         \nThe audit is to be performed by a person or persons having adequate technical training and\nproficiency as an auditor.\n.02\n         \n The statement in the preceding paragraph recognizes that however capable a person may be in\nother fields, including business and finance, he cannot meet the requirements of the auditing standards\nwithout proper education and experience in the field of auditing.\n.03\n        \nIn the performance of the audit which leads to an opinion, the independent auditor holds himself\nout as one who is proficient in accounting and auditing. The attainment of that proficiency begins with the\nauditor\'s formal education and extends into his subsequent experience. The independent auditor must\nundergo training adequate to meet the requirements of a professional. This training must be adequate in\ntechnical scope and should include a commensurate measure of general education. The junior assistant,\njust entering upon an auditing career, must obtain his professional experience with the proper supervision\nand review of his work by a more experienced superior. The nature and extent of supervision and review\nmust necessarily reflect wide variances in practice. The engagement partner must exercise seasoned\njudgment in the varying degrees of his supervision and review of the work done and judgments exercised\nby his subordinates, who in turn must meet the responsibilities attaching to the varying gradations and\nfunctions of their work.\n.04\n        \nThe independent auditor\'s formal education and professional experience complement one\nanother; each auditor exercising authority upon an engagement should weigh these attributes in\ndetermining the extent of his supervision of subordinates and review of their work. It should be recognized\nthat the training of a professional man includes a continual awareness of developments taking place in\nbusiness and in his profession. He must study, understand, and apply new pronouncements on accounting\nprinciples and auditing procedures as they are developed by authoritative bodies within the accounting\nprofession.\n.05\n        \nIn the course of his day-to-day practice, the independent auditor encounters a wide range of\njudgment on the part of management, varying from true objective judgment to the occasional extreme of\ndeliberate misstatement. He is retained to audit and report upon the financial statements of a business\nbecause, through his training and experience, he has become skilled in accounting and auditing and has\nacquired the ability to consider objectively and to exercise independent judgment with respect to the\ninformation recorded in books of account or otherwise disclosed by his audit.\n \nCopyright В© 2002, American Institute of Certified Public Accountants, Inc.\nFor FYE on and after 6/15/2025-12/14/2025\n(that is, fiscal years beginning on and after 6/16/2024-12/14/2024)\n12\nAS 1015: Due Professional Care in the Performance of Work\nAS 1015: Due Professional Care in the Performance\nof Work\n \nAmendments: \nAmending releases and related SEC approval orders\nGuidance on AS 1015:\n \nStaff Audit Practice Alerts\n \n \nNo. 9\n \n \nand\n \n \nNo. 10\n.07\n  \nProfessional Skepticism\n.10\n  \nReasonable Assurance\n.01\n       \nDue professional care is to be exercised in the planning and performance of the audit and the\npreparation of the report.\nNote: For audits that involve other auditors, the other auditors are responsible for performing their work\nwith due professional care.\n.02\n       \nThe statement in the preceding paragraph requires the independent auditor to plan and perform\nhis or her work with due professional care. Due professional care imposes a responsibility upon each\nprofessional within an independent auditor\'s organization to observe the standards of field work and\nreporting.\n.03\n       \nCooley on Torts\n, a legal treatise, describes the obligation for due care as follows:\nEvery man who offers his services to another and is employed assumes the duty to exercise in the\nemployment such skill as he possesses with reasonable care and diligence. In all these employments\nwhere peculiar skill is requisite, if one offers his\n \nservices, he is understood as holding himself out to\nthe public as possessing the degree of skill commonly possessed by others in the same\nemployment, and if his pretentions are unfounded, he commits a species of fraud upon every man\nwho employs\n \nhim in reliance on his public profession. But no man, whether skilled or unskilled,\nundertakes that the task he assumes shall be performed successfully, and without fault or error; he\nundertakes for good faith and integrity, but not for infallibility,\n \nand he is liable to his employer for\nnegligence, bad faith, or dishonesty, but not for losses consequent upon pure errors of judgment.\n.04\n       \nThe matter of due professional care concerns what the independent auditor does and how well he\nor she does it. The quotation from \nCooley on Torts\n \nprovides a source from which an auditor\'s responsibilitySummary Table of Contents\n21\nFor FYE on and after 6/15/2025-12/14/2025\n(that is, fiscal years beginning on and after 6/16/2024-12/14/2024)\n13\nAS 1015: Due Professional Care in the Performance of Work\nfor conducting an audit with due professional care can be derived. The remainder of the section discusses\nthe auditor\'s responsibility in the context of an audit.\n.05\n       \nAn auditor should possess "the degree of skill commonly possessed" by other auditors and should\nexercise it with "reasonable care and diligence" (that is, with due professional care).\n.06\n       \nEngagement team\n members should be assigned to tasks and supervised commensurate with their\nlevel of knowledge, skill, and ability so that they can evaluate the audit evidence they are examining. The\nengagement partner should know, at a minimum, the relevant professional accounting and auditing\nstandards and should be knowledgeable about the client. The engagement partner is responsible for the\nassignment of tasks to, and supervision of, the members of the engagement team.\nProfessional Skepticism  \n.07       Due professional care requires the auditor to exercise professional skepticism . Professional \nskepticism is an attitude that includes a questioning mind and a critical assessment of audit evidence.\n \nThe\nauditor uses the knowledge, skill, and ability called for by the profession of public accounting to diligently\nperform, in good faith and with integrity, the gathering and objective evaluation of evidence.\n.08\n       \nGathering and objectively evaluating audit evidence requires the auditor to consider the\ncompetency and sufficiency of the evidence. Since evidence is gathered and evaluated throughout the\naudit, professional\n \nskepticism should be exercised throughout the audit process.\n \n.09\n       \nThe auditor neither assumes that management is dishonest nor assumes unquestioned honesty. In\nexercising professional skepticism, the auditor should not be satisfied with less than persuasive evidence\nbecause\n \nof a belief that management is honest.\n \nReasonable Assurance  \n.10       The exercise of due professional care allows the auditor to obtain reasonable assurance  about \nwhether the financial statements are free of material misstatement, whether caused by error or fraud, or\nwhether any material weaknesses exist as of the date of management\'s assessment. Absolute assurance is\nnot attainable because of the nature of audit evidence and the characteristics of fraud. Although not\nabsolute assurance, reasonable assurance is\n \na high level of assurance. Therefore, an audit conducted in\naccordance with the standards of the Public Company Accounting Oversight Board (United States) may not\ndetect a material weakness in internal control over financial reporting or a material\n \nmisstatement to the\nfinancial statements.\n.11\n       \nThe independent auditor\'s objective is to obtain sufficient appropriate evidential matter to provide\nhim or her with a reasonable basis for forming an opinion. The nature of most evidence derives, in part,\nfrom the concept of selective testing of the data being audited, which involves judgment regarding both\nthe areas to be tested and the nature, timing, and extent of the tests to be performed. In addition,\njudgment is required in interpreting the results\n \nof audit testing and evaluating audit evidence. Even with43\nFor FYE on and after 6/15/2025-12/14/2025\n(that is, fiscal years beginning on and after 6/16/2024-12/14/2024)\n14\nAS 1015: Due Professional Care in the Performance of Work\ngood faith and integrity, mistakes and errors in judgment can be made. Furthermore, many accounting\npresentations contain accounting estimates, the measurement of which is inherently uncertain  and \ndepends on the outcome of future events. The auditor exercises professional judgment in evaluating the\nreasonableness of accounting estimates in significant accounts and disclosures based on information that\ncould reasonably be expected to be\n \navailable through the date of the auditor\'s report.\n As a result of\nthese factors, in the great majority of cases, the auditor has to rely on evidence that is persuasive rather\nthan convincing.\n.12\n       \nBecause of the characteristics of fraud, a properly planned and performed audit may not detect a\nmaterial misstatement. Characteristics of fraud include (\na\n) concealment through collusion among\nmanagement,\n \nemployees, or third parties; (\nb\n) withheld, misrepresented, or falsified documentation; and\n(\nc\n) the ability of management to override or instruct others to override what otherwise appears to be\neffective controls. For example, auditing\n \nprocedures may be ineffective for detecting an intentional\nmisstatement that is concealed through collusion among personnel within the entity and third parties or\namong management or employees of the entity. Collusion may cause the auditor who has\n \nproperly\nperformed the audit to conclude that evidence provided is persuasive when it is, in fact, false. In addition,\nan audit conducted in accordance with the standards of the PCAOB rarely involves authentication of\ndocumentation, nor are auditors\n \ntrained as or expected to be experts in such authentication. (\nSee\nparagraph .09 of AS 1105, \nAudit Evidence\n.) Furthermore, an auditor may not discover the existence of a\nmodification of documentation through a side agreement that\n \nmanagement or a third party has not\ndisclosed. Finally, management has the ability to directly or indirectly manipulate accounting records and\npresent fraudulent financial information by overriding controls in unpredictable ways.\n.13\n       \nSince the auditor\'s opinion on the financial statements or internal control over financial reporting is\nbased on the concept of obtaining reasonable assurance, the auditor is not an insurer and his or her report\ndoes not constitute a guarantee. Therefore, the subsequent discovery that either a material misstatement,\nwhether from error or fraud, exists in the financial statements or a material weakness in internal control\nover financial reporting exists does\n \nnot, in and of itself, evidence (a) failure to obtain reasonable\nassurance, (b) inadequate planning, performance, or judgment, (c) the absence of due professional care,\nor (d) a failure to comply with the standards of the Public Company Accounting\n \nOversight Board (United\nStates).\nFootnotes (AS 1015 - Due Professional Care in the Performance of Work):\nThe lead auditorвҖҷs responsibilities for planning the audit and supervising the other auditorsвҖҷ work are\nset forth in AS 2101, \nAudit \nPlanning\n, and AS 1201, \nSupervision\n \nof the Audit Engagement\n. The terms вҖңlead\nauditorвҖқ and вҖңother auditor,вҖқ as used in this standard, have the same meaning as defined in Appendix A\nof AS 2101.\nD. Haggard,\n Cooley on Torts\n, 472 (4th ed., 1932).\nThe term вҖңengagement team,вҖқ as used in this standard, has the same meaning as defined in Appendix\nA of AS 2101.1\n2\n35\nFor FYE on and after 6/15/2025-12/14/2025\n(that is, fiscal years beginning on and after 6/16/2024-12/14/2024)\n15\nAS 1015: Due Professional Care in the Performance of Work\nSee AS 1201.\nSee\n \nAS 2501, \nAuditing Accounting Estimates, Including Fair Value Measurements\n, which discusse')
```

```json
AnnotatedDocument(extractions=[], text='Cover image:  Emrah Turudu /  \nGetty ImagesSeries Editor\nStuart Soroka \nUniversity of \nCaliforniaAbout the Series\nCambridge Elements in Politics and \nCommunication publishes research \nfocused on the intersection of media, \ntechnology, and politics. The series \nemphasizes forward-looking reviews  \nof the field, path-breaking theoretical  \nand methodological innovations,  \nand the timely application of social- \nscientific theory and methods to  \ncurrent developments in politics and \ncommunication around the world.To comeThe YouTube Apparatus Mung Er\nISSN 2633-9897 (online)\nISSN 2633-9889 (print)\nKevin Munger\nThe YouTube \nApparatus\nPolitics and  \nCommunication\n\nElements in Politics and Communication\nedited by\nStuart Soroka\nUniversity of California\nTHE YOUTUBE APPARATUS\nKevin Munger\nPenn State University\n\nShaftesbury Road, Cambridge CB2 8EA, United Kingdom\nOne Liberty Plaza, 20th Floor, New York, NY 10006, USA\n477 Williamstown Road, Port Melbourne, VIC 3207, Australia\n314вҖ“321, 3rd Floor, Plot 3, Splendor Forum, Jasola District Centre,\nNew Delhi вҖ“ 110025, India\n103 Penang Road, #05вҖ“06/07, Visioncrest Commercial, Singapore 238467\nCambridge University Press is part of Cambridge University Press & Assessment,\na department of the University of Cambridge.\nWe share the UniversityвҖҷs mission to contribute to society through the pursuit of\neducation, learning and research at the highest international levels of excellence.\nwww.cambridge.org\nInformation on this title: www.cambridge.org/9781009486002\nDOI: 10.1017/9781009359795\nВ© Kevin Munger 2024\nThis publication is in copyright. Subject to statutory exception and to the provisions\nof relevant collective licensing agreements, with the exception of the Creative\nCommons version the link for which is provided below, no reproduction of any part may\ntake place without the written permission of Cambridge University Press & Assessment.\nAn online version of this work is published at doi.org/10.1017/9781009359795\nunder a Creative Commons Open Access license CC-BY-NC 4.0 which permits\nre-use, distribution and reproduction in any medium for non-commercial purposes\nproviding appropriate credit to the original work is given and any changes made\nare indicated. To view a copy of this license visit\nhttps://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/4.0\nWhen citing this work, please include a reference to the DOI 10.1017/9781009359795\nFirst published 2024\nA catalogue record for this publication is available from the British Library.\nISBN 978-1-009-48600-2 Hardback\nISBN 978-1-009-35975-7 Paperback\nISSN 2633-9897 (online)\nISSN 2633-9889 (print)\nCambridge University Press & Assessment has no responsibility for the persistence\nor accuracy of URLs for external or third-party internet websites referred to in this\npublication and does not guarantee that any content on such websites is, or will\nremain, accurate or appropriate.\nThe Y ouTube Apparatus\nElements in Politics and Communication\nDOI: 10.1017/9781009359795\nFirst published online: May 2024\nKevin Munger\nPenn State University\nAuthor for correspondence: Kevin Munger, kevinmunger@gmail.com\nAbstract: The academic agenda for studying social media and politics has\nbeen somewhat haphazard. Thanks to rapid technological change, a\ncascade of policy-relevant crises, and sheer scale, we do not have a\ncoherent framework for deciding what questions to ask. This Element\narticulates such a framework by taking existing literature from media\neconomics and sociology and applying it reflexively, to both the academic\nagenda and to the specific case of politics on YouTube: the Supply and\nDemand Framework. The key mechanism, traced over the past century, is\nthe technology of audience measurement. The YouTube audience comes\npre-rationalized in the form of Likes, Views and Comments, and is thus\nunavoidable for all actors involved. The phenomenon of вҖқradicalizationвҖқ is\nbest understood as a consequence of accelerated feedback between\naudiences and creators, radicalizing each other. I use fifteen years of\nsupply and demand data from YouTube to demonstrate how different\ntypes of producers respond more or less to this feedback, which in turn\nstructures the ideological distribution of content consumed on the\nplatform. This title is also available as Open Access on Cambridge Core.\nKeywords: YouTube, supply and demand, quantitative description, academic\nagenda-setting, YouTube is other people\nВ© Kevin Munger 2024\nISBNs: 9781009486002 (HB), 9781009359757 (PB), 9781009359795 (OC)\nISSNs: 2633-9897 (online), 2633-9889 (print)\nContents\n1Foreword 1\n2 Setting the Academic Agenda 2\n3 There Is Something Wrong on the Internet 7\n4 Media Panics within the Supply and Demand Framework 13\n5 The Apparatus 19\n6 YouTube System 22\n7 Supply System 33\n8 Demand System 42\n9 Describing YouTube Politics 46\n10 The Academic Agenda for Studying Social Media 63\n11 Self-Indulgent Postscript: Poetry as Social Science\nMethodology 68\nReferences 74\nThe YouTube Apparatus 1\n1 Foreword\nThis Element is structured as an extended essay intended to be read sequentially\nbut divided into sections that can be read separately. The first three sections\nclear the ground for the analysis that follows, and are necessary to justify my\napproach. The next four sections introduce my Supply and Demand Framework\nand draw connections between relevant literatures and the distinct systems\nwhich are part of what I call the YouTube Apparatus. The following section\npresents my empirical evidence, the final section my suggestions for future\nresearch.\nMy substantive thesis is that demand creates its own supply: The automat-\nically measured and quantified audience causes YouTube creators to adapt\ntheir offerings. My empirical strategy is to classify a large sample of politi-\ncal YouTube channels as either affiliated with some larger media entity or as\nindependent вҖ“ the latter are the classic вҖңYouTube creators,вҖқ whose personalistic\nstyle and intense audience cultivation engenders para-social relationships with\ntheir viewers. With over fifteen years of data, I demonstrate that this distinction\nbetween affiliated channels and independent YouTubers results in significant\nsystem-level differences in a variety of platform-defined audience metrics.\nMy meta-scientific thesis, however, is that dry science-y prose like the pre-\nceding paragraph is the final but perhaps least important step in the study of\nsocial media. Setting the academic agenda is paramount; our answers can only\nbe as good as our questions. So I begin by applying theories of media agenda\nsetting reflexively, in order to understand why such disproportionate effort has\nbeen applied to the relatively narrow question of the effect of the YouTube\nrecommendation algorithm on viewersвҖҷ political beliefs.\nIt is not yet possible to bring comprehensive data to bear on this meta-\nscientific question. My hope is that beginning to theorize about setting the\nacademic agenda will inspire more systematic investigation. But this area of\ninquiry is distinct from our normal science work: When we study ourselves, we\nimmediately change ourselves. So for this process to yield benefits in our study\nof others, we need some sense of our goals, of what we are trying to achieve. It\nis both untenable and irresponsible to maintain a distinction between positive\nand normative meta-science.\nMy goal, then, is to move the academic agenda for studying social media\nin the direction of my Supply and Demand Framework. Compared to the sta-\ntus quo, this means more attention to the production of content, to the forces\nthat shape the way in which people decide to post. More broadly, my goal is to\nalign the study of social media with what social media is . This is why I study\n2 Politics and Communication\nYouTube, the platform for which the ratio of academic attention to social and\npolitical importance seems to me to be the most out of whack. Methodologi-\ncally, I eschew causal inference in favor of pure quantitative description, the\nmost important tool for understanding what andhow much social media is.\nAs I argue in the final section, another underappreciated method for set-\nting the academic agenda is poetry. The creation and circulation of resonant\nmetaphors carves up the conceptual space in ways that imply certain research\nquestions (and therefore also the answers generated by rigorous normal sci-\nence). So while the reader may or may not find compelling the poetic flourishes\nthroughout, I hope you understand why I feel they are necessary.\nTo restate my substantive conclusion in the words of media theorist VilГ©m\nFlusser: вҖңThose who participate actively in the production of information вҖҰ are\nthemselves being programmed by the mass-media meat choppers for informa-\ntion production.вҖқ\nOr more simply, YouTubers are not вҖңCreatorsвҖқ but Creations . Audiences,\nrationalized by the platform, and the vloggers who upload the videos those audi-\nences consumer are not separable either theoretically or empirically. Together,\nthey make up the YouTube Apparatus.\n2 Setting the Academic Agenda\nTo date, research on social media and politics has been somewhat haphazard.\nBecause of the speed of technological change, the sheer scale of our object\nof inquiry, the necessity of disciplinary and methodological pluralism, and a\ncascade of policy-relevant crises, our epistemic community is not yet mature.\nPerhaps this is for the best; some вҖңmatureвҖқ areas of social science are facing\na variety of crises grappling with methodological revolutions, and the creation\nof new subfields is an integral part of the scientific process.\nThis generally congenial anarchy has some drawbacks, however. The chain\nof academic knowledge production is only as strong as its weakest link, and\nour first link is the weakest. There is a lack of rigor allocated to the selection\nofquestions we ask.\nThere is a meta-irony here. In this Element, I will argue in favor of a Supply\nand Demand Framework for studying social media, building on the literature\nin media economics and how these models have understood previous media-\ntechnological regimes. I will advocate for the use of these models in the study\nof both the contemporary media environment and the вҖңmedia systemsвҖқ at the\nheart of a variety of social media platforms.\nThe YouTube Apparatus 3\nAnd I will also, in passing, use these models reflexively to explain how our\nsociotechnical environment has affected the process by which the academic\nagenda is set.\nFor example, the theory of вҖңmedia stormsвҖқ developed in Boydstun (2013 )\nfalls under what I call the вҖңindustrial organizationвҖқ of news media. This means\ncentering the physical aspect of the logistical supply chain required for news\nproduction, rather than abstracting away from temporality and distance. This\napproach yields the insight that news is unexpectedly вҖңstickyвҖқ: The fixed costs\ninvolved in paying for reportersвҖҷ and producersвҖҷ travel reduce the speed at\nwhich news organizations can switch from one story to another. Furthermore,\nthe physical proximity of news teams to a story makes it more likely that they\nuncover unexpected aspects or follow-ups to that story. Boydstun, Hardy, and\nWalgrave (2014 ) argues that вҖңimitation plays a big role in determining the dura-\ntion of a storm; until another hot item hits, news outlets are loathe to be the first\nto drop coverage of an ongoing storm, even if the event or issue itself has run\nits courseвҖқ (p513).\nLike all models, this model should not be trivially applied beyond the context\nit was developed to explain. To understand вҖңacademic storms,вҖқ we must use our\nsubstantive (qualitative and quantitative) knowledge to adjust the parameters\nBoydstun identifies as important. Two such parameters strike me. The audience\nfor academic journal articles, and the physical and temporal reality confronted\nby the authors of journal articles, is different than that of general news media\nand reporters, respectively. The psychological aspect of the drive for imitation,\na key part of human nature, remains the same across contexts.\nA related example comes from Usher (2014 ), an ethnographic investigation\nof the status games within the New York Times . I call this work вҖңeconomic\nsociology,вҖқ a crucial refinement of the lived experience of a class of news pro-\nducers that provides a more realistic picture of the incentives and pressures\nthey perceive as most important. She describes the status competition among\nveteran journalists not to maximize online news sharing or some other immedi-\nately relevant economic metric, but to get their bylines on the front page of the\nprint edition. While the baseline approach from Economics would be to pro-\nceed with standard assumptions about the agentsвҖҷ utility functions, this more\nsociological approach begins by actually understanding the day-to-day experi-\nence of those actors вҖ“ undeniably a more accurate micro-foundation, but one\nwhich is necessarily more difficult to generalize from.\nWithout putting too fine a point on it, we likely all appreciate the distance\nbetween the nominal goals of academics to generate knowledge or to inform\n4 Politics and Communication\npolicy debates and the daily experience of academics embedded in our own\nkind of status competition.\nMatt HindmanвҖҷs work provides a final example, as well as quantitative\nmodels that directly inspire some of the analysis in this Element. Hindman\n(2008 ) and Hindman (2018 ) both argue against two of the fundamental myths\nof contemporary political communication: that the lack of physical frictions\nfrom previous media regimes means that the online media landscape is more\ndemocratic and that it can change overnight .\nRather than analyzing individual actorsвҖҷ preferences, incentives, and infor-\nmation flows, HindmanвҖҷs macro-level approach makes claims about the system\nas a whole. In a memorable example, Hindman (2018 ) argues that Trump-era\nFederal Communications Commissioner Ajit Pai is incorrect that the contem-\nporary digital news environment requires fewer regulations because вҖңwe get\nnews and analysis throughout the day from countless national and local web-\nsites, podcasts and social media outletsвҖқ: вҖңit is flatly not true that there are\nвҖҳcountlessвҖҷ local digital news outlets. We know, because we counted them вҖқ\n(p131). HindmanвҖҷs analysis also demonstrates that the forces of concentration\nin digital media outweigh the forces of democratization, and that advantages to\nscale tend to compound over time, further reinforcing the advantage of estab-\nlished incumbents. Although the technical capacity to broadcast information\nhas indeed been spread more evenly by digital media, the reality of attention\nas the scarce resource has tended to dominate.\nWithin academic research, everyone is now technically able to share their\nresearch freely and to anyone in the world. Consonant with HindmanвҖҷs theory,\nhowever, the use of Twitter to share published research in Political Science\ntends to reinforce rather than undercut existing status hierarchies ( Bisbee, Lar-\nson, and Munger ,2022 ). And despite the revolutionary potential of online\nmodels for academic publishing, peer review at top journals is grinding to a\nhalt as their gatekeeping function has become even more entrenched.\nAlthough I have laid out the applications of these models and the parameters\nI believe have shifted between the news media and scholarship, I expect that my\nreaders bring a wealth of their own knowledge to this area of meta-scientific\ninquiry. I have ideas about how the academic agenda is set now and how it\nmight be set differently, some of which I lay out in the following section.\nMy primary goal, however, is to put the question of academic agenda setting\non the academic agenda , to argue that this is an important link in the chain of\nknowledge creation, and to suggest the Supply and Demand Framework as a\nuseful first step.\nI am far from the first to make such a claim. Indeed, I draw heavily on Bennett\nand Iyengar (2008 ), a high-profile and influential effort to reset the academic\nThe YouTube Apparatus 5\nagenda in light of вҖңthe changing foundations of political communication.вҖқ They\nargue that paradigms developed by the pioneers of the discipline may no longer\nbe relevant, specifically citing вҖңagenda settingвҖқ as a broadcast-era theory that\nhas outlived its relevance.\nMuch of the ensuing debate rested on their provocative title claim of a return\nto the era of вҖңminimal effects,вҖқ but this is not my focus. Ironically, debating the\nvalidity of old paradigms merely serves to reinforce their relevance: Agenda\nsetting, after all, tells us that the media (here, academic journal articles) is\neffective at telling us what to think about.\nSo I agree with Bennett and Iyengar (2008 ) that the shadow of Theory in\na dynamic world can obscure social science practice. They predicted вҖңanother\ntime of unsettled findings accompanied by the risk of undertheorized sociotech-\nnological conditionsвҖқ (p708), a prediction which I believe has been borne out.\nI will pick up on several trends they identify as newly important in the cur-\nrent media environment, an environment that has changed faster and more\ndramatically than most scholars (and indeed, most humans) thought possible.\nIn the broadest possible strokes, the вҖңbroadcast paradigmвҖқ is primarily\nfocused on the following relationship:\nProducer вҶ’MediaвҶ’Consumer\nThere are a fantastic array of refinements that have made this a fruitful area\nof research, but this relationship is the core of the previous era.\nBennett and Iyengar (2008 ) argue that the coming era, our era, will be pri-\nmarily characterized by a rise in the importance of the exact inverse of this\nrelationship:\nConsumer вҶ’Feedback вҶ’Producer\nand the following prediction by Chaffee and Metzger (2001 ) seems even\nmore prescient than it did in 2008: вҖңThe key problem for agenda setting theory\nwill change from what issues the media tell people to think about to what issues\npeople tell the media they want to think aboutвҖқ (p375).\nOf course, both of these phenomena exist; the relationship between audience\nand creators is bidirectional, reciprocally causal, and embedded in overlapping\nsociotechnical contexts. I focus on how this relationship develops on each of\nthe social media platforms that have come to dominate online communication;\nthat is, this Element centers the вҖңtechnology that shapes consumption, distribu-\ntion, and content productionвҖқ (p712). Or, as I call it, the Supply and Demand\nFramework.\nThe central dynamic of my framework is circular, so it is difficult to\nfully capture in the linear medium of text or with the unidirectional causality\n6 Politics and Communication\napproaches currently in vogue in much of social science. As much as I love\nwriting and causal inference, our objects of study do not always contort them-\nselves into ideal forms for our preferred models and paradigms. And I believe\nthat both the general media ecosystem and the local environments on a given\nplatform are best understood through my Supply and Demand Framework. This\nElement provides a detailed application of the Framework to the study of You-\nTube Politics, one of the most important yet under-studied components of the\ncontemporary US media environment.\nAs a political scientist, my primary (but far from exclusive) interest is in the\neffect of YouTube on the beliefs of citizens who consume YouTube videos and\nwho might vote based on the information they receive in doing so. Taking this\nlarger question of вҖңWhat does YouTube do to American Politics?вҖқ as largely\nfixed, both by disciplinary convention and self-evident importance to a citizen\nof the United States, my framework can be used to divide up this daunting task\ninto more manageable, empirical study-sized chunks. This is what I mean by\nвҖңsetting the academic agenda.вҖқ\nMy hope is that this framework will allow scholars to situate and synthesize\nexisting approaches to studying social media. That is, I do not see this frame-\nwork as invalidating any existing research, but rather enhancing its value by\nsuggesting pathways of knowledge circulation. In particular, I argue that map-\nping out all of the potential inputs to the YouTube system is helpful in keeping\ntrack of what scholars do and do not yet know, and therefore to decide what\nthey should study next.\nThis cannot be a deterministic process, and different scholars can of course\ncontribute in different ways based on their preexisting interests and competen-\ncies. I will, however, propose a heuristic for researchers deciding on a specific\nresearch question:\nHeuristic: Study what is under-studied.\nThis sounds tautological, but it requires keeping two parameters in mind.\nFirst, we should spend more of our time studying what is more important; IвҖҷm\nnot arguing for an equal distribution of scholarly attention across every con-\nceivable area of inquiry. Note also that this parameter is intrinsically normative:\nWhile I will propose some baseline metrics for importance, this parameter is\nultimately determined by our collective or respective goals .\nSecond, we need to invest in some empirical measures of how we are cur-\nrently distributing our scholarly attention. Even if we know how much a given\ntopic should be studied, in order to know whether we should study it more or\nless, we need to know what weвҖҷre doing now.\nLike all human endeavor, this process is not perfectible: Some kind of\noverreaction to thermostatic correction is inevitable, and the social phenomena\nThe YouTube Apparatus 7\nwe study are far from static. Worse, these two processes are not independent:\nPart of the knowledge generated by research is precisely about the importance\nof a given topic. The edge case of this problem are the вҖңunknown unknownsвҖқ:\nWhen the world produces some novel phenomenon, we start out knowing noth-\ning about it and thus cannot deliberate over its importance or decide to allocate\nour time to studying it.\nAs a scholar of digital media for nearly a decade, this very first link in\nthe chain of knowledge production has frequently posed a challenge. My\nlong-standing habits of trawling obscure corners of the internet (now fully\nrationalized as вҖңexploratory researchвҖқ) reveal some new platform, trend, or ide-\nological current that might be important. How can I check my intuitions вҖ“ that\nis, how can I make this case to myself?\nMore importantly for the academic environment, how can I convince funders\nand peer reviewers that this new phenomenon is important? In 2015, my first\nresearch on Twitter faced significant headwinds from my peers, who did not\nsee why online behavior was important for understanding contemporary poli-\ntics. Sure, Twitter was important as a tool for protesters and activists spreading\ntactical information; the Arab Spring and the wave of anti-capitalists protests\nin the early 2010s had put that topic firmly on the academic agenda. But why,\nas the old saw goes, should we care about what anyone had for lunch?\n3 There Is Something Wrong on the Internet\nDespite an explosion of academic interest in digital media over the past decade,\nthe topic is still dramatically understudied. If you believe, like I do, that we are\nliving through a media-technological upheaval rivaled only by that occasioned\nby the printing press, it is difficult to imagine studying anything else.\nBut even a skeptical, hard-nosed social scientist, beginning with the Aristo-\ntelian premise that humans arewhat we do, is forced to recognize that digital\nmedia production and consumption makes up an increasingly large percentage\nof the average humanвҖҷs waking hours.\nAllen et al. (2020 ) provides one of the most comprehensive pictures of total\nmedia consumption in the United States to date; they find that as of 2018, the\naverage American spends 460 minutes a day (7.5 hours) consuming media.\nAmong the youngest age group, eighteen to twenty-four years old, this number\nis just under six hours a day (351 minutes); 207 of those minutes are spent on\na mobile device, and another fifty-four minutes on a desktop computer.\n8 Politics and Communication\nUnfortunately for traditional methods for measuring media consumption,\nthe diversity of options makes survey enumeration useless for all but the\nbroadest categories. Someone might be able to tell you that they spent fifteen\nminutes on TikTok, but asking them вҖңwhich videosвҖқ or even вҖңwhich creatorsвҖқ\nthey watched is not a robust measurement strategy.\nThe situation on the supply side of digital media is if anything more dire:\nThe number of people empowered to produce media has exploded, rendering\nquantitative comparison absurd. More hours of video media are recorded and\nuploaded to YouTube and TikTok in a day than were created in the world in a\ndecade during heyday of the postwar broadcast media.\nThis deluge of content makes the task of setting the academic agenda\ndifficult. Where do we even begin!?\nOne entry point for many scholars is simply to use our intuitions, developed\nfrom our personal experience with digital media. This was a reasonable strategy\nwith broadcast media; Walter Cronkite was in fact broadly representative of the\npolitical media diet of the country as a whole.\nThe rise of the internet changed everything. It allows production and con-\nsumption at a scale far beyond mass media, rendering it impossible for an\nindividual to know what everyone else is up to simply by being an avid and\nbroad consumer of political media. This in turn makes the nonrepresenta-\ntiveness of social scientistsвҖҷ media diets a problem for our understanding of\nimportant trends in the media sphere.\nWe know that social scientists are intensely nonrepresentative of the popula-\ntions that we study. Despite considerable progress over the previous decades,\nthis nonrepresentativeness persists for demographic groups other than white\nAmerican men. This legacy of discrimination persists in the inherited bound-\naries of the canonical areas of study within media and communication, partic-\nularly when it comes to the boundary of вҖңpoliticalвҖқ media ( Freelon, Malmer,\nand Pruden ,2023 ).\nEven if these biases were fully rectified, we would still face a fundamental\nproblem: By construction , political science professors are much better educated\nand more interested in politics, and we have the capacity to consume massive\namounts of political media. There is widespread recognition that we need to\nremind ourselves of these baseline facts вҖ“ the central insight of perhaps the\nmost important book about American public opinion ( Converse ,1964 ) is that\nmost people simply donвҖҷt care about politicsвҖ“but it is all too easy to rely on the\nintuitions of our colleagues, peer reviewers, and funders.\nWe are deeply, professionally invested in the technologies of writing, delib-\neration, and scientific debate. As the sociologist of science Bruno Latour\nThe YouTube Apparatus 9\npointed out, if we simply observe the actions that scientists take rather than\naccepting our stated higher purpose, it becomes clear that our job is to read\nand write papers (Latour and Woolgar ,2013 ). Boundary-pushing weirdos in\nmedia studies departments aside, we are not primarily invested in watching\nand recording videos.\nIn fact, through both introspection and discussion with colleagues, it is clear\nthat many academics actively canвҖҷt stand to consume information in video\nformat; weвҖҷd much rather read it. The average person, unfortunately for the aca-\ndemic agenda, is the exact opposite: They dislike reading, and have abandoned\ntext-based digital media like blogs as soon as possible. Twitter, despite being\nperhaps the best-studied social media platform, has never been used by more\nthan 25 percent of US adults. Many professors have noticed a steep decline in\nthe writing ability of incoming undergrads. People who have been teaching for\nforty years noticed a slight degradation over the decades, but the past ten and\nespecially five years have seen a collapse.\nThe academic agenda has yet to adapt to the reality that the majority of time\nspent on social media is spent on non-textual platforms. For a variety of reasons,\nsome legitimate others merely omphalocentric, we spend far too much time\nstudying Facebook and especially Twitter.\nThe media is how we learn about the world outside of our own experi-\nence ( Mutz ,1998 ). We rely on the media to tell us about important trends\nin other realms of social life. The problem is that academics and media\nprofessionals inhabit a mutually constituted and reinforcing echo chamber.\nThe term вҖңecho chamberвҖқ here is not used casually. This phrase has been\ndisproportionately effective in setting the academic agenda for studying social\nmedia, especially in the first decade after its birth. вҖңEcho chamberвҖқ simply\nrings true, a fundamentally poetic achievement that is upstream of quantitative\nresearch. One of the ways the academic agenda is set, then, is through reso-\nnant metaphors ; pun on вҖңecho chamberвҖқ intended and not at all incidental. For\nexample, Simon and Camargo (2023 ) investigate the usage and implications\nof the вҖңinfodemicвҖқ metaphor that emerged to describe misinformation around\nCOVID-19.\nConsiderable energy has been spent investigating the phenomenon of echo\nchambers. My summary of the literature is that it finds that they donвҖҷt exist вҖ“\nexcept among users in specialized (partisan or professional) networks.\nWhat threshold of diversity of media diet would be sufficient to falsify\nthe existence of вҖңecho chambers?вҖқ No one denies that online media diets are\nhighly skewed. But the threshold canвҖҷt just be greater than 50 percent con-\ngruent information; thatвҖҷs trivial given any amount of media choice. For now,\nletвҖҷs consider a definition based on the offline baseline: Online echo chambers\n10 Politics and Communication\nexist if the online news consumers get a higher percentage of their news from\ncongruent sources than do offline news consumers.\nUsing this conception, the most comprehensive peer-reviewed paper on the\ntopic, Guess (2021 ) concludes that вҖңif вҖҳecho chambersвҖҷ exist, they are a reality\nfor relatively few people who may no')
```

## Source: file:///Users/pikeymickey/Library/CloudStorage/GoogleDrive-founder@shadowtagai.com/.shortcut-targets-by-id/1kAidkMEUaPeNQ9q0wJpcHM7TD9mAs4HF/AiYou_Phase_Docs/Ai Resources/GDPR (EUR-Lex text).pdf
