You prompt Gemini in Gmail by selecting Help me write. You type:\nRequest a follow-up conversation on [date] at [time] with this customer who provided negative feedback \nto understand their concern and offer resolutions. Include example solutions. Understanding your market and making \ninformed, strategic decisions is paramount, and \nso is getting urgent tasks done while youвҖҷre on \nthe go.\nThis section provides you with simple ways to \nintegrate prompts in your daily tasks. \n \nGetting started\nFirst, review the general prompt-writing tips on page 2 and the Prompting 101 section at the beginning of  \nthis guide.\nEach prompt below is presented with an accompanying scenario to serve as inspiration for how you can \ncollaborate with Gemini for Google Workspace. To be considered for \npublication, please visit www.forbesbooks.com. \n\nTo those who believe in the bold promise of democratizing \nSaaS, including our advisors, investors, customers, partners, \nand team members who are encouraging, inspiring, and \ngalvanizing this vision, aspiration, and movement. \n\nThe revolution is based on non-linear innovations \nin business concepts: radical вҖңreconceptionsвҖқ of \nexisting business models in ways that would \ncreate new value for customers, rude surprises for \ncompetitors, and new wealth for investors. \nвҖ”-GARY HAMEL \nAMERICAN MANAGEMENT CONSULTANT AND FOUNDER OF STRATEGOS \n\nCONTENTS \nCrRIMGG 25s PEE oe cs wa ec ase be ee xix \nINECDOUCIIOIN woes Ste nN Lo 5 os wie 1 \nA Multi-Trillion-Dollar Epiphany \nCanter OnG@e wire at coe Ge sre nes oe ah В«ee 9 \nSaaS in the Past \nCRADTRE EL WOe tk cate eke sk ue ss Sigealo 23 \nSaaS in the Present \nChapteminree rc. ey veer teen orale alee ahets 51 \nSaaS in the Future \nCONCINSI ON mee teccacat cnehstenc acl cknis a: von Seah > Ss 89 \nLiberation \nThe Journey to Democratizing SaaS........... 93 \nA Timeline \nAcknowledgments ..........-----eeeeees 105 \nA Note about the Upcoming Sequel to This Book ...111 \n\nThe reason why it is so difficult for existing firms \nto capitalize on disruptive innovations is that \ntheir processes and their business model that make \nthem good at the existing business actually make \nthem bad at competing for the disruption. \nвҖ”CEAYTON CHRISTENSEN \n\nPrelude \nThis book is, in fact, not a book. \nIt is a manifesto for a new movement in the \nsoftware industry! \nAn entrepreneurial movement that would create \na radical change in the way we develop, deploy, and \nmanage softwareвҖ”as a Service. \nA movement that opens the gateway to millions \nof net new good-paying jobs and the resulting economic \nand social good. \nNo matter if you are an entrepreneur bringing a \nSaaS solution to market, or an intrapreneur within \na corporation working on publishing one or more \nof your internally developed software assets вҖңas a \nService,вҖқ this is a call to action for you! \nXIX \n\nDemocratize: \nTo make (something) accessible to everyone. \nSaaS: \nSoftware as a Service \nDisruptive Innovation: \nReshaping the disciplines of an established system, \nleading to new opportunities and markets \n\nINTRODUCTION \nA Multi-Trillion- \nDollar Epiphany \nIn September 2021, as I was putting the finishing \ntouches on the manuscript of this book, my wife, our \ntwo daughters, and I took a long weekend to Palm \nSpringsвҖ”conveniently about two hours away from \nour residence in SoCalвҖ”as a symbolic makeup for \nour typical weeklong end-of-summer family vacation \nthat we had forfeited due to the pandemic. вҖҳThe deal I \nmade with the family was for me to work during the \ndaysвҖ”including working on the manuscriptвҖ”and \nin the evenings, as the temperature cooled down, we \nwould all go out for a walk and dinner, spending some \nquality family time together. The arrangement worked \n\nDEMOCRATIZING SaaS \nout well and created a nice memory as our younger \ndaughter, Leila, was about to start her last term at \nUC Irvine. \nWhile there, a recently published McKinsey \nreport came to my attention on both CNN \nBusiness and on Bloomberg, suggesting that by \n2030, the SaaS industry will be a multi-trillion- \ndollar industry. While I was not surprised, to me, \nthe most conspicuous aspect of the report was its \nsingular focus on SaaS and SaaS alone as an inde- \npendent economic driver of prosperity for the next \ndecade and beyond. The report predicted that just \nIndiaвҖҷs 6 percent share of this next global economic \nvalue-creation boom will be about $1 trillion by \n2030, creating a thousand SaaS providers that \nwould subsequently create five hundred thousand \nnew well-paying jobs. Furthermore, the report \ncommentaries went on to predict that the high-tech \njobsвҖҷ typical 4x multiplier effect for creating other \nsupporting jobs would lead to creating 2.5 million \nnew jobs in India alone and, by extrapolation, over \n40 million new jobs globally. \nThe timing of this highly researched and insight- \nful report could not have been any better, since the \nmotive behind writing this book was to show, with \nevidence, how an вҖңautomation platformвҖқ for SaaS- \n\nINTRODUCTION: A MULTI-TRILLION-DOLLAR EPIPHANY \nenablement and SaaS operations management can \nlower the barriers to becoming a SaaS provider by \nmaking it cheaper, faster, and betterвҖ”and therefore \nlead to democratizing SaaS. By liberating SaaS from \nthe purview of the elite and those few with access to \nsignificant capital and presenting the opportunity to \nthe masses, we intend to show how such a platform \nwill empower everyday SaaS entrepreneurs to create \nsuccessful SaaS offerings without the need for a sizable \ninfusion of capital to start their venture and further \naccelerate the predictions within the McKinsey \nreport.\' \nThe Genesis of \nвҖңDemocratizing SaaSвҖқ \nAlmost twenty years back, in early January 2001, \nwhile driving on scenic Highway 280 in the Bay \nArea, I received a call from a conference organizer \nfrom London inviting me to be a keynote speaker for \nthe first Pan-European ASP Summit in early March. \n1 Saritha Rai, вҖңIndiaвҖҷs SaaS Firms May Be Worth $1 Trillion \nby 2030,вҖҷ Bloomberg, July 7, 2021, https://www.bloomberg. \ncom/news/articles/2021-07-07/india-s-saas-firms-may- \nbe-worth-1-trillion-by-2030-report-says; Diksha Madhok, \nвҖңIndia\'s got the next big thing in tech, and it could be worth $1 \ntrillionвҖқ CNN Business, September 9, 2021, https://www.cnn. \ncom/2021/09/09/tech/india-software-saas-intl-hnk/index.html. \n\nDEMOCRATIZING SaaS \nFast forward to March 4. My flight landed in \nRome, and I checked in at the legendary Hilton \nCavalieri, the site of the ASP? Summit. \nTwo days later, I delivered the opening keynote, \nwhich I had titled вҖңThe Value Web: The End of Value \nChain as We Know It!вҖқ In that talk, I predicted \nthat with the prevalence of broadband internet and \nutility computing (read: cloud), the traditional value \nchainвҖ”at least in the IT sectorвҖ”would collapse and \nbe replaced with a much more streamlined version, \ncombined with the emergence of new players. Other \nkeynoters during the summit included Marc Benioff \nof Salesforce.com, Richard Lawson of Lawson \nSoftware, and executives from Microsoft, Intel, \nNortel, Compaq, and Fujitsu, to name a few. \nFrom the many conversations we had during the \nsummit, it became clear that there was a consensus \namong the attendees that one key element of the IT \nvalue chain that was too complex, time consuming, and \n2 "ASP (application service provider)вҖқ was a term used in the late \nвҖҳ90s and early 2000s and is the closest predecessor to a SaaS \nprovider. ASPs hosted, managed, and delivered third-party \nsoftware applications such as SAP R3, JD Edwards, or People- \nsoft from a data center for their customers for a fee. The term \nevolved into MSP (managed service provider) with an expanded \nscope, such as cloud-management-related services. In todayвҖҷs \nparlance, the summit would have been called the Cloud and \nSaaS Summit! \n\nINTRODUCTION:A MULTI-TRILLION-DOLLAR EPIPHANY \ncostly was the development of a flexible and scalable \ndistribution mechanism for each and every software \napplication from scratch. In other words, we had iden- \ntified a bottleneck in the value chain: that it was too \ncumbersome to transform software applications into \nefficient, scalable, and fully instrumented SaaS. \nInspired to explore this opportunity, I teamed \nup with a few like-minded individuals to pursue the \ncreation of what we later called the вҖңSaaS Operating \nSystem.вҖқ In other words, we decided to take the вҖңas \na ServiceвҖқ capabilities of SaaS and deliver them in a \nplug-and-play, configurable, customizable, and вҖңas a \nServiceвҖқ way so that each and every SaaS company \nwouldn\'t have to rebuild them alone from scratch, \nthereby making the main takeaway from the summit \nin Rome actionable. \nLater, we dubbed the company Corent with the \nvisionary objective of developing the SaaS OS (a.k.a. \nSaaSOpsв„ў) or the Software Defined SaaS Platformв„ў, a \nplug-and-play вҖңas a ServiceвҖқ service! Some have compared \nthe Corent SaaS OS to the emergence of databases in the \nВ°80s or app servers in the 90s. That is, in the same way \nthat those abstraction layers accelerated the development \nof software applications, we now had created an abstrac- \ntion layer accelerating the development of SaaS offerings \nas a natural extension to DevOps. \n\nDEMOCRATIZING SaaS \nOthers have used вҖңNetflix for the software \nindustryвҖқ as an analogy for the SaaS OS, because in the \nsame way that movie producers focus on producing \nthe movie, and then Netflix (or other like services) \nprovide the turnkey delivery mechanism to distrib- \nute the movie вҖңas a ServiceвҖқ with all the subscription- \nrelated matters handled, SaaSOps can deliver prac- \ntically any software application вҖңas a Service,вҖқ with \nall the subscription bells and whistles taken care of, \nleaving the developers totally focused on develop- \ning the core application rather than its distribution \nmechanism. \nWhat we mean by вҖңdemocratizingвҖқ SaaS is not \nonly broadening the accessibility of software appli- \ncations and reaching more people in more places \nthrough the power of SaaS, but also extending that \naccess up the value chain to the software developers, \nindependent software vendors (ISVs), entrepreneurs, \nand enterprises so that they are empowered to deliver \ntheir applications as SaaS without the enormous \nupfront investment and time that the traditional SaaS \ndevelopment demands by simply deploying the SaaS \ndelivery model with a simple subscription to SaaSOps \nservices. \nIn this context, SaaS has a lot more going for \nit than simply providing convenience, ease for the \n\nINTRODUCTION: A MULTI-TRILLION-DOLLAR EPIPHANY \ncustomer experience, lower cost of service delivery, \nand decentralization. We truly believe that democra- \ntizing SaaS will further ignite the engine of innovation \nin the software industry and generate a new wave of \nboth entrepreneurship and intrapreneurship and asso- \nciated financial growth that is on the horizon. \nOur intention here is to show how the current \nGen-1 cloud vision is incomplete and how the tech- \nenabled democratization of SaaS will complete the \nvision, resulting in the collapse of the current costly \nand tedious traditional IT value chain. We predict \nthat this will lead to an вҖңapplication-awareвҖқ Gen-2 \ncloud serving the end users, enterprises, and entre- \npreneurs alike. We will clarify the ambiguities sur- \nrounding вҖңWhat is SaaS?вҖқ versus a cloud application, \nan application offered in a subscription model, or one \npublished on a cloud marketplace. \nWe will also walk through a brief history of SaaS, \nfrom past to presentвҖ”and especially from present to \nfutureвҖ”to demonstrate how democratizing SaaS can \nbe a massively disruptive yet positive change for you \nand your organization, no matter whether you are a \ncorporate executive, a software vendor, or a potential \nentrepreneur. We hope by the end of this book that \nthe insights gained will ignite innovative business \nideas on harnessing the new ways you will look at \n\nDEMOCRATIZING SaaS \nthe SaaS possibilities and inspire positive action and \ninitiative on one or more fronts. \nAllow me to conclude this introduction by \nreminding us of AppleвҖҷs 1997 вҖңHere\'s to the Crazy \nOnesвҖқ commercial, which was narrated by Steve Jobs. \nIвҖҷve always been inspired by his words: \nHere\'s to the crazy ones, the misfits, the rebels, \nthe troublemakers, the round pegs in the \nsquare holes ... the ones who see things dif- \nferentlyвҖ”theyвҖҷre not fond of rules ... You can \nquote them, disagree with them, glorify, or vilify \nthem, but the only thing you can\'t do is ignore \nthem because they change things ... They push \nthe human race forward, and while some may \nsee them as the crazy ones, we see genius, \nbecause the ones who are crazy enough to \nthink that they can change the world, are the \nones who do. \n\nCH AP.TER (O\'NE \nSaaS 1n the Past \nBefore we discuss where SaaS is going and its unprec- \nedented potential impact, it would help if we had a \nclear picture of where we have been, especially in \nterms of the evolution of business software. Like any \nindustry, changes in technology have been prompted \nas a process of value migration to better meet customer \nneeds. In the case of infor- \nChanges in \ntechnology have \nbeen prompted \nas a process of \nvalue migration \nto better meet \ncustomer needs. mation technology, the \nprogression of value \nmigration could be summa- \nrized by the value (as \nmeasured by the market \nvaluation of representative \ncompanies adjusted for \n\nDEMOCRATIZING SaaS \nrevenue and margins) migrating from hardware to \nsoftware and then software to services, such as in \ncloud (Infrastructure as a Service) or SaaS (Software \nas a Service). \nValue Migration from Hardware and Software to Service \nHigh expectations for IBM\'s Smart Cloud \n(Infrastructure as a Service) \n200 \n150 \n100 Switch from traditional to SaaS Model \nV\\ Adobe \n50 \nVALUE (MARKET CAP В§ BILLIONS) \n2010 2015 2020 \nВ© IBM (hardware, some services) \nВ© Adobe \nВ® Qualcomm \nSalesforce (SaaS) \nWith this in mind, letвҖҷs start with a general \noverview of how these eras of modern technology \nhave come to pass. \nThe Hardware Era \nUsing hardware for computing purposes can be taken \nback as far as ancient times when devices like the \nabacus were developed for solving complicated sums. \n\nCHAPTER ONE: SAAS IN THE PAST \nFast forward, early computers like ENIAC and \nHarvard Mark in the midforties would then open the \ndoor to the concept of storing data electronically, but \nthat storage was dependent upon the hardware itself. \nIn the realm of computing hardware, IBM would be \nestablished as the king, with its massive mainframe \ncomputers providing вҖңtime-sharingвҖқ computing \nservices for millions. \nIn the late вҖҷ60s, Robert Noyce and his team \nfounded Intel and invented the microprocessor. \nTheir idea was truly innovative at the time: instead \nof building a fully operational computer, they would \nfocus their resources on creating an integrated \ncircuit that could be used by many different types of \ncomputers. While they created the worldвҖҷs first com- \nmercial microprocessor chip in 1971, it would not \nbe until the popularization of the PC in the вҖҷ80s that \ntheir foresight would truly pay off. \nSo successful were their marketing campaigns in \nthe вҖҷ90s that IвҖҷm willing to bet even today, many cant \nhear their name without also hearing their jingle in their \nheads. Even ifa consumer didnвҖҷt know what a microchip \nwas, they knew what Intel was. In this way, Intel provided \nthe вҖңengineвҖқ needed to power the rest of the hardware. \nThey democratized the role of the microprocessor, which \ncreated more value for the company so that they are still \n17 \n\nDEMOCRATIZING SaaS \nranked as the worldвҖҷs top semiconductor chip manufac- \nturer based on revenue. This value rested primarily in \nthe fact that hardware companies like IBM, Hewlett- \nPackard, and Dell did not have to spend time or money \nto create their own processors but could simply вҖңplug \ninвҖҷ IntelвҖҷs microprocessor to superpower their machines. \nThe Software Era (When Software \nStarted Eating the World!) \nMuch of software theory and AI theory can also be \ntraced back to Turing. A true visionary, he could see \nthe direction in which technology would move, but \nit took a lot of years and work for technology to catch \nup to the idea. \nDuring the days of the Apollo program in the late \nвҖҷ60s, NASA employee Margaret Hamilton coined the \nterm вҖңsoftware engineeringвҖқ and started to promote \nthe concept that software operation should not be \nconsidered just a part of the hardware but also could \nbe separated into its own unique component to \ninteract with both the user and the machine.*? IBM \nlatched onto this concept and, by the late 60s, began \n3 On November 22, 2016, Hamilton received the Presidential \nMedal of Freedom from president Obama for her work leading \nto the development of on-board flight software for NASAвҖҷs \nApollo Moon missions, \n\nCHAPTER ONE: SAAS IN THE PAST \nmarketing the idea of вҖңunbundlingвҖқ software from the \nhardware for direct use by consumers. \nWhen I started working for HP in the mid-вҖҷ80s, \nHP was practicing vertical integration, building all \ncomponents of the machine, from our own processor \nto operating system to applications, and then we \nwould turn around and sell the whole stack as a fully \nfunctioning bundle to the customer. In the late вҖҷ80s, \nwe saw the shift to horizontal integration, in which \nthere was a value chain created as different companies \nspecialized in different elements of the solution and \neach provided a valuable ring in the value chain. \nFor example, the industry saw the rise of software \ncompanies like Microsoft to provide an operating system \nand applications like word processing and spread- \nsheets; Oracle, providing a database system; and SAP, \nproviding ERP, HR, and logistics software solutions. \nNow software developers could focus on developing \nsoftware solutions that could run on existing hardware. \nSince this same concept had already been successful in \nthe hardware space (e.g., Intel), it was a fairly seamless \ntransition for the industry as a whole and was mutually \nbeneficial by allowing companies to streamline their \nprocesses and focus on what they did best. \nSoftware became a democratized concept because \nconsumers did not have to rely on the hardware, \n13 \n\nDEMOCRATIZING SaaS \nbut uncoupling the hardware and software allowed \nconsumers to essentially customize the hardware \nwith the software tools they needed. For example, a \nphotographer could install photo-enhancing software \nlike Photoshop that an accountant would not need. \nMeanwhile, the accountant could utilize software like \nQuickBooks that would add value to their business \nand allow them to serve a greater number of customers. \nOn the enterprise side, the value started shifting from \nthe IBMs or HPs of the world to the Oracles, SAPs, \nand PeopleSofts of the world. This allowed software \nto compete on its merits of functionality, reliability, \nand cost, independent of the hardware that it ran on \n(hence, the term independent software vendor, or ISV, \nwas coined). \nThis led to the commoditization of hardware \nand was a big factor in the rise of Intel as the standard \nhardware core for computing since the market did not \ncare how one hardware was superior to the other as long \nas the desired software application ran on them well. As \nthe software value contribution rose, the relative value \ncontribution of hardware decreased. Marc Andreessen \nwrote in his seminal 2011 article in The Wall Street \nJournal, titled вҖңWhy Software is Eating the WorldвҖқ: \n14 \n\nCHAPTER ONE: SAAS IN THE PAST \nMore and more major businesses and indus- \ntries are being run on software and delivered \nas online servicesвҖ”from movies to agriculture \nto national defense. Many of the winners are \nSilicon Valley-style entrepreneurial technology \ncompan')
```
